immediate_advice: Sit or lie down in a safe, calm place and practice slow diaphragmatic breathing (inhale 4 seconds, hold 4 seconds, exhale 8 seconds)
Use grounding techniques: name 5 things you can see, 4 you can touch, 3 you can hear
Avoid caffeine, nicotine, and stimulants until the episode fully subsides
Do not drive or operate machinery during an active panic attack
quick_solutions: Practice daily mindfulness meditation and progressive muscle relaxation to reduce baseline anxiety
Maintain a consistent sleep schedule and limit caffeine intake
Keep a symptom diary to identify and avoid personal triggers
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is the most effective long-term intervention — seek referral
panic disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care if chest pain is new or severe, if you faint, or if symptoms suggest a cardiac event. Consult a psychiatrist or psychologist if panic attacks are recurrent, unpredictable, or significantly impairing daily function.

immediate_advice: Use over-the-counter water-based vaginal moisturizers or lubricants to relieve dryness and discomfort
Avoid soaps, douches, or scented products in the vaginal area as they worsen irritation
Wear loose, breathable cotton underwear to reduce friction and moisture retention
quick_solutions: Non-hormonal vaginal moisturizers used regularly (every 2–3 days) provide significant symptom relief
Topical estrogen therapy (cream, ring, or tablet) is highly effective — discuss with your doctor
Regular sexual activity or vaginal stimulation helps maintain vaginal tissue health
Stay well hydrated and avoid smoking, which accelerates vaginal tissue thinning
atrophic vaginitis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor if symptoms persist despite moisturizers, if vaginal bleeding occurs post-menopause, if there is pain during urination, or if recurrent urinary tract infections develop.

immediate_advice: Avoid rubbing the eyes as this can worsen muscle strain
If double vision occurs suddenly, cover one eye to reduce disorientation and seek urgent evaluation
Do not drive if experiencing active double vision or significant visual disturbance
quick_solutions: Corrective lenses (prism glasses) can compensate for mild alignment issues
Vision therapy exercises under supervision of an orthoptist may strengthen eye muscles
Patching therapy may be recommended by your specialist for amblyopia-related alignment disorders
Surgical correction (strabismus surgery) is an option for significant or non-responsive cases
eye alignment disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek immediate care if double vision or eye misalignment appears suddenly in an adult, as this may indicate a neurological emergency. Children with squinting or misaligned eyes should be evaluated promptly to prevent permanent vision loss.

immediate_advice: Keep the vaginal area clean and dry; wash with warm water only — no soaps or douches
Wear loose-fitting, breathable cotton underwear and avoid tight synthetic clothing
Avoid sexual intercourse until the cause is identified and treated to prevent worsening or transmission
quick_solutions: Over-the-counter antifungal treatments are appropriate if yeast infection is suspected
Avoid scented products, bubble baths, and fabric softeners that irritate vaginal tissue
Stay well hydrated and wipe front to back after toileting
Complete the full course of any prescribed antibiotic or antifungal treatment even if symptoms resolve early
vaginitis.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor if symptoms persist beyond one week, if there is unusual discharge (gray, green, or foul-smelling), pelvic pain, fever, or if this is a first episode to confirm the diagnosis.

immediate_advice: Avoid prolonged exposure to loud noises and use ear protection in noisy environments
Reduce caffeine and salt intake, which can temporarily worsen tinnitus in some individuals
Avoid complete silence — use background white noise or soft music to mask the ringing
quick_solutions: Sound therapy (white noise machines or tinnitus masking apps) provides significant relief for many patients
Tinnitus Retraining Therapy (TRT) or Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) helps manage the psychological impact
Check all current medications with your doctor — many drugs including aspirin and some antibiotics can cause or worsen tinnitus
Manage stress through relaxation techniques as stress reliably worsens tinnitus perception
tinnitus of unknown cause.when_to_seek_help: Seek prompt evaluation if tinnitus is in one ear only, if it is pulsatile (beating in time with your heartbeat), if hearing loss accompanies it, or if it starts after a head injury.

immediate_advice: Do not delay prescribed eye drop medications — consistent use is critical to prevent irreversible vision loss
Avoid activities that significantly increase intraocular pressure such as heavy lifting or inverted yoga poses
Protect eyes from bright light and wear sunglasses outdoors
quick_solutions: Adhere strictly to prescribed intraocular pressure-lowering eye drops (e.g., prostaglandin analogues, beta-blockers)
Attend all follow-up appointments for visual field testing and optic nerve monitoring
Laser therapy (SLT) or surgical procedures (trabeculectomy) may be recommended if drops are insufficient
Avoid corticosteroid eye drops unless specifically prescribed, as they can raise eye pressure
glaucoma.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for sudden severe eye pain, halos around lights, rapidly blurred vision, nausea and vomiting with eye pain — these suggest acute angle-closure glaucoma which requires same-day treatment.

immediate_advice: Do not attempt rapid refeeding without medical supervision as this can cause dangerous electrolyte shifts
Remove or reduce access to diet pills, laxatives, or other purging aids in the immediate environment
Ensure the person is not left alone if there are concerns about self-harm or extreme restriction
quick_solutions: Seek a multidisciplinary treatment team including a psychiatrist, dietitian, and therapist experienced in eating disorders
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT-E) is the first-line evidence-based treatment for bulimia and binge eating disorder
Family-Based Treatment (FBT/Maudsley approach) is recommended for adolescents with anorexia nervosa
Regular medical monitoring of electrolytes, heart rhythm, and bone density is essential throughout recovery
eating disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for fainting, chest pain, severe muscle weakness, irregular heartbeat, or BMI below 15. Seek urgent specialist referral for any suspected eating disorder — early intervention significantly improves outcomes.
eating disorder.specialist_recommendation: Psychiatrist / Eating Disorder Specialist / Registered Dietitian

immediate_advice: Call emergency services immediately — a TIA is a medical emergency even if symptoms have resolved
Do not drive yourself to the hospital
Note the exact time symptoms started and what symptoms occurred — this information is critical for treatment decisions
quick_solutions: Urgent brain imaging (CT or MRI) and vascular studies are required within 24 hours
Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin or clopidogrel) is typically initiated immediately after ruling out hemorrhage
Identify and aggressively control risk factors: hypertension, diabetes, high cholesterol, atrial fibrillation, and smoking
Carotid endarterectomy may be recommended if significant carotid artery stenosis is identified
transient ischemic attack.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately — every TIA must be treated as an impending stroke. Do not wait to see if symptoms return. Time-sensitive evaluation within 24 hours significantly reduces stroke risk.

immediate_advice: Drink at least 2–3 liters of water daily to help flush bacteria from the urinary tract
Begin prescribed antibiotics immediately and complete the full course even if symptoms improve
Rest and use paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever and flank pain relief (avoid NSAIDs if kidney function is impaired)
quick_solutions: A urine culture should guide antibiotic selection — do not self-treat without confirmation
Monitor temperature twice daily; if fever does not improve within 48–72 hours of antibiotics, return for reassessment
Avoid urinary catheters unless absolutely necessary as they increase infection risk
Investigate for underlying causes (kidney stones, structural abnormalities) after the acute episode resolves
pyelonephritis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for high fever with rigors, severe vomiting preventing oral medication intake, severe flank pain, confusion, or if you are pregnant, diabetic, or immunocompromised.

immediate_advice: Maintain gentle daily movement — complete rest worsens chronic pain and leads to deconditioning
Apply heat or cold packs to painful areas as needed for temporary relief
Avoid opioid analgesics for long-term management without specialist supervision due to dependency risk
quick_solutions: A multidisciplinary pain management program combining physiotherapy, psychology, and pharmacology is the most effective approach
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) for pain is evidence-based and reduces pain perception and disability
Low-impact regular exercise (swimming, walking, yoga) consistently reduces chronic pain intensity over time
Sleep hygiene improvement is critical as poor sleep amplifies pain perception significantly
chronic pain disorder.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor if pain is progressively worsening, if new neurological symptoms develop (weakness, numbness), if pain is disrupting sleep, work, and relationships, or if depression and anxiety are accompanying the pain.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for any vaginal bleeding, severe abdominal pain, reduced fetal movement, severe headache, or visual changes during pregnancy
Do not take any new medications, herbal supplements, or over-the-counter drugs without consulting your obstetrician
Stay hydrated, rest, and monitor symptoms closely
quick_solutions: Attend all scheduled antenatal appointments and do not skip ultrasound or blood test monitoring
Report even minor symptoms to your midwife or obstetrician — many pregnancy complications are manageable if caught early
Maintain a nutritious diet with adequate folic acid, iron, and calcium as recommended by your care team
Avoid smoking, alcohol, recreational drugs, and raw/undercooked foods throughout pregnancy
problem during pregnancy.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for heavy vaginal bleeding, absence of fetal movement for more than 12 hours after 28 weeks, severe abdominal cramping, sudden swelling of face or hands, or fever above 38°C.

immediate_advice: Avoid fatty foods immediately as they stimulate bile flow and can worsen biliary colic
Seek emergency care if you develop jaundice (yellowing of skin/eyes), high fever with chills, or severe right upper abdominal pain
Do not take NSAIDs if there is any suspicion of liver or kidney involvement
quick_solutions: Endoscopic Retrograde Cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) is the standard procedure for removing common bile duct stones
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy is typically recommended to prevent recurrence if the gallbladder is also affected
Maintain a low-fat diet before and after treatment to minimize bile duct stimulation
Liver function tests and imaging (ultrasound or MRCP) should be performed urgently to assess the bile duct
choledocholithiasis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for jaundice with fever and chills (Charcot's triad), which indicates acute cholangitis — a life-threatening infection requiring urgent intervention.

immediate_advice: Stop all alcohol consumption immediately and permanently — further intake accelerates liver failure
Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) and limit paracetamol strictly as both are hepatotoxic in cirrhosis
Seek emergency care for vomiting blood, black tarry stools, sudden abdominal swelling, or confusion
quick_solutions: Sodium restriction (less than 2g/day) and prescribed diuretics help manage ascites
Regular endoscopic surveillance for esophageal varices is mandatory to prevent life-threatening bleeding
Vaccinations for hepatitis A, B, influenza, and pneumococcal disease are recommended in all cirrhosis patients
Liver transplant evaluation should be considered for eligible patients with decompensated cirrhosis
cirrhosis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for hematemesis (vomiting blood), melena (black stools), rapid increase in abdominal girth, confusion or drowsiness (encephalopathy), or jaundice that worsens suddenly.

immediate_advice: Maintain strict blood glucose control — HbA1c below 7% significantly slows disease progression
Control blood pressure (target below 130/80 mmHg) as hypertension accelerates retinal damage
Report any sudden vision changes, floaters, flashing lights, or visual loss to your ophthalmologist immediately
quick_solutions: Annual dilated fundus examination is mandatory for all diabetic patients regardless of symptoms
Anti-VEGF injections (ranibizumab, bevacizumab) are first-line treatment for diabetic macular edema
Laser photocoagulation may be used for proliferative diabetic retinopathy to prevent neovascularization
Quit smoking as it significantly accelerates diabetic microvascular complications including retinopathy
diabetic retinopathy.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent ophthalmological care for any sudden change in vision, appearance of a curtain or shadow in the visual field, sudden onset of floaters or flashing lights, or any vision loss — these may indicate retinal detachment or vitreous hemorrhage.

immediate_advice: Continue gentle movement despite pain — immobility worsens fibromyalgia symptoms significantly
Apply heat to painful muscle areas and take warm baths for temporary pain relief
Prioritize sleep hygiene as disrupted sleep is both a cause and consequence of fibromyalgia flares
quick_solutions: Aerobic exercise (walking, swimming, cycling) is the single most evidence-based intervention for fibromyalgia
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) reduces pain catastrophizing and improves functional capacity
Medications such as duloxetine, pregabalin, or amitriptyline may be prescribed for pain and sleep
Pace activities and use energy management strategies to avoid post-exertional flares
fibromyalgia.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor if pain is progressively worsening despite management, if new neurological symptoms develop, if depression or suicidal ideation accompanies pain, or if daily functioning is severely impaired.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately — bowel ischemia is a life-threatening emergency
Do not eat or drink anything until medically evaluated
Report exact location and onset of abdominal pain to emergency personnel
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — CT angiography and immediate surgical or interventional radiology consultation are required
Bowel resection may be necessary if necrosis has occurred
Identify and treat underlying causes such as atrial fibrillation, arterial emboli, or hypotension
Post-treatment anticoagulation or antiplatelet therapy is typically required to prevent recurrence
ischemia of the bowel.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately — any sudden severe abdominal pain, especially with bloody diarrhea or pain out of proportion to physical findings, requires immediate emergency evaluation.
ischemia of the bowel.specialist_recommendation: Vascular Surgeon / General Surgeon / Gastroenterologist (Emergency evaluation first)

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately — peritonitis is a life-threatening surgical emergency
Do not eat or drink anything
Do not take analgesics before medical assessment as this can mask important diagnostic signs
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — surgical intervention (exploratory laparotomy) is almost always required
IV antibiotics and fluid resuscitation are initiated in hospital before and after surgery
Identify the underlying cause (perforated ulcer, appendicitis, bowel perforation) to guide surgical management
Intensive care monitoring is required post-operatively for multi-organ support
peritonitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for severe diffuse abdominal pain, rigid board-like abdomen, high fever, rapid heart rate, or signs of shock — these are hallmarks of peritonitis requiring immediate surgery.
peritonitis.specialist_recommendation: General Surgeon / Critical Care Specialist (Emergency evaluation first)

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for severe upper abdominal pain radiating to the back, especially with vomiting
Do not eat or drink anything until medically assessed — fasting reduces pancreatic stimulation
Avoid alcohol entirely — in alcohol-induced pancreatitis, any further intake is immediately dangerous
quick_solutions: IV fluid resuscitation is the cornerstone of initial management and should be started promptly in hospital
Pain management with appropriate analgesics is essential — do not delay seeking care due to pain severity
Early enteral nutrition (within 24–48 hours) via nasogastric tube is preferred over parenteral nutrition
Identify and treat the underlying cause: gallstones (ERCP), alcohol, hypertriglyceridemia, or medications
acute pancreatitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for severe constant upper abdominal pain, pain radiating to the back, vomiting, fever, or rapid deterioration. Severe acute pancreatitis has significant mortality risk.
acute pancreatitis.specialist_recommendation: Gastroenterologist / General Surgeon / Critical Care Specialist

immediate_advice: Elevate the affected limb above heart level to reduce swelling and pain
Apply warm compresses to the affected area for 15–20 minutes several times daily
Avoid prolonged standing or sitting — gentle walking is encouraged to maintain circulation
quick_solutions: NSAIDs (ibuprofen) reduce inflammation and pain in superficial thrombophlebitis
Compression stockings improve venous return and reduce symptoms
If located near the deep venous system (saphenofemoral junction), anticoagulation may be required
Treat any underlying varicose veins with specialist intervention to prevent recurrence
thrombophlebitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care if pain, redness, or swelling extends rapidly, if fever develops, if the clot is near the groin or behind the knee (risk of DVT), or if shortness of breath or chest pain develops suggesting pulmonary embolism.

immediate_advice: Use your prescribed short-acting bronchodilator (reliever inhaler, e.g., salbutamol) immediately for acute symptoms — 2–4 puffs via spacer
Sit upright in a comfortable position and try to remain calm; anxiety worsens bronchospasm
If symptoms do not improve after 4 puffs in 4 minutes, call emergency services immediately
quick_solutions: Use prescribed controller inhalers (inhaled corticosteroids) daily as directed, even when symptom-free
Identify and avoid personal triggers: dust mites, pet dander, mold, cold air, exercise, smoke, and certain medications
Develop a written Asthma Action Plan with your doctor to guide self-management during flares
Annual influenza vaccination is recommended as respiratory infections are the most common asthma trigger
asthma.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for severe breathlessness at rest, inability to complete sentences, blue lips or fingertips, confusion, or if reliever inhaler provides no relief — these indicate a life-threatening attack.

immediate_advice: Walk around or stretch the legs when symptoms occur — movement provides immediate but temporary relief
Apply warm or cold compresses to the legs before bedtime
Establish a regular sleep and wake schedule to minimize symptom disruption
quick_solutions: Reduce or eliminate caffeine, alcohol, and nicotine as all three worsen restless leg syndrome
Check iron levels with your doctor — iron deficiency is a common and treatable cause
Review all current medications with your doctor as antidepressants, antihistamines, and antipsychotics can worsen symptoms
Dopamine agonists (pramipexole, ropinirole) are first-line prescribed medications for moderate-to-severe cases
restless leg syndrome.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor if symptoms are occurring nightly, significantly disrupting sleep, or if symptoms worsen despite lifestyle changes. Also seek evaluation if symptoms are associated with leg pain, weakness, or numbness.

immediate_advice: Rest for 24 hours following the procedure; light spotting and cramping are expected for up to 2 weeks
Use prescribed pain relief (ibuprofen is typically first choice) for cramping
Avoid inserting anything into the vagina (tampons, menstrual cups, sexual intercourse) for at least 2 weeks to reduce infection risk
quick_solutions: Take all prescribed antibiotics to complete the full course to prevent post-procedural infection
Use a heating pad for abdominal cramping
Normal menstruation typically returns within 4–6 weeks — ovulation can occur within 2 weeks so contraception should be discussed
Contact your provider if bleeding is heavier than a heavy period, if fever develops, or if there is foul-smelling discharge
induced abortion.when_to_seek_help: Seek immediate care for soaking more than two sanitary pads per hour for two consecutive hours, fever above 38°C, severe abdominal pain not relieved by ibuprofen, or foul-smelling vaginal discharge.

immediate_advice: Give the infant a clean, chilled (not frozen) teething ring to chew on — cold reduces gum inflammation
Gently massage the sore gums with a clean finger for 1–2 minutes to relieve pressure
Use age-appropriate paracetamol (acetaminophen) for infants over 3 months if discomfort is significant — follow weight-based dosing
quick_solutions: Chilled (not frozen) solid foods such as cucumber or carrot pieces can provide relief for older infants (with supervision)
Avoid teething gels containing benzocaine in infants under 2 years as they carry a risk of methemoglobinemia
Maintain routine feeding and sleep schedules as much as possible to reduce overall distress
Keep skin dry around the mouth and chin to prevent drool rash — apply a barrier cream if needed
teething syndrome.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor if fever exceeds 38.5°C (teething does not cause high fever — this suggests infection), if the child refuses all fluids, appears lethargic, has diarrhea lasting more than 24 hours, or if a tooth has not emerged within 3 months of expected timing.

immediate_advice: Rehydrate immediately with oral rehydration solution (ORS) — small sips frequently rather than large amounts at once
Avoid solid food for a few hours if vomiting is active, then gradually reintroduce bland foods (toast, rice, banana, boiled potato)
Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water after using the toilet and before handling food to prevent spread
quick_solutions: Oral rehydration salts (ORS sachets) are more effective than water alone — available at pharmacies without prescription
Avoid dairy, high-fat foods, caffeine, and alcohol until fully recovered
Probiotics (Lactobacillus rhamnosus, Saccharomyces boulardii) can reduce duration by approximately one day
Antibiotics are not indicated for most viral gastroenteritis — use only if bacterial cause is confirmed by stool culture
infectious gastroenteritis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care for signs of dehydration (no urination for 8 hours, dry mouth, sunken eyes, confusion), blood in stool or vomit, fever above 39°C, symptoms lasting more than 3 days, or in young children, elderly, or immunocompromised individuals.
infectious gastroenteritis.specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Infectious Disease Specialist (severe cases)

immediate_advice: Use saline nasal irrigation (neti pot or saline spray) 2–3 times daily to flush mucus and reduce congestion
Apply warm compresses over the cheeks and forehead for 10–15 minutes to relieve sinus pressure and pain
Stay well hydrated — adequate fluid intake thins mucus secretions and promotes drainage
quick_solutions: Nasal decongestant sprays (oxymetazoline) provide rapid relief but should not be used for more than 3 consecutive days to avoid rebound congestion
Most acute sinusitis is viral and does not require antibiotics — antibiotics are indicated only if symptoms persist beyond 10 days or worsen after initial improvement
Steam inhalation with a bowl of hot water and a towel can temporarily relieve congestion
Oral decongestants (pseudoephedrine) may help but should be avoided in patients with hypertension or heart disease
acute sinusitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care for severe headache, stiff neck, high fever, swelling around the eyes, visual changes, or confusion — these may indicate spread of infection to surrounding structures (orbital cellulitis or meningitis).
acute sinusitis.specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Otolaryngologist (ENT) for recurrent cases

immediate_advice: Do not attempt abrupt withdrawal from alcohol, benzodiazepines, or opioids without medical supervision — this can be life-threatening
Ensure the person is in a safe environment and remove access to substances if possible
Call emergency services if the person is unresponsive, has a seizure, or shows signs of overdose
quick_solutions: Medically supervised detoxification is the safe first step for physical dependence on alcohol, opioids, or benzodiazepines
Medication-assisted treatment (methadone, buprenorphine for opioids; naltrexone for alcohol) significantly improves long-term outcomes
Behavioral therapies (Motivational Interviewing, CBT, 12-step facilitation) are essential components of recovery
Family support programs and peer support groups (AA, NA) provide sustained recovery scaffolding
substance-related mental disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for overdose, seizures, severe agitation, or loss of consciousness. Seek urgent addiction medicine consultation for anyone who wants to stop using substances safely.

immediate_advice: Ensure the mother is not left alone if she is expressing thoughts of harming herself or the baby — seek emergency help immediately in this case
Encourage rest whenever the baby sleeps and accept help from family or friends with infant care
Reassure the mother that postpartum depression is a medical condition, not a character flaw or failure as a parent
quick_solutions: Consult a doctor promptly — postpartum depression is highly treatable with therapy and/or medication
Antidepressants (SSRIs such as sertraline) are considered safe during breastfeeding under medical guidance
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) and Interpersonal Therapy (IPT) are effective first-line psychological treatments
Social support is a critical protective factor — structured peer support groups for new mothers are beneficial
postpartum depression.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for any thoughts of self-harm, harming the baby, hallucinations, or severe confusion — these may indicate postpartum psychosis, which is a psychiatric emergency. Consult a doctor within days for persistent low mood, inability to bond with the baby, or inability to function.
postpartum depression.specialist_recommendation: Psychiatrist / Perinatal Mental Health Specialist / Obstetrician

immediate_advice: Maintain upright posture and avoid prolonged sitting or lying in a flexed position
Apply heat to the lower back and buttocks to relieve stiffness, particularly in the morning
Begin gentle stretching and range-of-motion exercises daily — inactivity rapidly worsens spinal stiffness
quick_solutions: NSAIDs (naproxen, diclofenac) are first-line pharmacological treatment and significantly reduce inflammation and pain
Physiotherapy focusing on spinal extension exercises and breathing exercises preserves mobility and function
Biologic medications (TNF inhibitors such as adalimumab) are highly effective for patients not responding to NSAIDs
Swimming is particularly recommended as it maintains flexibility without impact loading on the spine
spondylitis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor if back pain is worse in the morning and improves with movement (an inflammatory pattern), if eye pain or redness develops (uveitis associated with spondylitis), or if new neurological symptoms appear in the legs.

immediate_advice: Apply a heat pad to the lower abdomen for cramp and pelvic pain relief
Take NSAIDs (ibuprofen, mefenamic acid) for pain during heavy menstrual periods — start 1–2 days before expected period onset
Seek urgent care for extremely heavy bleeding (soaking more than one pad per hour for several consecutive hours)
quick_solutions: Hormonal medications (combined oral contraceptives, progestins, levonorgestrel IUD) can reduce bleeding significantly
Uterine fibroid embolization (UFE) is a minimally invasive procedure effective at shrinking fibroids without surgery
Myomectomy (surgical fibroid removal) is recommended for women wishing to preserve fertility
Hysterectomy is a definitive treatment option for women who have completed childbearing and have severe symptoms
uterine fibroids.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care for extremely heavy menstrual bleeding causing dizziness or faintness, acute severe pelvic pain, or new urinary retention. Consult a gynecologist for any fibroids causing symptoms affecting quality of life.

immediate_advice: Apply a warm compress to the eyelid for 10–15 minutes, 3–4 times daily — this is the single most effective initial treatment
Gently massage the eyelid after each warm compress session to encourage drainage
Do not attempt to squeeze or puncture the chalazion yourself as this increases infection risk
quick_solutions: Most chalazia resolve spontaneously within 2–8 weeks with warm compress treatment alone
Maintain good eyelid hygiene using diluted baby shampoo or eyelid wipes to prevent recurrence
A short course of topical antibiotic-steroid ointment may be prescribed if infection is also present
Persistent chalazia (beyond 2–3 months) may require surgical incision and drainage by an ophthalmologist under local anesthetic
chalazion.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor if the chalazion is very large, causes significant visual disturbance, becomes acutely painful and red (suggesting infected stye), or has not improved after 3 months of warm compress treatment.

immediate_advice: Keep the affected area clean and dry — wear loose cotton underwear and avoid tight synthetic clothing
Avoid using scented soaps, bubble baths, or douches which disrupt the natural vaginal flora
Over-the-counter antifungal treatments (clotrimazole vaginal cream or miconazole suppository) are appropriate if this is a previously diagnosed recurrent infection
quick_solutions: Single-dose oral fluconazole (150mg) is effective and convenient — available on prescription
Complete the full course of any antifungal treatment even if symptoms improve early
Probiotics containing Lactobacillus species may help restore vaginal flora and reduce recurrence
Avoid unnecessary antibiotic use as antibiotics disrupt the normal flora that prevents yeast overgrowth
vaginal yeast infection.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor if this is your first ever episode (to confirm diagnosis), if symptoms recur more than 4 times per year, if OTC treatments fail, if you are pregnant, if there is pelvic pain or fever, or if the discharge has an unusual odor (which may indicate bacterial vaginosis rather than yeast).

immediate_advice: Soak the affected foot in warm (not hot) salty water for 15–20 minutes twice daily to soften the skin and reduce inflammation
Gently lift the edge of the nail away from the skin using a small piece of clean cotton wool placed underneath after soaking
Wear open-toed footwear or shoes with a wide toe box to reduce pressure on the nail
quick_solutions: Trim nails straight across — never cut them in a curved shape or too short at the corners
Wear well-fitting footwear with adequate toe space at all times
A topical antibiotic cream (mupirocin) may be applied if mild infection is present
Partial nail avulsion (surgical removal of the ingrown nail edge) under local anesthetic is recommended for recurrent or severe cases
ingrown toe nail.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor if the area is significantly red, swollen, and warm with pus (indicates infection requiring antibiotics), if you are diabetic (any foot wound requires prompt medical attention), or if conservative treatment fails after 2 weeks.

immediate_advice: Do not rub the eye — this can worsen corneal damage significantly
If wearing contact lenses, remove them immediately and do not reinsert until evaluated by a doctor
Protect the eye from bright light (wear sunglasses) as photophobia is common with corneal conditions
quick_solutions: Preservative-free artificial tears can provide comfort relief for mild corneal surface conditions
Antibiotic or antiviral eye drops will be prescribed depending on the underlying cause of the corneal disorder
Avoid contact lens wear until the cornea has fully healed and a specialist has cleared you for resumed use
Corneal transplant (keratoplasty) may be recommended for severe or progressive corneal disease
corneal disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent ophthalmological care for sudden eye pain, significant redness, blurred vision, photophobia, or any visible opacity or white spot on the cornea — corneal infections can progress to permanent scarring within hours if untreated.

immediate_advice: Cover the wart with a waterproof plaster when swimming or in shared wet environments to prevent spread
Avoid picking, scratching, or biting warts as this can spread the virus to other body sites
Do not share towels, socks, or footwear with others
quick_solutions: Salicylic acid preparations (gels or plasters) applied daily after soaking and filing the wart are the first-line OTC treatment
Cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen freezing) by a doctor or nurse is effective for resistant warts — typically requires multiple sessions
Many warts resolve spontaneously within 1–2 years without treatment, particularly in children
Maintain immune health as warts persist longer in immunocompromised individuals
viral warts.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor if warts are spreading rapidly, are on the face or genitals (genital warts require specific evaluation and treatment), if they are painful and affecting daily activities, or if they have not responded to 3 months of OTC treatment.

immediate_advice: Call emergency services immediately — use the FAST acronym: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency services
Do not give food, water, or medication by mouth as swallowing may be impaired
Note the exact time symptoms started — this is critical for thrombolysis eligibility (must be within 4.5 hours)
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — thrombolytic therapy (tPA) or mechanical thrombectomy can reverse stroke damage if given within the treatment window
Strict blood pressure and blood sugar control are initiated in hospital immediately
Early mobilization and intensive physiotherapy, speech therapy, and occupational therapy rehabilitation improves recovery outcomes
Secondary prevention (antiplatelet or anticoagulation therapy, statins, antihypertensives) must be initiated before discharge
stroke.when_to_seek_help: Call emergency services immediately for any sudden facial drooping, arm or leg weakness, slurred or absent speech, sudden severe headache, sudden vision loss, or loss of balance. Every minute without treatment results in further irreversible brain damage.
stroke.specialist_recommendation: Neurologist / Stroke Unit Team (Emergency services first)

immediate_advice: Apply warm compresses to the coccygeal area for 10–15 minutes several times daily to encourage drainage if not yet abscessed
Keep the area clean and dry; shave or use depilatory cream to remove hair from the area
Avoid prolonged sitting on hard surfaces — use a donut cushion if necessary
quick_solutions: If acutely infected and fluctuant, incision and drainage under local anesthetic provides rapid relief
Complete the prescribed antibiotic course to clear any surrounding cellulitis
Definitive surgical excision is recommended after the acute infection settles to prevent recurrence
Weight reduction and improved hygiene significantly reduce recurrence risk
pilonidal cyst.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor if the area becomes acutely swollen, red, painful, and fluctuant (abscess requiring drainage), if fever develops, if there is spreading redness (cellulitis), or if a sinus tract forms with persistent discharge.

immediate_advice: Avoid tight hairstyles (braids, ponytails, weaves) that place traction on hair follicles
Use gentle, sulfate-free shampoos and minimize heat styling
Reduce stress through exercise, mindfulness, or counseling as stress is a significant trigger for many types of alopecia
quick_solutions: Minoxidil (topical 5% solution or foam for men; 2% or 5% for women) is first-line treatment for androgenetic alopecia
Intralesional corticosteroid injections are effective for alopecia areata patches
Ensure adequate iron, vitamin D, zinc, and protein intake as nutritional deficiencies cause hair loss
Finasteride (oral, for men only) is effective for androgenetic alopecia but requires ongoing use to maintain results
alopecia.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for any sudden significant hair loss, patchy hair loss, associated scalp scaling or scarring, or hair loss occurring with other systemic symptoms (fatigue, weight change, joint pain) which may indicate an underlying medical condition.

immediate_advice: Avoid heavy lifting (more than 5kg) and activities that increase intra-abdominal pressure during symptomatic periods
Begin pelvic floor muscle exercises (Kegel exercises) immediately — perform 3 sets of 10 contractions daily
Use a supportive panty liner if there is associated urinary incontinence
quick_solutions: Pelvic floor physiotherapy with a specialized women's health physiotherapist is the first-line treatment
A vaginal pessary (ring or shelf type) inserted by a gynecologist provides effective symptom relief without surgery
Manage constipation aggressively with dietary fiber and fluids to reduce straining
Surgical repair (colporrhaphy, sacrocolpopexy) is recommended for significant prolapse causing significant symptoms
pelvic organ prolapse.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor if there is a sensation of a bulge at the vaginal opening, difficulty with urination or defecation, recurrent urinary tract infections, or if the prolapse is causing significant discomfort affecting daily activities.

immediate_advice: Immobilize the arm in the position found using a makeshift sling or splint — do not attempt to straighten a deformed arm
Apply ice wrapped in cloth (not directly) to the fracture area for 20 minutes to reduce swelling
Seek emergency care or go to an emergency department immediately for X-ray and formal immobilization
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately for formal X-ray, reduction if displaced, and casting or splinting
Elevation of the limb above heart level reduces post-fracture swelling
Analgesia with paracetamol and NSAIDs is appropriate for pain management post-stabilization
Physiotherapy is required after immobilization period to restore full range of motion and strength
fracture of the arm.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for any suspected fracture, especially if the arm is deformed, there is numbness or tingling (nerve involvement), pallor or pulselessness in the hand (vascular compromise), or the fracture is open (bone visible through skin).

immediate_advice: Call emergency services immediately — this is a life-threatening neurological emergency
Do not give food, water, or any medication by mouth
Keep the person calm and lying down with head slightly elevated at 30 degrees if possible
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — urgent CT brain imaging is required for diagnosis
Blood pressure control, reversal of anticoagulation, and neurosurgical intervention may all be required
Intensive care unit monitoring for airway protection and neurological deterioration is standard
Rehabilitation (physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy) begins as soon as medically stable
intracranial hemorrhage.when_to_seek_help: Call emergency services immediately for sudden severe headache described as 'the worst of my life', sudden weakness or numbness on one side, sudden speech difficulty, loss of consciousness, or seizure — all may indicate intracranial hemorrhage.
intracranial hemorrhage.specialist_recommendation: Neurosurgeon / Neurologist / Critical Care (Emergency services first)

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for severe hyperkalemia — avoid any potassium-containing foods or supplements
Do not take NSAIDs, ACE inhibitors, or potassium-sparing diuretics until medically reassessed
Seek emergency care if muscle weakness, paralysis, or palpitations are present
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — IV calcium gluconate, insulin with dextrose, and sodium bicarbonate may be required to stabilize cardiac membrane and shift potassium
Dialysis may be required for severe or refractory hyperkalemia
Identify and treat the underlying cause (kidney failure, medications, adrenal insufficiency)
Dietary potassium restriction and sodium polystyrene sulfonate or patiromer may be used for maintenance management
hyperkalemia.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for muscle weakness, chest pain, palpitations, or any ECG changes — severe hyperkalemia can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias with little warning.

immediate_advice: Remove contact lenses immediately and do not reinsert until fully treated and cleared by a doctor
Do not rub the eye as this worsens corneal damage
Apply cool compresses for comfort but do not use any eye drops not prescribed for this specific episode
quick_solutions: Seek urgent ophthalmological evaluation — bacterial keratitis requires intensive topical antibiotic therapy (e.g., ciprofloxacin or moxifloxacin eye drops)
Acanthamoeba or fungal keratitis require specific targeted treatment and are more resistant — contact lens wearers are at particular risk
Do not use topical corticosteroid eye drops without specialist guidance as they can worsen infectious keratitis dramatically
Contact lens hygiene must be thoroughly reviewed and improved to prevent recurrence
cornea infection.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency ophthalmological care within hours for eye redness, pain, photophobia, and discharge in a contact lens wearer — corneal infections can cause permanent vision loss within 24–48 hours if untreated.

immediate_advice: Perform daily saline nasal irrigation with a neti pot or squeeze bottle to clear mucus and reduce inflammation
Use a humidifier in your bedroom to prevent nasal drying, especially during winter
Avoid known environmental triggers including cigarette smoke, strong perfumes, and air pollution
quick_solutions: Intranasal corticosteroid sprays (fluticasone, mometasone) are the first-line treatment and must be used regularly for at least 8–12 weeks to assess benefit
Short courses of oral corticosteroids may be prescribed to reduce severe nasal polyps
Antibiotics are reserved for acute bacterial exacerbations — chronic sinusitis itself is rarely caused by bacteria
Functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS) is recommended when medical treatment fails after adequate trial
chronic sinusitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care for severe headache, swelling around the eyes, visual changes, neck stiffness, or high fever as these may indicate spread of infection to the orbit or intracranially.

immediate_advice: Do not insert objects including cotton buds into the ear canal as this can worsen the cause
Protect ears from further damage by avoiding loud noise environments without hearing protection
Seek evaluation promptly — many causes of conductive hearing loss are reversible if treated early
quick_solutions: Ear wax impaction (a common cause) can be treated with olive oil drops or microsuction by a nurse or doctor
Acute otitis media causing conductive hearing loss typically resolves with appropriate antibiotic treatment
Glue ear (otitis media with effusion) in children may resolve spontaneously or require grommet insertion
Hearing aids are effective for persistent conductive hearing loss where surgical correction is not suitable
conductive hearing loss.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for any sudden onset of hearing loss (requires urgent assessment within 24–48 hours), hearing loss in one ear only, associated vertigo or tinnitus, or hearing loss following head trauma.

immediate_advice: Avoid heavy lifting and straining activities that increase intra-abdominal pressure

Seek emergency care immediately if the hernia becomes hard, irreducible (cannot push back in), or is associated with severe pain, vomiting, or a red/purple discoloration — this indicates strangulation
A supportive hernia truss or belt may temporarily relieve discomfort but does not treat the hernia
quick_solutions: All symptomatic hernias should be surgically repaired to prevent strangulation — the definitive treatment is herniorrhaphy (open or laparoscopic)
Manage chronic cough, constipation, and prostate enlargement which increase hernia recurrence risk
Maintain a healthy body weight as obesity significantly increases hernia risk and complicates repair
Avoid smoking as it impairs wound healing and increases cough, both of which worsen hernias
abdominal hernia.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for a hernia that cannot be pushed back in (irreducible), severe pain at the hernia site, vomiting, bowel obstruction symptoms, or skin over the hernia turning red, purple, or dark — these indicate strangulation requiring emergency surgery.

immediate_advice: Move to a calm, quiet environment if experiencing acute anxiety or paranoia from cannabis use
Do not drive or operate machinery — cannabis significantly impairs reaction time and judgment
Stay hydrated and avoid further use until symptoms resolve
quick_solutions: Motivational interviewing and Cognitive Behavioral Therapy are evidence-based treatments for cannabis use disorder
Gradual reduction strategies with support are more effective than abrupt cessation for heavy long-term users
Address co-occurring mental health conditions (anxiety, depression) which often underlie heavy cannabis use
Support groups and peer recovery programs provide valuable sustained accountability
marijuana abuse.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent psychiatric care for cannabis-induced psychosis (paranoia, delusions, hallucinations), acute panic attacks not settling, or if cannabis use is significantly impairing daily functioning, relationships, or mental health.

immediate_advice: Avoid lying down immediately after eating — remain upright for at least 2–3 hours after meals
Eat smaller, more frequent meals rather than large portions
Avoid trigger foods: fatty foods, spicy foods, citrus, chocolate, caffeine, and alcohol
quick_solutions: Over-the-counter antacids (calcium carbonate, magnesium hydroxide) provide rapid but temporary relief
H2 blockers (famotidine) or proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole) are more effective for frequent indigestion
Eat slowly and avoid swallowing air by not talking while chewing
Raise the head of your bed by 15–20cm if symptoms are worse at night or on lying down
indigestion.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor if indigestion is persistent (more than 2 weeks), associated with weight loss, difficulty swallowing, persistent vomiting, black stools, or if you are over 55 with new onset indigestion — these features require endoscopic investigation to exclude serious pathology.

immediate_advice: Rest the affected joint and avoid activities that provoke or worsen the pain
Apply ice wrapped in cloth for 15–20 minutes every 2–3 hours during the first 48–72 hours to reduce inflammation
Elevate the affected limb if swelling is present
quick_solutions: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) reduce inflammation and pain effectively in the acute phase
Once acute inflammation settles, gentle range-of-motion exercises prevent stiffness and strengthen surrounding muscles
Corticosteroid injection directly into the bursa provides rapid, targeted relief for resistant cases
Address the underlying cause: repetitive occupational movements, poor posture, or biomechanical issues require modification
bursitis.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor if the bursa is acutely red, hot, and very tender (infected bursitis/septic bursitis requiring antibiotic aspiration), if fever is present, if symptoms do not improve within 2 weeks, or if there is significant restriction of joint movement.

immediate_advice: Sit upright or in a high Fowler's position (legs dangling) — this reduces preload and relieves breathlessness immediately
Call emergency services immediately for severe sudden breathlessness, pink frothy sputum, or inability to speak in full sentences
Avoid all salt and fluid excess immediately
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — IV diuretics (furosemide), oxygen, and hemodynamic monitoring are the standard acute interventions
Optimize heart failure medications (ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics) under cardiology guidance
Restrict sodium intake to less than 2g/day and monitor daily weights to detect early fluid accumulation
Identify and treat the underlying precipitant: new arrhythmia, medication non-compliance, dietary indiscretion, infection
pulmonary congestion.when_to_seek_help: Call emergency services immediately for sudden severe breathlessness, pink or blood-tinged frothy sputum, inability to lie flat, oxygen saturation below 94%, or any signs of respiratory failure.
pulmonary congestion.specialist_recommendation: Cardiologist / Pulmonologist / Critical Care (Emergency services first)

immediate_advice: Apply broad-spectrum sunscreen (SPF 50+) daily to all sun-exposed skin regardless of weather
Wear protective clothing, wide-brimmed hats, and seek shade between 10am and 4pm
Do not pick or scratch lesions as this does not treat them and can cause bleeding and infection
quick_solutions: Cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen) is the most common treatment for individual lesions — quick, effective, and done in clinic
Topical field therapy (5-fluorouracil cream, imiquimod, ingenol mebutate) treats multiple lesions simultaneously
Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is effective for large areas of sun-damaged skin with multiple lesions
Annual skin checks with a dermatologist are recommended as actinic keratoses are precancerous and can progress to squamous cell carcinoma
actinic keratosis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a dermatologist for any actinic keratosis that becomes thickened, raised, ulcerated, or bleeds — these features suggest malignant transformation to squamous cell carcinoma requiring urgent biopsy.

immediate_advice: Administer age-appropriate paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain and fever relief — this is the priority in initial management
Apply a warm cloth or heat pad over the affected ear for comfort
Keep the child upright as much as possible to encourage Eustachian tube drainage
quick_solutions: Most cases in children over 2 years resolve within 72 hours without antibiotics — watchful waiting with analgesia is appropriate initially
Antibiotics (amoxicillin first line) are indicated for children under 2 years, both ears affected, very severe symptoms, or if no improvement after 48–72 hours
Decongestants and antihistamines do not improve outcomes and are not recommended in children
Recurrent acute otitis media (4+ episodes per year) warrants ENT referral for grommet consideration
acute otitis media.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor promptly for ear pain with high fever, ear discharge, severe headache or neck stiffness (meningitis), swelling behind the ear (mastoiditis), facial nerve weakness, or if a child appears very unwell or toxic.
acute otitis media.specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Pediatrician / Otolaryngologist (ENT) for recurrent cases

immediate_advice: Avoid driving at night if vision is significantly blurred or distorted until corrected
Ensure adequate lighting when reading or doing close work to reduce eye strain
Schedule an optometrist or ophthalmologist appointment for refraction testing and prescription glasses or contact lenses
quick_solutions: Corrective lenses (spectacles or toric contact lenses) are the primary management and provide immediate visual correction
Refractive surgery (LASIK, PRK, LASEK) can permanently correct astigmatism in suitable candidates
Regular eye examinations every 1–2 years ensure the prescription remains accurate as astigmatism can progress
Orthokeratology (overnight rigid contact lenses) is an option for myopic astigmatism, particularly in children
astigmatism.when_to_seek_help: Consult an optometrist or ophthalmologist for regular prescription updates. Seek urgent evaluation if there is sudden change in vision, monocular diplopia (double vision in one eye), or progressive worsening of astigmatism.

immediate_advice: Use your prescribed short-acting bronchodilator (salbutamol or ipratropium) immediately during acute breathlessness
Sit upright and use pursed-lip breathing (inhale through nose, exhale slowly through pursed lips) to reduce air trapping
Call emergency services for severe breathlessness at rest, blue lips or fingertips, confusion, or rapid deterioration
quick_solutions: Stop smoking immediately — it is the single most effective intervention to slow COPD progression
Use prescribed inhalers (LAMA, LABA, ICS combinations) correctly and consistently as directed by your respiratory team
Annual influenza and pneumococcal vaccinations are strongly recommended to prevent exacerbation triggers
Pulmonary rehabilitation program significantly improves exercise capacity, quality of life, and reduces hospitalizations
chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (c.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for severe breathlessness, cyanosis (blue lips/fingertips), altered consciousness, oxygen saturation below 90%, or an exacerbation not responding to increased bronchodilator use within 30 minutes.

immediate_advice: Apply heat to the affected area of the spine for 15–20 minutes to relieve stiffness and muscle spasm
Avoid prolonged static postures — take a short walk or stretch every 30–45 minutes if seated at work
Use a firm, supportive mattress and ergonomic pillow to maintain spinal alignment during sleep
quick_solutions: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) are first-line for pain and inflammation management
Physiotherapy focusing on postural correction, core strengthening, and spinal mobility exercises is essential
Cervical or lumbar traction and heat therapy provided by a physiotherapist can provide significant relief
Epidural steroid injections may be recommended for nerve root compression causing radiating pain
spondylosis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor if pain radiates down the arm or leg, if there is numbness, tingling, or weakness in the limbs, if bladder or bowel control is affected, or if pain is progressively worsening despite conservative management.

immediate_advice: Ensure adequate fluid intake — cold water, ice chips, and cold milk help soothe painful mouth sores and prevent dehydration
Give age-appropriate paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever and pain relief
Avoid acidic or spicy foods which worsen mouth ulcer pain
quick_solutions: Herpangina is caused by Coxsackievirus and is self-limiting — antibiotics are not effective and not indicated
Offer cold soft foods (yogurt, ice cream, cold purees) to maintain nutrition while mouth is painful
Saltwater gargles or topical anesthetic mouth rinses (lidocaine gel) can reduce ulcer pain in older children and adults
Monitor fluid intake closely in young children — dehydration is the primary complication to prevent
herpangina.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care for inability to swallow fluids (dehydration risk), fever above 39.5°C not responding to antipyretics, severe headache with neck stiffness (meningitis), or symptoms persisting beyond 10 days.

immediate_advice: Apply ice wrapped in a cloth for 20 minutes every 2 hours for the first 48 hours to reduce swelling
Rest the shoulder and use a sling if movement is very painful or if dislocation is suspected
Do not attempt to relocate a dislocated shoulder yourself — seek emergency care immediately
quick_solutions: Seek emergency evaluation if dislocation, fracture, or complete tendon rupture is suspected
NSAIDs (ibuprofen) provide effective pain and inflammation relief in the acute phase
Physiotherapy is essential after the acute phase for full restoration of range of motion and strength
Rotator cuff tears may require surgical repair depending on severity and functional demand
injury to the shoulder.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for suspected dislocation (visible deformity, inability to move arm), fracture, numbness or tingling down the arm, or complete inability to lift the arm after trauma.

immediate_advice: Seek urgent medical evaluation immediately — do not massage the leg or apply heat if DVT is suspected
Elevate the affected leg when resting to reduce swelling
Avoid prolonged immobility — if confirmed, early mobilization with anticoagulation is encouraged
quick_solutions: Anticoagulation therapy (low molecular weight heparin, rivaroxaban, or apixaban) is initiated immediately after confirmation by Doppler ultrasound
Compression stockings reduce post-thrombotic syndrome and swelling after acute DVT
Identify and address underlying risk factors: prolonged immobility, recent surgery, malignancy, oral contraceptive use, thrombophilia
Long-haul travel precautions (hydration, leg exercises, compression stockings) are recommended after DVT recovery
deep vein thrombosis (dvt).when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for sudden shortness of breath, chest pain, or coughing blood — these indicate pulmonary embolism which is a life-threatening complication of DVT.
deep vein thrombosis (dvt).specialist_recommendation: Hematologist / Vascular Medicine Specialist / General Physician

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for complete heart block — do not delay
Avoid strenuous physical activity until the degree of heart block is determined by a cardiologist
If prescribed medications known to worsen heart block (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers, digoxin), do not stop them without medical guidance — report symptoms promptly
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — temporary or permanent pacemaker implantation is the definitive treatment for high-degree heart block
Identify and reverse reversible causes: electrolyte abnormalities (hyperkalemia), drug toxicity, ischemia, or Lyme disease
Continuous cardiac monitoring is required in hospital until definitive management is established
Regular cardiology follow-up is mandatory for all patients with any degree of heart block
heart block.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for fainting, near-fainting, severe dizziness, very slow pulse, chest pain, or sudden shortness of breath — these may indicate high-degree heart block with hemodynamic compromise.

immediate_advice: Follow the recommended colonoscopy surveillance schedule provided by your gastroenterologist without delay
Maintain a high-fiber diet with adequate fruit, vegetables, and whole grains to promote bowel health
Report any rectal bleeding, change in bowel habits, or unexplained weight loss to your doctor promptly
quick_solutions: Polypectomy (removal during colonoscopy) is performed at the time of detection for most accessible polyps
Surveillance colonoscopy intervals (1, 3, or 5 years) depend on number, size, and histology of polyps removed
Maintain a healthy body weight, exercise regularly, and limit red and processed meat intake to reduce polyp recurrence risk
Quit smoking and minimize alcohol consumption as both are associated with increased polyp risk
colonic polyp.when_to_seek_help: Consult a gastroenterologist for any rectal bleeding, iron deficiency anemia, unexplained change in bowel habits lasting more than 4 weeks, or if due for scheduled colonoscopy surveillance.

immediate_advice: Establish a structured, predictable daily routine with clear and consistent expectations
Break tasks into small, manageable steps and use visual reminders, checklists, and timers
Minimize distractions in the study or work environment — use noise-cancelling headphones or a quiet space
quick_solutions: Behavioral therapy is the recommended first-line treatment for children under 6 — medication is adjunctive
Stimulant medications (methylphenidate, amphetamine salts) are evidence-based and highly effective when properly titrated
Parent training in behavior management techniques significantly reduces ADHD-related functional impairment in children
School-based accommodations (extended time, preferential seating, written instructions) should be formally requested
attention deficit hyperactivity disorder.when_to_seek_help: Consult a pediatrician or psychiatrist for formal assessment if ADHD symptoms are causing significant impairment at school, work, or in relationships. Seek urgent care if stimulant medication causes chest pain, palpitations, or significant mood changes.

immediate_advice: Apply ice for the first 48 hours to reduce inflammation, then switch to heat for ongoing relief
Avoid activities that provoke or worsen chest pain such as heavy lifting, reaching overhead, or sleeping on the affected side
Reassure yourself that Tietze syndrome, while painful, is not dangerous and does not affect the heart
quick_solutions: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) are the primary treatment and are highly effective for costochondral inflammation
Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac gel) applied to the affected area provide localized relief with fewer systemic side effects
Corticosteroid injection into the affected costochondral joint provides significant relief in resistant cases
Physiotherapy and postural correction help reduce recurrence, particularly in patients with desk-based work
tietze syndrome.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor to confirm the diagnosis and rule out cardiac causes of chest pain. Seek emergency care immediately if chest pain is severe, radiates to the arm or jaw, is associated with sweating, shortness of breath, or palpitations.

immediate_advice: Seek urgent neurological evaluation — new cranial nerve palsy requires same-day or next-day imaging
If the eye cannot close fully (facial nerve palsy), use lubricating eye drops during the day and eye ointment with tape at night to prevent corneal damage
Do not drive if double vision is present
quick_solutions: Urgent MRI or CT brain imaging is required to exclude stroke, aneurysm, tumor, or demyelination as the cause
Bell's palsy (idiopathic facial nerve palsy) is treated with oral prednisolone (within 72 hours of onset) and antiviral medications
Eye protection measures for incomplete eye closure are critical to prevent corneal ulceration
Physiotherapy (facial exercises) accelerates recovery in facial nerve palsy
cranial nerve palsy.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for sudden onset double vision, facial drooping, eyelid drooping, or any new cranial nerve weakness — particularly if associated with headache, neck stiffness, or other neurological symptoms.

immediate_advice: Apply the RICE protocol: Rest, Ice (wrapped in cloth for 20 minutes every 2 hours), Compression with a bandage, and Elevation above heart level
Immobilize the arm in the position found if fracture or significant injury is suspected
Seek emergency evaluation if there is significant deformity, inability to move the arm, or numbness and tingling
quick_solutions: X-ray is required to rule out fracture before commencing soft tissue injury management
NSAIDs provide effective pain and swelling relief after significant arm injuries
Physiotherapy is essential after any significant arm injury to restore strength and mobility
Occupational therapy may be required if hand or wrist function is affected
injury to the arm.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for visible deformity, inability to move fingers or wrist, pallor or absent pulse in the hand, open wound over the injury, or severe swelling that develops rapidly.

immediate_advice: Continue gentle use of the affected limb — immobilization dramatically worsens CRPS outcomes
Apply gentle desensitization techniques (touching different textures to the affected area) as tolerated
Seek specialist referral urgently — early treatment within 3 months significantly improves outcomes
quick_solutions: Physiotherapy and graded motor imagery are the cornerstone treatments and must begin as early as possible
Medications including gabapentinoids, NSAIDs, tricyclic antidepressants, and bisphosphonates may reduce pain
Mirror therapy and graded motor imagery are evidence-based techniques that reduce pain and improve function
Sympathetic nerve blocks or spinal cord stimulation may be offered by a pain specialist for refractory cases
complex regional pain syndrome.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent specialist referral for any burning pain, skin color and temperature changes, or swelling in a limb following injury or surgery — early diagnosis and treatment are critical to prevent chronic disability.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care for any trunk injury following significant trauma, fall from height, or road traffic accident
Do not move the person if spinal injury is suspected — maintain spinal immobilization until emergency services arrive
Apply pressure to external bleeding wounds with a clean cloth
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately for major trauma — internal organ injury must be excluded with CT imaging
Rib fractures are managed with adequate analgesia (to allow deep breathing) and physiotherapy
Abdominal wall muscle strains are treated with rest, ice, compression, and gradual return to activity
Breathing exercises and incentive spirometry are essential after rib fractures to prevent pneumonia
injury to the trunk.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for any significant trunk trauma, difficulty breathing, severe abdominal pain, blood in urine after blunt trauma, or any suspected spinal injury.
injury to the trunk.specialist_recommendation: Emergency Physician / Trauma Surgeon / Orthopedic Surgeon

immediate_advice: Avoid potential irritants: scented soaps, fabric softeners, synthetic underwear, tight clothing, and harsh toilet paper
Apply cool gel packs wrapped in a soft cloth to the vulvar area for 10–15 minutes for pain relief
Use only water for cleansing the vulvar area — no soaps, wipes, or douches
quick_solutions: Topical lidocaine (5% ointment) applied before intercourse or activities can provide effective localized analgesia
Physical therapy with a pelvic floor specialist is highly effective for hypertonic pelvic floor dysfunction contributing to vulvodynia
Oral medications (amitriptyline, gabapentin, duloxetine) reduce neuropathic pain in the vulvar region
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy and sex therapy address the significant psychological and relational impact of chronic vulvodynia
vulvodynia.when_to_seek_help: Consult a gynecologist for persistent vulvar pain lasting more than 3 months, pain that is worsening, or pain significantly affecting quality of life, relationships, and sexual function.
vulvodynia.specialist_recommendation: Gynecologist / Vulvar Specialist / Pelvic Floor Physiotherapist

immediate_advice: Apply a heat pad to the lower abdomen and lower back during painful menstrual periods
Take NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, mefenamic acid) starting 1–2 days before the expected onset of menstruation
Rest during severe pain episodes and avoid activities that markedly worsen symptoms
quick_solutions: Hormonal therapies (combined oral contraceptive pill, progestins, levonorgestrel IUD) reduce pain and slow disease progression
GnRH agonists (used for 3–6 months) significantly reduce endometriotic lesions but cause menopausal side effects
Laparoscopic excision of endometriotic lesions provides the best surgical outcome for pain and fertility
Fertility specialist referral is recommended early if conception is desired as endometriosis significantly affects fertility
endometriosis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a gynecologist for severe menstrual pain not controlled by NSAIDs, chronic pelvic pain, pain during intercourse, painful defecation during periods, or if you are struggling to conceive.

immediate_advice: Seek urgent medical evaluation — vasculitis can involve the kidneys, lungs, and nervous system and requires prompt diagnosis
Do not stop prescribed corticosteroids or immunosuppressants without medical guidance
Rest during acute flares and report any new symptoms (blood in urine, shortness of breath, visual changes) immediately
quick_solutions: High-dose corticosteroids (prednisolone) are the cornerstone of acute vasculitis management
Immunosuppressive agents (cyclophosphamide, azathioprine, methotrexate, rituximab) are used for maintenance remission in systemic vasculitis
Regular monitoring of kidney function, inflammatory markers, and ANCA levels guides treatment adjustment
Infection prevention (vaccinations, prophylactic co-trimoxazole) is critical in patients on immunosuppression
vasculitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for blood in urine, sudden vision loss, significant hemoptysis, severe abdominal pain, or any new neurological deficit — these indicate serious organ involvement requiring immediate intervention.

immediate_advice: Remove the person from activity immediately — return to sport or exercise is absolutely prohibited on the day of injury
Seek emergency care immediately if there is loss of consciousness lasting more than 1 minute, seizure, repeated vomiting, worsening headache, or deteriorating consciousness
Do not take NSAIDs or aspirin in the first 24 hours — use paracetamol only for headache
quick_solutions: Physical and cognitive rest for 24–48 hours is recommended, then gradual return to activity following a structured protocol
Avoid screen use (phones, computers, TV) that worsens headache or visual symptoms during the early recovery phase
Sleep is beneficial — the myth that concussed patients should not sleep is outdated and incorrect
Return to school, work, and sport should follow a stepwise graduated protocol under medical supervision
concussion.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for loss of consciousness, seizure, repeated vomiting, worsening headache, significant confusion, weakness or numbness in limbs, or unequal pupils — these indicate serious intracranial injury.
concussion.specialist_recommendation: Emergency Physician / Sports Medicine Doctor / Neurologist (for prolonged post-concussion syndrome)

immediate_advice: Maintain adequate hydration — at least 1.5–2 liters of water daily unless restricted by a doctor
Avoid bladder irritants: caffeine, alcohol, carbonated drinks, citrus juices, and spicy foods
Do not delay urination when the urge is felt as holding urine for prolonged periods worsens bladder dysfunction
quick_solutions: Keep a bladder diary (recording fluid intake, urination frequency, and episodes of leakage) — this guides diagnosis and treatment
Pelvic floor exercises improve bladder control for both stress and urge incontinence
Bladder training (gradually extending time between voids) is effective for overactive bladder
Anticholinergic medications or beta-3 agonists (mirabegron) may be prescribed for overactive bladder symptoms
bladder disorder.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for blood in urine, significant pain during urination, recurrent infections, sudden loss of bladder control, or symptoms significantly affecting daily activities and quality of life.

immediate_advice: Rest — this is the most important treatment; activity intolerance is normal in the acute phase
Stay well hydrated with fluids and use paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever and sore throat pain
Avoid contact sports and heavy lifting for at least 3–4 weeks due to splenomegaly (enlarged spleen) and risk of splenic rupture
quick_solutions: Mononucleosis is caused by Epstein-Barr virus — antibiotics are not effective and amoxicillin/ampicillin must be avoided as it causes a characteristic widespread rash
Throat discomfort can be managed with salt water gargles, throat lozenges, and cold fluids
Monitor for airway compromise if tonsils become very enlarged — seek emergency care if breathing becomes difficult
Most patients recover fully within 2–4 weeks though fatigue may persist for several months
mononucleosis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for severe throat swelling causing difficulty breathing or swallowing, sudden severe left upper abdominal pain (splenic rupture), very high fever not responding to antipyretics, or jaundice.
mononucleosis.specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Infectious Disease Specialist / Hematologist (for complications)

immediate_advice: Identify and avoid triggers specific to your neuralgia type — for trigeminal neuralgia these include eating, talking, touching the face, and cold wind
Apply gentle heat or cold to the affected area if it provides relief without worsening pain
Avoid tight clothing or pressure over affected nerves in post-herpetic or peripheral neuralgia
quick_solutions: Carbamazepine is first-line pharmacological treatment for trigeminal neuralgia and is highly effective
Gabapentin and pregabalin are effective for most neuropathic pain conditions including post-herpetic neuralgia
Topical lidocaine patches or capsaicin cream provide localized relief for post-herpetic neuralgia
Microvascular decompression surgery offers long-term pain freedom for medication-resistant trigeminal neuralgia
neuralgia.when_to_seek_help: Consult a neurologist for any severe, recurrent, or electric shock-like facial or nerve pain. Seek urgent care if neuralgia follows a herpes zoster rash (to initiate antivirals within 72 hours and prevent post-herpetic neuralgia).

immediate_advice: Seek emergency psychiatric assessment immediately if the person is in a manic episode with dangerous behavior, psychosis, or if there is any risk of self-harm or harm to others
Do not stop prescribed mood stabilizers abruptly — this can precipitate severe rebound mania or depression
Maintain a regular sleep-wake schedule as sleep disruption is the most common trigger for both manic and depressive episodes
quick_solutions: Mood stabilizers (lithium, valproate, lamotrigine) are the cornerstone of long-term bipolar management
Psychoeducation about the illness, triggers, and early warning signs is critical for self-management
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy adapted for bipolar disorder improves outcome and reduces relapse rates
A clear crisis plan developed with the treating psychiatrist should be documented and accessible to family
bipolar disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency psychiatric care for acute mania with dangerous behavior, psychosis, severe depression with suicidal ideation, or any abrupt change in behavior, sleep, or judgment suggesting a mood episode.

immediate_advice: Avoid lying down for at least 3 hours after meals and elevate the head of your bed by 15–20cm
Identify and eliminate personal trigger foods: fatty foods, chocolate, caffeine, alcohol, citrus, spicy food, peppermint, and tomato-based products
Eat smaller, more frequent meals and avoid eating within 3 hours of bedtime
quick_solutions: Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, lansoprazole) are the most effective medical treatment — take 30 minutes before the first meal
H2 blockers (famotidine) provide effective symptom relief and are suitable for on-demand use
Lose excess weight — even modest weight reduction (5–10%) significantly reduces reflux symptoms
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin where possible as they directly irritate the esophageal and gastric mucosa
gastroesophageal reflux disease (gerd).when_to_seek_help: See a doctor for symptoms persisting beyond 2 weeks, difficulty swallowing, persistent vomiting, unintentional weight loss, chest pain, or if symptoms start after age 55 — endoscopy is required to exclude Barrett's esophagus or malignancy.

immediate_advice: Keep hands and feet warm at all times — wear gloves and thermal socks, even indoors in cold weather, to prevent Raynaud's episodes
Moisturize the skin frequently with fragrance-free emollients to prevent skin tightening and cracking
Avoid smoking entirely as it markedly worsens vascular complications of scleroderma
quick_solutions: Calcium channel blockers (nifedipine, amlodipine) are first-line for Raynaud's phenomenon associated with scleroderma
Proton pump inhibitors are required for virtually all scleroderma patients due to severe esophageal dysmotility and reflux
Regular pulmonary function tests and echocardiograms are essential for early detection of interstitial lung disease and pulmonary hypertension
Physiotherapy preserves hand function and prevents contractures — begin early and continue regularly
scleroderma.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care for new or worsening breathlessness (pulmonary hypertension or ILD), hypertensive crisis with acute kidney injury (scleroderma renal crisis — requires emergency ACE inhibitor therapy), or digital ulcers not healing.

immediate_advice: If conscious and able to swallow, give 15–20g of fast-acting carbohydrate immediately: 4–6 glucose tablets, 150ml fruit juice, or 5–6 jelly beans
Recheck blood glucose after 15 minutes and repeat if still below 4 mmol/L
Follow with a slower-acting carbohydrate snack (biscuits, toast) once blood glucose has normalized
quick_solutions: If unconscious, do not give anything by mouth — call emergency services and administer glucagon injection if available
Identify and address the cause: missed meal, excess insulin, unusual physical activity, or alcohol consumption
Review insulin doses and diabetes medication with your endocrinologist if hypoglycemia is recurrent
Wear medical alert identification and carry glucose tablets at all times if diabetic
hypoglycemia.when_to_seek_help: Call emergency services immediately for loss of consciousness, seizures, severe confusion, or if the person cannot be roused. Consult your doctor for recurrent hypoglycemic episodes to review and adjust medication regimen.

immediate_advice: Exclude pregnancy as the first step — a home pregnancy test is an essential initial investigation
Review recent changes in weight, exercise intensity, stress levels, and medications as all can suppress menstruation
Avoid excessive exercise and caloric restriction while awaiting investigation results
quick_solutions: Hormonal blood tests (FSH, LH, prolactin, thyroid function, estradiol) and pelvic ultrasound are the standard initial investigations
Address reversible causes: normalize body weight, reduce excessive exercise, manage chronic stress
Hormone replacement or combined oral contraceptive may be prescribed to restore cycling and protect bone density
Investigate for PCOS, hyperprolactinemia, premature ovarian insufficiency, and thyroid dysfunction
idiopathic absence of menstruation.when_to_seek_help: Consult a gynecologist if menstruation is absent for more than 3 months (excluding pregnancy), if associated with hot flushes, galactorrhea (breast milk without pregnancy), or significant weight change.

immediate_advice: Eat smaller, more frequent meals and avoid eating within 3 hours of lying down
Elevate the head of the bed by 15–20cm to reduce nighttime reflux
Avoid bending over or wearing tight waistbands, especially after meals
