immediate_advice: Keep the ear dry at all times — avoid swimming and use a cotton wool ball with petroleum jelly to protect the ear during showering
Do not insert cotton buds, ear drops, or any objects into the ear with a suspected perforation
Avoid nose-blowing forcefully as this increases middle ear pressure and can worsen the perforation
quick_solutions: Most small traumatic perforations heal spontaneously within 6–8 weeks — protection from water and infection during this period is essential
Antibiotic ear drops (ciprofloxacin) are prescribed if concurrent infection (otitis media) is present
Audiological assessment is important to quantify the degree of associated hearing loss
Tympanoplasty (surgical repair of the eardrum) is recommended for perforations not healing spontaneously after 3 months or causing significant hearing loss
ear drum damage.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent ENT evaluation for hearing loss after ear drum injury, discharge from the ear, vertigo, or facial weakness — these suggest more extensive injury to the middle ear or inner ear structures.

immediate_advice: Recognize that erectile dysfunction is a common medical condition — it affects up to 50% of men over 40 and is highly treatable
Avoid recreational drugs, excessive alcohol, and tobacco which all directly impair erectile function
Discuss concerns openly with your partner and with your doctor — psychological factors play a significant role
quick_solutions: PDE5 inhibitors (sildenafil/Viagra, tadalafil/Cialis, vardenafil) are first-line pharmacological treatment and are effective in approximately 70% of men — take as prescribed
Cardiovascular risk factor assessment is mandatory — erectile dysfunction is often the first presentation of occult coronary artery disease or diabetes
Testosterone testing is appropriate to exclude hypogonadism as a cause, particularly in men with low libido
Psychosexual therapy and couples counseling address performance anxiety and relationship factors which commonly contribute to ED
erectile dysfunction.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for persistent erectile dysfunction — it is a treatable condition with multiple effective options. All men with new onset ED should be screened for cardiovascular disease, diabetes, and hypogonadism.

immediate_advice: Increase fluid intake to at least 2–3 liters of water daily to dilute urine and encourage the passing of any blood clots
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin which can worsen urinary bleeding
All hematuria (blood in urine) requires medical evaluation — even a single episode of visible blood must never be dismissed as benign without investigation
quick_solutions: Urinalysis, urine culture, and blood tests (renal function, PSA in men over 45) are the essential initial investigations
CT urogram (CT of the kidneys, ureters, and bladder) is the gold standard imaging investigation for visible hematuria
Cystoscopy (examination of the bladder lining) is required for all patients over 40 with visible hematuria to exclude bladder cancer
Common benign causes (vigorous exercise, urinary tract infection, kidney stone) must be confirmed by excluding sinister pathology first
temporary or benign blood in urine.when_to_seek_help: All episodes of visible blood in urine require prompt medical evaluation regardless of associated symptoms. Seek emergency care for heavy bleeding with clot retention causing difficulty urinating, or blood in urine with severe flank pain and fever.

immediate_advice: Keep feet clean and dry — change socks daily and wear moisture-wicking socks in breathable footwear
Do not share nail clippers, files, or footwear with others to prevent spread
Trim affected nails as short as possible and file them thin to improve topical treatment penetration
quick_solutions: Oral terbinafine (250mg daily for 6 weeks for fingernails, 12 weeks for toenails) is the most effective treatment with cure rates of 70–80%
Topical antifungal nail lacquers (amorolfine, ciclopirox) are used for mild superficial disease or when oral treatment is contraindicated
Treatment requires patience — infected nails take 6–12 months to fully clear even after fungal eradication
Confirm diagnosis with nail clipping for microscopy and culture before starting treatment — discolored nails are not always fungal
onychomycosis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor or podiatrist for nail changes that are painful, spreading to other nails, or associated with skin infection, particularly if you are diabetic, as nail infections can be a portal of entry for foot infections.

immediate_advice: Maintain adequate hydration to keep urine dilute and reduce urethral irritation
Avoid urethral irritants: caffeinated drinks, alcohol, and acidic juices if urethral sensitivity is heightened
Seek prompt evaluation for any urethral discharge, pain, or blood — do not self-treat without diagnosis
quick_solutions: Formal diagnosis requires urinalysis, urine culture, urethral swab for STI testing, and potentially urethroscopy or urodynamics
Specific treatment depends on the underlying cause: antibiotics for infection, urethral dilation for stricture, or behavioral modification for urethral syndrome
Urethral caruncle (small urethral polyp common in post-menopausal women) is treated with topical estrogen cream or surgical excision
Alpha-blockers improve urethral smooth muscle relaxation and reduce obstructive symptoms in urethral dysfunction
urethral disorder.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for persistent urethral discharge, blood at the urethral meatus, pain during urination not resolving within a week, difficulty urinating, or any urethral lump or lesion.

immediate_advice: If a tick is attached, remove it immediately with fine-tipped tweezers by grasping close to the skin surface and pulling upward with steady even pressure — do not twist or crush the tick
Disinfect the bite site with rubbing alcohol or soap and water after removal
Seek medical evaluation within 72 hours of a tick bite from an endemic area for consideration of prophylactic doxycycline
quick_solutions: Early Lyme disease (erythema migrans rash) is treated with doxycycline 100mg twice daily for 14–21 days — early treatment is highly effective and prevents dissemination
Disseminated Lyme disease (neurological, cardiac, or joint involvement) requires IV ceftriaxone and specialist management
Wear long sleeves, long trousers tucked into socks, and insect repellents containing DEET in tick-endemic wooded areas
Tick checks after outdoor activity in endemic areas and prompt removal of attached ticks dramatically reduce transmission risk
lyme disease.when_to_seek_help: Seek prompt medical evaluation for a spreading bulls-eye rash (erythema migrans) after a tick bite, flu-like illness after outdoor activity in an endemic area, facial palsy, palpitations, or joint swelling appearing weeks after a tick bite.
lyme disease.specialist_recommendation: Infectious Disease Specialist / Rheumatologist (for Lyme arthritis) / Neurologist (for Lyme neuroborreliosis)

immediate_advice: Begin prescribed iron supplementation (ferrous sulfate or ferrous fumarate) on an empty stomach if tolerated — take with vitamin C (orange juice) to enhance absorption
Avoid taking iron supplements with tea, coffee, dairy, antacids, or calcium supplements as these significantly reduce absorption
Rest during periods of significant fatigue and avoid strenuous exercise until hemoglobin has improved
quick_solutions: Identify and treat the source of blood loss — iron deficiency anemia is not a diagnosis in itself, it is a consequence of blood loss or malabsorption that requires investigation
Dietary sources of iron (red meat, lentils, fortified cereals, dark leafy greens) support treatment but are insufficient alone for significant deficiency
IV iron infusion is preferred when oral iron is not tolerated, absorption is impaired, or rapid repletion is required
Recheck complete blood count and ferritin after 3 months of treatment to confirm response and after 6 months to confirm iron stores are replenished
iron deficiency anemia.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for all cases of iron deficiency anemia in adult men and post-menopausal women — GI blood loss from cancer must be excluded urgently. Seek urgent care for severe fatigue, palpitations, breathlessness at rest, or hemoglobin below 7g/dL.

immediate_advice: Call emergency services immediately — ARDS is a life-threatening critical illness requiring intensive care
Maintain the patient in an upright sitting position while awaiting emergency services to maximize ventilation
Do not delay emergency care — ARDS progresses rapidly and mortality increases significantly with delayed treatment
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — mechanical ventilation using low tidal volume (lung-protective) strategy is the cornerstone of ARDS management
Prone positioning (face-down ventilation) significantly reduces mortality in moderate-to-severe ARDS
Identify and aggressively treat the precipitating cause: sepsis, pneumonia, aspiration, pancreatitis, or trauma
Neuromuscular blocking agents and conservative fluid strategy are evidence-based components of ARDS management in the ICU
acute respiratory distress syndrome (ard.when_to_seek_help: Call emergency services immediately for severe breathlessness, rapidly worsening respiratory failure, oxygen saturation below 90% despite supplemental oxygen, cyanosis, confusion, or any critically ill patient with bilateral lung infiltrates on imaging.

immediate_advice: Seek medical evaluation to determine the specific type and cause of anemia before self-treating — different causes require entirely different treatments
Rest during significant fatigue and avoid strenuous exercise until anemia is adequately treated
Maintain adequate nutrition including iron-rich foods, folate (green vegetables), and B12 (meat, dairy, fortified foods)
quick_solutions: Blood tests including full blood count, reticulocyte count, iron studies, B12, folate, and peripheral blood film are essential to classify the anemia
Iron deficiency anemia is treated with oral or IV iron supplementation after identifying and treating the source of blood loss
B12 deficiency anemia is treated with intramuscular hydroxocobalamin injections or high-dose oral supplementation
Folate deficiency is treated with oral folic acid supplementation — always exclude B12 deficiency first as folate supplementation alone in B12 deficiency can precipitate neurological deterioration
anemia.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care for hemoglobin below 7g/dL, severe breathlessness, chest pain, palpitations at rest, or any signs of hemodynamic instability. Seek prompt evaluation for unexplained anemia — it may be the first sign of malignancy, gastrointestinal bleeding, or autoimmune disease.
anemia.specialist_recommendation: Hematologist / General Practitioner / Gastroenterologist (for GI blood loss)

immediate_advice: Rest from the aggravating activity immediately — continuing through tendinitis pain significantly delays healing and risks tendon rupture
Apply ice wrapped in a cloth for 20 minutes every 2–3 hours during the first 48–72 hours
Elevate the affected limb if lower limb tendinitis is present to reduce swelling
quick_solutions: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) reduce inflammation and pain during the acute phase
Eccentric strengthening exercises (lengthening the tendon under load) are the most evidence-based rehabilitation for chronic tendinopathy
Physiotherapy assessment identifies biomechanical factors (poor training technique, footwear, posture) contributing to tendinitis
Corticosteroid injection into the tendon sheath provides rapid short-term relief but is associated with tendon weakening with repeated injections — use is limited
tendinitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care for sudden severe pain with a popping sensation during activity — this may indicate complete tendon rupture (e.g., Achilles rupture) requiring surgical repair. Consult a doctor for tendinitis not improving after 6 weeks of conservative management.
tendinitis.specialist_recommendation: Orthopedic Surgeon / Sports Medicine Doctor / Physiotherapist

immediate_advice: Rest and maintain high fluid intake — staying well hydrated thins nasal secretions and supports immune function
Use paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever, headache, and muscle aches
Use saline nasal spray or rinse to relieve nasal congestion without the rebound effect of decongestant sprays
quick_solutions: The common cold is caused by rhinovirus and other viruses — antibiotics are not effective and should not be used
Zinc lozenges started within 24 hours of symptom onset modestly reduce duration and severity
Honey (1–2 teaspoons in warm water) is as effective as OTC cough suppressants in adults and children over 1 year
Regular handwashing is the single most effective measure to prevent spread of the common cold to others
common cold.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor for symptoms lasting more than 10 days without improvement, fever above 39°C, ear pain, facial pain and pressure (suggesting sinusitis), significant throat pain (strep throat), or if symptoms worsen significantly after initial improvement.

immediate_advice: Call emergency services immediately if associated with chest pain, breathlessness, syncope, or if you feel very unwell
Perform vagal maneuvers if trained to do so: Valsalva maneuver (bear down as if having a bowel motion for 15 seconds) or carotid sinus massage (by trained clinician only)
Modified Valsalva (strain for 15 seconds, then lie flat with legs raised for 15 seconds) is the most effective vagal maneuver and can be self-administered
quick_solutions: IV adenosine administered in an emergency setting terminates most PSVT within seconds — it is the pharmacological treatment of choice
Beta-blockers (metoprolol) or calcium channel blockers (verapamil, diltiazem) are used for rate control if adenosine fails
Catheter ablation of the accessory pathway or AV node re-entry circuit is curative in over 95% of cases and is recommended for recurrent symptomatic PSVT
Record episodes with a smartwatch or heart rate monitor — rhythm documentation during an attack guides ablation planning
paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for palpitations with chest pain, breathlessness, near-syncope, or syncope. Consult a cardiologist for recurrent episodes of palpitations with rapid heart rate, particularly if lasting more than 30 minutes or requiring emergency attendance.

immediate_advice: Elevate the legs above heart level whenever resting or sleeping to reduce venous pooling and swelling
Wear prescribed compression stockings from the moment you get up in the morning to the time you go to bed
Avoid prolonged standing or sitting — take regular walking breaks to activate the calf muscle pump
quick_solutions: Medical-grade compression stockings (class 2 or 3, 23–40 mmHg) are the cornerstone of conservative management
Regular walking and calf muscle strengthening exercises improve venous return significantly
Skin care with emollients prevents venous eczema and lipodermatosclerosis — apply daily to dry, thickened skin
Endovenous thermal ablation (laser or radiofrequency) or sclerotherapy of incompetent superficial veins reduces symptoms and prevents progression to venous ulceration
venous insufficiency.when_to_seek_help: Consult a vascular surgeon for venous eczema (itchy, weeping skin around the ankles), lipodermatosclerosis (hardened brown skin), venous ulceration (open wound on the lower leg), rapidly worsening edema, or varicose veins causing significant symptoms.

immediate_advice: Avoid sexual intercourse until both you and your partner(s) have completed treatment and are symptom-free
Do not douche or use vaginal deodorants which worsen vaginal irritation and disrupt natural flora
Inform recent sexual partners so they can be tested and treated simultaneously
quick_solutions: Metronidazole (2g single dose or 400mg twice daily for 7 days) is the highly effective first-line treatment for both partners
Both partners must be treated simultaneously even if asymptomatic — reinfection is the most common cause of treatment failure
Avoid alcohol during metronidazole treatment and for 48 hours after — the combination causes severe nausea and vomiting
Screen for co-existing STIs: chlamydia, gonorrhea, HIV, and syphilis should all be offered at the time of diagnosis
trichomonas infection.when_to_seek_help: Consult a sexual health clinic or doctor for any unusual vaginal or urethral discharge, genital itching, or burning on urination — particularly after unprotected sexual contact. Trichomonas in pregnancy requires prompt treatment due to associated risks of preterm birth.
trichomonas infection.specialist_recommendation: Sexual Health Specialist / Gynecologist / General Practitioner

immediate_advice: Wash the face twice daily with a gentle, non-comedogenic cleanser — avoid scrubbing which worsens inflammation
Do not squeeze, pick, or pop pimples as this worsens inflammation, increases scarring risk, and spreads bacteria
Use only non-comedogenic (oil-free) moisturizers and cosmetics labeled as suitable for acne-prone skin
quick_solutions: Topical retinoids (tretinoin, adapalene) are the cornerstone of acne treatment — apply nightly to the entire affected area, not just individual spots
Topical benzoyl peroxide reduces Cutibacterium acnes bacterial load and is available without prescription
Oral antibiotics (doxycycline, lymecycline) are used for moderate inflammatory acne but should be combined with topical retinoids and limited to 3–6 months
Oral isotretinoin (Roaccutane) is highly effective for severe or scarring acne and requires specialist monitoring due to side effects including teratogenicity
acne.when_to_seek_help: Consult a dermatologist for moderate-to-severe acne not responding to 12 weeks of OTC treatment, acne causing significant psychological distress, any nodular or cystic acne, or acne resulting in scarring.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency psychiatric care immediately for any active suicidal ideation, plan, or intent — do not leave the person alone
Maintain basic self-care routines: sleep, meals, and gentle physical activity even when motivation is severely reduced
Reach out to a trusted person — isolation significantly worsens depressive episodes
quick_solutions: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is as effective as medication for mild-to-moderate depression and produces more durable long-term outcomes
SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine, escitalopram) are first-line pharmacological treatment — full effect takes 4–6 weeks and medication should be continued for at least 6 months after remission
Regular aerobic exercise (30 minutes, 5 days per week) has robust evidence for antidepressant effect comparable to medication in mild-to-moderate depression
Combined therapy (antidepressant plus psychotherapy) produces the best outcomes for moderate-to-severe depression
depression.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for suicidal ideation with intent or plan. Consult a doctor or mental health professional for persistent low mood most days for more than 2 weeks, loss of interest in all activities, inability to function, or significant weight change.

immediate_advice: For overdose from any substance: call emergency services immediately, place the person in the recovery position if unconscious but breathing, and administer naloxone if opioid overdose is suspected
Do not attempt unsupervised withdrawal from alcohol, benzodiazepines, or opioids — seizures and death can result
Remove access to substances from the immediate environment if safe to do so
quick_solutions: Medically supervised detoxification is the safe first step for physical dependence on any substance
Medication-assisted treatment (buprenorphine for opioids, naltrexone for alcohol or opioids) significantly reduces relapse rates and mortality
Behavioral therapies (Motivational Interviewing, CBT, Contingency Management) are evidence-based treatments for all substance use disorders
Long-term recovery support through peer groups, sober living, and ongoing counseling prevents relapse
drug abuse.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for overdose, seizures, severe withdrawal, or loss of consciousness. Seek urgent addiction medicine assessment for anyone wishing to stop substance use safely or whose use is causing significant harm.

immediate_advice: Seek urgent urological evaluation — urinary tract obstruction can cause rapid and irreversible kidney damage if not relieved
Do not restrict fluid intake — maintain hydration unless advised otherwise by a doctor
Seek emergency care immediately for complete inability to urinate, severe flank pain with fever, or rapidly worsening kidney function
quick_solutions: Urgent catheterization (urethral or suprapubic) relieves bladder outlet obstruction immediately
Ureteral stenting or percutaneous nephrostomy relieves upper urinary tract obstruction from kidney stones, tumors, or strictures
Identify and definitively treat the underlying cause: BPH, kidney stone, ureteral stricture, pelvic malignancy, or retroperitoneal fibrosis
Monitor kidney function (creatinine, eGFR) closely during and after relief of obstruction — post-obstructive diuresis requires careful fluid management
urinary tract obstruction.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for complete urinary retention, severe flank pain with fever and rigors (infected obstructed kidney), bilateral obstruction causing acute kidney injury, or obstruction in a solitary functioning kidney.

immediate_advice: Seek urgent ophthalmological evaluation — iridocyclitis (anterior uveitis) can cause permanent vision loss if not treated promptly
Wear dark glasses to reduce photophobia while awaiting assessment
Do not use over-the-counter eye drops without specialist guidance as these may worsen inflammation or mask a serious diagnosis
quick_solutions: Topical corticosteroid eye drops (prednisolone acetate) are the cornerstone of treatment and must be started promptly
Topical cycloplegic agents (cyclopentolate, atropine) reduce pain from ciliary spasm and prevent posterior synechiae (adhesions) formation
Investigate for underlying systemic causes: HLA-B27 related conditions (ankylosing spondylitis, psoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis, IBD), sarcoidosis, Behcet's disease
Recurrent or chronic uveitis requires immunosuppressive therapy with methotrexate, mycophenolate, or biologics under rheumatology guidance
iridocyclitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent ophthalmological care for any red, painful eye with photophobia and blurred vision — iridocyclitis is a time-sensitive condition where delayed treatment causes cataract, glaucoma, and permanent vision loss.

immediate_advice: Wear supportive underwear (snug-fitting briefs or athletic supporter) to reduce scrotal discomfort from venous engorgement
Avoid prolonged standing and heavy lifting which worsen venous pooling and discomfort
Apply ice wrapped in a cloth to the scrotum for 10–15 minutes for acute pain relief
quick_solutions: Most varicoceles are asymptomatic and require only monitoring — not all varicoceles need treatment
Varicocele repair (microsurgical varicocelectomy or percutaneous embolization) is recommended for varicoceles causing pain, testicular atrophy, or infertility
Semen analysis is performed to assess the impact of varicocele on sperm parameters — varicocele is the most common correctable cause of male infertility
NSAIDs provide symptomatic pain relief for mildly symptomatic varicoceles not yet requiring surgical intervention
varicocele of the testicles.when_to_seek_help: Consult a urologist for persistent scrotal discomfort, a 'bag of worms' sensation in the scrotum, testicular asymmetry suggesting atrophy, or if fertility is a concern. Seek urgent evaluation for any new scrotal mass to exclude testicular torsion or tumor.

immediate_advice: Identify and avoid personal dietary triggers — common triggers include wheat, dairy, high-FODMAP foods, caffeine, alcohol, and large fatty meals
Do not skip meals or eat irregularly — consistent meal timing regulates bowel function
Manage stress proactively — the gut-brain axis is central to IBS and psychological stress directly worsens symptoms
quick_solutions: The low-FODMAP diet (supervised by a dietitian) reduces symptoms in 70% of IBS patients — involves a structured elimination and reintroduction phase
Soluble fiber supplementation (psyllium) improves both constipation and diarrhea-predominant IBS
Antispasmodics (mebeverine, hyoscine butylbromide) provide effective relief of abdominal cramping
Gut-directed hypnotherapy and Cognitive Behavioral Therapy have strong evidence for long-term IBS symptom reduction
irritable bowel syndrome.when_to_seek_help: Consult a gastroenterologist for rectal bleeding, unintentional weight loss, nocturnal symptoms waking from sleep, family history of bowel cancer or IBD, or if symptoms begin after age 50 — these are alarm features that require colonoscopy to exclude serious pathology.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for sudden severe pelvic pain with vomiting — this may indicate ovarian torsion or ruptured cyst with hemorrhage, both requiring emergency surgery
Avoid strenuous exercise and heavy lifting if a large cyst has been identified as these activities may precipitate torsion
Take NSAIDs (ibuprofen) for pelvic pain while awaiting medical evaluation
quick_solutions: Most simple ovarian cysts in pre-menopausal women are functional (follicular or corpus luteum cysts) and resolve spontaneously within 1–3 menstrual cycles
Pelvic ultrasound follow-up at 6–12 weeks confirms resolution of simple cysts
The combined oral contraceptive pill prevents new functional cyst formation but does not accelerate resolution of existing cysts
Surgical removal (laparoscopic cystectomy) is recommended for complex cysts, persistent cysts larger than 5–7cm, post-menopausal cysts, or those with features suspicious for malignancy
ovarian cyst.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for sudden severe one-sided pelvic pain, vomiting with severe pain, or faintness (ovarian torsion or ruptured hemorrhagic cyst). Consult a gynecologist for any complex cyst, cyst persisting beyond 3 months, or any ovarian cyst in a post-menopausal woman.
ovarian cyst.specialist_recommendation: Gynecologist / Gynecologic Oncologist (for complex or suspicious cysts)

immediate_advice: Most lipomas are benign and require no urgent action — they do not become cancerous
Monitor for any rapid increase in size, hardening, irregular shape, or pain — these features warrant prompt evaluation
Avoid repeatedly pressing or squeezing a lipoma as this causes discomfort without affecting the underlying lesion
quick_solutions: Most lipomas require no treatment — observation is appropriate for small, asymptomatic, and stable lesions
Surgical excision under local anesthetic is the definitive treatment for lipomas causing pain, cosmetic concern, or functional limitation
Liposuction is an alternative for soft lipomas in cosmetically sensitive areas
Any rapidly growing lipoma or one with unusual features (firm, deep, vascular) requires biopsy to exclude liposarcoma
lipoma.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for any lump growing rapidly, becoming firm or irregular, causing pain, or measuring more than 5cm — these features may indicate a liposarcoma which requires urgent histological assessment.

immediate_advice: Avoid strenuous exercise and high-altitude environments — both significantly increase pulmonary artery pressure and can precipitate right heart failure
Take all prescribed pulmonary hypertension medications without omission — abrupt cessation of prostacyclin therapy is immediately life-threatening
Seek emergency care for worsening breathlessness at rest, syncope, or ankle swelling increasing rapidly
quick_solutions: Specialist pulmonary hypertension medications (phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors, endothelin receptor antagonists, prostacyclin analogues) slow disease progression and improve functional capacity
Supplemental oxygen improves symptoms and reduces pulmonary vasoconstriction in hypoxic patients
Anticoagulation with warfarin is indicated in idiopathic and heritable pulmonary arterial hypertension
Lung transplantation or heart-lung transplantation is considered for eligible patients with advanced disease not responding to medical therapy
pulmonary hypertension.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for acute severe breathlessness, syncope, haemoptysis, rapidly worsening peripheral edema, or chest pain — these may indicate right heart decompensation or pulmonary embolism superimposed on pulmonary hypertension.

immediate_advice: Do not panic — the vast majority (over 90%) of thyroid nodules are benign
Avoid iodine-containing supplements and contrast media before thyroid evaluation as these can affect subsequent radionuclide imaging
Seek timely medical evaluation — avoid unnecessary delay in assessment but equally avoid excessive alarm
quick_solutions: Thyroid ultrasound is the primary imaging modality to characterize the nodule and guide management decisions
Thyroid function tests (TSH, free T4) and thyroid antibodies are performed alongside ultrasound
Fine needle aspiration cytology (FNAC) is recommended for nodules with suspicious ultrasound features or those larger than 1–2cm
Radionuclide thyroid scan is used when TSH is suppressed to determine if the nodule is hyperfunctioning (hot — almost never malignant)
thyroid nodule.when_to_seek_help: Consult an endocrinologist or ENT specialist for any thyroid nodule discovered on imaging or examination. Seek urgent evaluation for rapidly growing thyroid swelling, hoarseness of voice, difficulty swallowing, or breathing difficulty caused by the nodule.

immediate_advice: Identify and treat any obvious local infection (dental abscess, skin infection, throat infection) that may be causing the lymph node enlargement
Apply warm compresses to the enlarged lymph node area to reduce discomfort
Avoid squeezing or massaging the lymph node as this can spread infection
quick_solutions: Bacterial lymphadenitis requires antibiotics targeted at the most likely causative organism — Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus pyogenes are most common
Fluctuant lymph nodes (abscess formation) require surgical drainage in addition to antibiotics
If the lymph node does not begin to improve within 48–72 hours of antibiotics, reassessment and imaging (ultrasound) are required
Persistent lymphadenopathy (beyond 4–6 weeks) requires investigation to exclude lymphoma, tuberculosis, or other systemic causes
lymphadenitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent evaluation for rapidly enlarging lymph node, hard fixed lymph node, lymphadenopathy associated with fever, night sweats, and weight loss (B-symptoms of lymphoma), or lymph node enlargement in multiple body regions simultaneously.
lymphadenitis.specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Infectious Disease Specialist / Hematologist (for suspected lymphoma)

immediate_advice: Apply direct pressure with a clean cloth to control facial bleeding — facial wounds bleed profusely due to rich blood supply
Seek emergency care for significant facial trauma, suspected facial fractures, eye injury, or any loss of consciousness
Do not blow the nose if facial fractures are suspected — this can force air into the orbit or cranial cavity
quick_solutions: Seek emergency evaluation for facial trauma with significant swelling, step deformity, numbness, double vision, or dental occlusion changes suggesting facial fractures
Facial lacerations should be cleaned thoroughly and closed within 6–8 hours for optimal cosmetic outcome — facial wounds have lower infection risk than extremity wounds
Ice applied promptly reduces bruising and swelling after facial soft tissue injury
Dental injuries require prompt dental assessment — avulsed teeth reimplanted within 30 minutes have the best prognosis
injury to the face.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for significant facial trauma, suspected orbital fracture (double vision, sunken eye), mandibular fracture (bite misalignment), nasal fracture with septal hematoma, or any loss of consciousness after facial impact.
injury to the face.specialist_recommendation: Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeon / Plastic Surgeon / Emergency Physician

immediate_advice: Take all prescribed cardiac medications consistently — do not stop without cardiology guidance
Avoid strenuous exertion if experiencing exertional chest pain, breathlessness, or syncope
Seek emergency care immediately for sudden severe breathlessness, syncope, or chest pain
quick_solutions: Regular echocardiographic surveillance (annually for severe, every 3–5 years for mild disease) monitors disease progression and determines optimal timing for intervention

Surgical aortic valve replacement (SAVR) or transcatheter aortic valve implantation (TAVI) is recommended for severe symptomatic aortic stenosis — symptoms (syncope, angina, heart failure) dramatically worsen prognosis without intervention
Diuretics and ACE inhibitors are used for symptomatic aortic regurgitation while monitoring for timing of surgical intervention
Anticoagulation with warfarin is required lifelong after mechanical valve replacement — maintain INR in the target range
aortic valve disease.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for sudden severe breathlessness, syncope, or chest pain in a patient with known aortic valve disease — these are cardinal symptoms of severe aortic stenosis indicating urgent cardiology intervention.

immediate_advice: Do not stop prescribed disease-modifying drugs (DMARDs) or biologics during a flare without rheumatology guidance — consistent treatment prevents irreversible joint damage
Apply ice to acutely inflamed joints and heat to stiff joints in the morning
Rest acutely inflamed joints but maintain gentle range-of-motion exercises to prevent stiffness and muscle wasting
quick_solutions: Methotrexate is the anchor DMARD for rheumatoid arthritis and must be accompanied by folic acid supplementation to reduce toxicity
Biologic therapies (TNF inhibitors, IL-6 inhibitors, JAK inhibitors) are highly effective for disease not responding adequately to methotrexate
NSAIDs and short courses of oral corticosteroids bridge flares while awaiting DMARD effect
Physiotherapy and occupational therapy preserve joint function and independence — refer early and maintain throughout the disease course
rheumatoid arthritis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a rheumatologist urgently for any new joint swelling and stiffness lasting more than 6 weeks — early aggressive treatment prevents irreversible joint destruction. Seek urgent care for fever with joint swelling (septic arthritis must be excluded), or rapidly worsening neurological symptoms (cervical myelopathy from atlantoaxial instability).

immediate_advice: Keep the affected areas clean — gently wash with warm soapy water and remove crusts softened with a warm compress
Cover lesions with a non-adherent dressing to prevent spread by direct contact
Wash hands thoroughly after touching lesions and do not share towels, bedding, or clothing with household members
quick_solutions: Topical antibiotics (mupirocin or fusidic acid) applied 3 times daily for 5–7 days are first-line for localized impetigo
Oral antibiotics (flucloxacillin or cefalexin) are required for widespread impetigo, bullous impetigo, or cases not responding to topical treatment
Children should be excluded from school until lesions are crusted and dry or 48 hours after starting antibiotic treatment
Nasal decolonization with mupirocin nasal ointment reduces recurrence in carriers of Staphylococcus aureus
impetigo.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor for widespread or rapidly spreading impetigo, bullous (blistering) impetigo, fever with skin infection, lesions near the eye, or if not responding to 48 hours of topical antibiotics. Seek urgent care for rapidly spreading redness and warmth suggesting cellulitis.

immediate_advice: Remain calm and avoid power struggles — escalating emotional responses worsen oppositional behavior significantly
Use consistent, predictable consequences for behavior and follow through every time without exception
Identify and acknowledge positive behaviors explicitly — positive reinforcement is more effective than punishment for ODD
quick_solutions: Parent Management Training (PMT) is the most evidence-based treatment for ODD in children and significantly reduces oppositional behaviors
Collaborative Problem Solving (CPS) approach teaches the child to identify triggers and develop problem-solving skills
Individual Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for the child addresses underlying emotional dysregulation and cognitive distortions
Screen for and treat co-occurring ADHD, anxiety, or learning difficulties which frequently underlie ODD
oppositional disorder.when_to_seek_help: Consult a child psychiatrist or psychologist for assessment if oppositional behaviors are severe, persistent across multiple settings, significantly impairing family life and schooling, or if there are safety concerns including aggression toward others.
oppositional disorder.specialist_recommendation: Child Psychiatrist / Clinical Psychologist / Family Therapist

immediate_advice: Take acute treatment (triptan or NSAIDs) at the very first sign of migraine — early treatment is significantly more effective than waiting until pain is severe
Rest in a quiet, dark room and apply a cold compress to the forehead or back of the neck
Stay hydrated — nausea and vomiting during migraines cause significant dehydration which prolongs the attack
quick_solutions: Triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan, zolmitriptan) are the most effective acute treatment for established migraine — take as early as possible in the attack
Anti-emetics (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine) improve nausea and also improve oral medication absorption
Preventive therapy (topiramate, amitriptyline, beta-blockers, CGRP monoclonal antibodies) is recommended for 4 or more migraine days per month
Keep a migraine diary to identify personal triggers: hormonal changes, stress, sleep disruption, dietary triggers, and weather changes are most common
migraine.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for the worst headache of your life (thunderclap headache), headache with fever and neck stiffness, headache after head injury, new neurological symptoms with headache, or migraine lasting more than 72 hours (status migrainosus).

immediate_advice: Inspect feet daily for cuts, blisters, calluses, redness, or swelling — sensation is reduced so injuries may go unnoticed until severe
Never walk barefoot — always wear well-fitting shoes with soft insoles, even indoors
Maintain strict blood glucose control — HbA1c below 7% is the most important measure to prevent progression of neuropathy
quick_solutions: Optimize glycemic control — this is the only intervention that addresses the underlying cause and slows progression
Neuropathic pain medications: duloxetine, pregabalin, or amitriptyline are first-line for painful diabetic neuropathy
Capsaicin cream (0.075%) applied topically provides localized pain relief for peripheral neuropathic pain
Annual podiatry review and custom orthotic footwear significantly reduce the risk of diabetic foot ulceration and amputation
diabetic peripheral neuropathy.when_to_seek_help: Consult a diabetologist or neurologist for numbness, tingling, or burning pain in the feet and legs. Seek emergency care for any foot ulcer, wound, or infection in a diabetic — diabetic foot infections are limb-threatening and can progress to osteomyelitis within days.

immediate_advice: Exclude pregnancy with a home pregnancy test as the first step for any missed or irregular period
Track menstrual cycles with a period-tracking app to document pattern, duration, and associated symptoms for your doctor
Review recent changes in weight, exercise intensity, stress levels, and medications as these are common reversible causes
quick_solutions: Hormonal blood tests (FSH, LH, prolactin, TSH, testosterone, AMH) guide diagnosis and establish the hormonal basis of cycle irregularity
The combined oral contraceptive pill regulates cycle timing effectively for women not currently seeking pregnancy
Treat reversible causes: restore healthy body weight, moderate excessive exercise, address stress, and review medications
Letrozole or clomiphene is used to induce ovulation for women with irregular cycles seeking to conceive
idiopathic irregular menstrual cycle.when_to_seek_help: Consult a gynecologist or endocrinologist for cycles consistently shorter than 21 days or longer than 35 days, amenorrhea lasting more than 3 months, irregular cycles with other symptoms (excessive hair growth, acne, weight gain), or difficulty conceiving.

immediate_advice: Stop smoking immediately — tobacco smoke dramatically accelerates progression of virtually all interstitial lung diseases
Avoid known environmental exposures relevant to your specific ILD: bird proteins, mold, silica, asbestos, or causative medications
Seek emergency care for acute severe breathlessness, rapid oxygen desaturation, or any acute exacerbation
quick_solutions: Antifibrotic medications (pirfenidone or nintedanib) slow progression in idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis — initiate early when diagnosis is confirmed
Corticosteroids and immunosuppressants are used for inflammatory ILDs (hypersensitivity pneumonitis, connective tissue disease-associated ILD)
Supplemental oxygen therapy improves exercise tolerance and quality of life when resting or exertional hypoxia is present
Pulmonary rehabilitation improves functional capacity and reduces dyspnea even in progressive ILD
interstitial lung disease.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for acute severe worsening of breathlessness over days (acute exacerbation of ILD which carries high mortality). Consult a pulmonologist promptly for any progressive breathlessness with dry cough, particularly in a patient with a known connective tissue disease or occupational exposure.

immediate_advice: Rest the affected limb and avoid positions or activities that compress the affected nerve
Seek urgent neurological evaluation for any sudden onset focal weakness, numbness, or loss of function in a limb
Do not apply ice or heat directly over a peripheral nerve as this can cause additional thermal injury to already damaged nerve tissue
quick_solutions: Identify the underlying cause urgently: diabetes (most common), vasculitis, compression, Lyme disease, or sarcoidosis
Nerve conduction studies and electromyography (EMG) localize the lesion and assess severity of axonal damage
Treat the underlying cause aggressively — diabetic mononeuritis typically improves spontaneously over months with optimized glucose control
Neuropathic pain medications (gabapentin, pregabalin, amitriptyline) reduce pain and allodynia during the recovery phase
mononeuritis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent neurological evaluation for sudden onset focal weakness or complete loss of function in a nerve distribution, particularly if bilateral (mononeuritis multiplex) or associated with systemic symptoms — this may indicate vasculitis requiring urgent immunosuppression.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for severe hypovolemia — significant fluid or blood loss causing dizziness, confusion, or collapse requires immediate IV fluid resuscitation
Lie flat and elevate the legs if feeling faint to improve cerebral perfusion while awaiting help
Control any external bleeding with direct firm pressure immediately
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — IV crystalloid fluid resuscitation (normal saline or Hartmann's solution) is the cornerstone of initial management
Blood transfusion is required for hemorrhagic hypovolemia with significant hemoglobin drop or ongoing hemorrhage
Identify and control the source of fluid or blood loss: GI hemorrhage, trauma, surgical bleeding, severe vomiting or diarrhea, burns
Vasopressors (norepinephrine) may be required if fluid resuscitation alone is insufficient to restore hemodynamic stability
hypovolemia.when_to_seek_help: Call emergency services immediately for significant blood loss, loss of consciousness, confusion, severe dizziness, rapid weak pulse, cold clammy skin, or any signs of circulatory shock — hypovolemic shock has high mortality without rapid intervention.
hypovolemia.specialist_recommendation: Emergency Physician / Critical Care Specialist / Trauma Surgeon

immediate_advice: Seek emergency psychiatric care immediately for acute psychosis, severe agitation, risk of harm to self or others, or if the person is unable to care for themselves
Do not abruptly stop antipsychotic medications — abrupt cessation causes rapid relapse and can be dangerous
Ensure the environment is calm, safe, and non-threatening during acute psychotic episodes
quick_solutions: Antipsychotic medications (risperidone, olanzapine, aripiprazole, clozapine for treatment-resistant schizophrenia) are the cornerstone of treatment
Long-acting injectable antipsychotics (LAIs) significantly improve medication adherence and reduce relapse rates
Psychosocial interventions: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for psychosis (CBTp), family psychoeducation, and supported employment are evidence-based adjuncts
Early intervention in psychosis programs (within 2 years of onset) dramatically improve long-term functional outcomes
schizophrenia.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency psychiatric care for acute psychosis, command hallucinations, disorganized behavior causing safety risk, refusal of all food and fluid, or any direct risk of harm. Seek urgent outpatient review for relapse warning signs: sleep disturbance, social withdrawal, or re-emerging positive symptoms.

immediate_advice: Call emergency services immediately — sepsis is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate hospital care
Do not delay seeking help while waiting to see if symptoms improve — the Sepsis Six must be implemented within one hour of recognition
Note any recent infections, procedures, or immunosuppressive medications — this information is critical for the treating team
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — the Sepsis Six bundle (high-flow oxygen, blood cultures, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, IV fluids, lactate measurement, urine output monitoring) must be initiated within one hour
Source control (draining an abscess, removing an infected catheter, or surgical intervention) is critical to eliminate the infectious focus
Vasopressors (norepinephrine) are required for septic shock unresponsive to fluid resuscitation — this requires ICU management
Antibiotic de-escalation guided by culture results reduces antimicrobial resistance and drug side effects
sepsis.when_to_seek_help: Call emergency services immediately for any patient with suspected infection who has confusion, very fast breathing, low blood pressure, fever or hypothermia, or mottled and clammy skin — these are signs of sepsis which kills within hours without treatment.
sepsis.specialist_recommendation: Critical Care Specialist / Infectious Disease Specialist (Emergency services first)

immediate_advice: Seek emergency medical evaluation — acute cholecystitis requires hospital admission for IV antibiotics, analgesia, and surgical planning
Do not eat or drink anything pending surgical assessment
Avoid NSAIDs if liver or kidney impairment is present — use paracetamol for pain relief while awaiting care
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — IV antibiotics (co-amoxiclav or piperacillin-tazobactam), IV fluids, and analgesia are the initial management
Laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 72 hours of admission is the recommended definitive treatment for acute cholecystitis
Percutaneous cholecystostomy is used for high surgical risk patients as a temporizing measure
Maintain a low-fat diet after the acute episode to minimize biliary colic until surgical removal
cholecystitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for severe right upper quadrant pain with fever and vomiting, jaundice with fever and rigors (cholangitis), or peritoneal signs suggesting perforation — these are all surgical emergencies.

immediate_advice: Call emergency services immediately and begin CPR: 30 chest compressions followed by 2 rescue breaths at a rate of 100–120 compressions per minute
Use an automated external defibrillator (AED) as soon as one is available — turn it on and follow voice instructions
Do not stop CPR until emergency services arrive and take over or the person shows clear signs of life
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — survival from cardiac arrest is entirely dependent on the speed of CPR initiation and defibrillation
Hands-only CPR (without rescue breaths) is recommended for untrained bystanders and is highly effective in the first few minutes
Post-resuscitation care in ICU includes targeted temperature management, coronary angiography for suspected cardiac cause, and neurological monitoring
Implantable cardioverter-defibrillator (ICD) is implanted after survived cardiac arrest to prevent recurrence
cardiac arrest.when_to_seek_help: Call emergency services immediately for any unresponsive person who is not breathing normally — start CPR immediately and do not delay for any reason. Every minute without CPR reduces survival by 10%.
cardiac arrest.specialist_recommendation: Emergency Physician / Cardiologist / Critical Care (Emergency services first)

immediate_advice: Take all prescribed heart failure medications (ACE inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics, aldosterone antagonists, SGLT2 inhibitors) consistently without omission
Monitor daily weight — report a gain of more than 2kg in 48 hours to your cardiologist as this indicates fluid accumulation
Avoid alcohol entirely — alcohol is directly cardiotoxic and a common cause of dilated cardiomyopathy
quick_solutions: Optimize heart failure medical therapy with an experienced cardiologist — guideline-directed medical therapy significantly reduces mortality and hospitalizations
Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) is indicated for eligible patients with dyssynchrony and reduced ejection fraction
ICD implantation is recommended for primary prevention of sudden cardiac death in patients with EF below 35%
Heart transplantation is considered for refractory end-stage cardiomyopathy — LVAD (left ventricular assist device) is a bridge or destination therapy option
cardiomyopathy.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for sudden severe breathlessness, acute pulmonary edema, syncope, chest pain, or palpitations with hemodynamic compromise. Contact your cardiologist for worsening breathlessness on minimal exertion, rapid weight gain, or increased ankle swelling.
cardiomyopathy.specialist_recommendation: Cardiologist / Heart Failure Specialist / Cardiothoracic Surgeon

immediate_advice: Avoid complete avoidance of feared social situations — avoidance provides short-term relief but maintains and worsens the phobia long-term
Practice slow diaphragmatic breathing before and during anxiety-provoking social situations
Challenge unhelpful thoughts by asking: 'What is the actual evidence that the worst outcome will happen?'
quick_solutions: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy with exposure component (gradually facing feared social situations) is the most effective treatment for social anxiety disorder
SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine, escitalopram) are first-line pharmacological treatment and are effective when combined with CBT
Social skills training and assertiveness training help build the competence and confidence that reduces social anxiety
Mindfulness-based approaches reduce the hypervigilance to social cues and reduce rumination after social interactions
social phobia.when_to_seek_help: Consult a mental health professional for social anxiety significantly affecting employment, relationships, or daily activities. Seek urgent care if social phobia is accompanied by depression with suicidal ideation or if alcohol is being used to manage social situations.

immediate_advice: Call emergency services immediately — bacterial meningitis is a life-threatening emergency where every hour of delay significantly worsens outcomes
If meningococcal disease is suspected and IV antibiotics are not available within 30 minutes, give intramuscular benzylpenicillin before transfer if not allergic
Do not wait for a lumbar puncture result before starting antibiotics in a patient who is deteriorating
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — IV ceftriaxone (third-generation cephalosporin) and dexamethasone must be started within the first hour of suspected bacterial meningitis
A non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash with fever is meningococcal septicemia until proven otherwise — this is an absolute emergency
Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis confirms the diagnosis and guides antibiotic selection after CT brain if indicated
Close contacts of bacterial meningitis cases require prophylactic rifampicin or ciprofloxacin to prevent secondary cases
meningitis.when_to_seek_help: Call emergency services immediately for the classic triad of severe headache, neck stiffness, and fever, or for any combination of photophobia, non-blanching rash, vomiting with severe headache, or altered consciousness — bacterial meningitis kills within hours without treatment.
meningitis.specialist_recommendation: Infectious Disease Specialist / Neurologist (Emergency services first)

immediate_advice: Do not insert cotton buds, fingers, or any object into the ear canal — this compacts wax further and can damage the eardrum
Apply 2–3 drops of olive oil, almond oil, or commercial ear drops twice daily for 3–5 days to soften wax before removal
Avoid ear candling — it is ineffective and causes burns to the ear canal and eardrum
quick_solutions: Softening with olive oil or sodium bicarbonate ear drops for 3–5 days prior to removal significantly improves success and comfort
Ear irrigation (syringing with warm water) by a nurse or doctor is effective for removing softened wax — contraindicated if eardrum perforation is present
Microsuction performed by an ENT nurse or audiologist is the safest and most effective removal method, particularly for impacted or hard wax
Wax softening drops used monthly can prevent recurrence in wax-prone individuals
ear wax impaction.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor or nurse for wax impaction causing significant hearing loss, tinnitus, dizziness, or ear pain. Seek ENT referral for failed irrigation, known eardrum perforation, or if ear surgery has previously been performed.

immediate_advice: Apply broad-spectrum SPF 50+ mineral sunscreen (zinc oxide or titanium dioxide) daily — sun exposure is the most common rosacea trigger
Avoid known triggers: alcohol, spicy foods, hot drinks, extreme temperatures, vigorous exercise, and emotional stress
Use gentle, fragrance-free skincare products and avoid exfoliants, astringents, and toners which worsen rosacea
quick_solutions: Topical metronidazole gel or cream (0.75% or 1%) applied twice daily is first-line treatment for papulopustular rosacea
Topical azelaic acid (15–20%) is an effective alternative with additional anti-inflammatory and antikeratinizing properties
Oral doxycycline (40mg modified-release) is used for moderate-to-severe rosacea and significantly reduces papules and pustules
Laser therapy (pulsed dye laser, KTP laser, IPL) targets persistent facial redness and telangiectasia not responsive to topical treatment
rosacea.when_to_seek_help: Consult a dermatologist for rosacea causing significant distress, not responding to topical treatments after 8 weeks, eye involvement (ocular rosacea — causing red irritated eyes), or rhinophyma (progressive thickening of nasal skin requiring surgical treatment).

immediate_advice: Seek emergency evaluation — facial fractures from significant trauma may be associated with traumatic brain injury, cervical spine injury, and airway compromise
Maintain airway — blood, teeth, and soft tissue can obstruct breathing after significant facial trauma
Apply gentle ice packs to reduce swelling while awaiting evaluation — do not apply pressure over suspected fracture sites
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — CT facial bones is the imaging of choice for suspected facial fractures
Nasal fractures causing significant cosmetic deformity or obstruction are reduced (manipulated) under local or general anesthesia within 2 weeks before bone hardens
Orbital floor fractures with diplopia or enophthalmos require ophthalmological assessment and consideration of surgical repair
Mandibular and zygomatic fractures typically require open reduction and internal fixation by maxillofacial surgeons
fracture of the facial bones.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for any significant facial trauma, difficulty opening the jaw, malocclusion (bite feels different), double vision, significant facial asymmetry, numbness of the face, or any associated head injury or loss of consciousness.
fracture of the facial bones.specialist_recommendation: Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeon / Plastic Surgeon / Ophthalmologist

immediate_advice: Eat a soft diet during painful episodes — avoid hard, chewy foods requiring wide jaw opening
Apply warm compresses to the jaw and temporal area for 15–20 minutes to relieve muscle tension
Avoid clenching teeth — place your tongue lightly between your teeth as a reminder to keep jaw muscles relaxed
quick_solutions: NSAIDs reduce pain and inflammation during acute jaw disorder flares
A custom occlusal splint (nightguard) fabricated by a dentist reduces bruxism-related jaw loading during sleep
Physiotherapy with jaw mobilization exercises, massage, and postural correction is highly effective for myofascial jaw pain
Botulinum toxin injection into the masseter and temporalis muscles significantly reduces pain from bruxism and jaw clenching
jaw disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent dental or maxillofacial evaluation for jaw locking (inability to open or close), significant clicking with pain and restricted opening, rapidly worsening jaw pain, or jaw pain with ear pain and hearing changes.
jaw disorder.specialist_recommendation: Oral and Maxillofacial Surgeon / Dentist / Physiotherapist

immediate_advice: Reach out to a trusted person — do not isolate yourself after a traumatic event
Avoid using alcohol or drugs to cope — these increase risk of developing PTSD and worsen long-term outcomes
Seek emergency psychiatric care for any suicidal ideation or self-harm urges following trauma
quick_solutions: Trauma-focused Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (TF-CBT) and EMDR (Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing) are the most evidence-based treatments and produce significant, sustained improvement
SSRIs (sertraline, paroxetine) are first-line pharmacological treatment when therapy alone is insufficient or unavailable
Prazosin is used specifically for PTSD-related nightmares and significantly reduces their frequency and intensity
Peer support groups with others who have experienced similar traumas provide powerful additional recovery scaffolding
post-traumatic stress disorder (ptsd).when_to_seek_help: Consult a mental health professional for intrusive memories, nightmares, avoidance of trauma reminders, hypervigilance, or emotional numbing persisting for more than 4 weeks after a traumatic event. Seek emergency care for active suicidal ideation or self-harm.

immediate_advice: Apply a warm compress to closed eyelids for 10 minutes twice daily to loosen crusts and improve meibomian gland secretion
Gently clean the eyelid margins with diluted baby shampoo or commercially available eyelid wipes immediately after warm compresses
Do not rub the eyes and avoid eye makeup during active flares
quick_solutions: Eyelid hygiene (warm compresses followed by lid scrubs) performed consistently twice daily is the cornerstone of blepharitis management
Topical antibiotic ointment (chloramphenicol or fusidic acid) applied to the lid margin reduces bacterial load in anterior blepharitis
Omega-3 fatty acid supplementation improves meibomian gland function and reduces posterior blepharitis over 3 months
Blepharitis is a chronic condition requiring ongoing maintenance treatment — twice-weekly lid hygiene prevents recurrence after achieving control
blepharitis.when_to_seek_help: Consult an ophthalmologist for blepharitis not improving with 4 weeks of lid hygiene, associated corneal involvement, significant loss of eyelashes, eyelid notching, or if Demodex mite infestation is suspected.

immediate_advice: Drink at least 2–3 liters of water daily to flush bacteria from the bladder and dilute urine
Take paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain and use a urinary alkalinizer (potassium citrate sachets) to reduce burning on urination
Urinate frequently — do not hold urine for prolonged periods as this allows bacterial multiplication
quick_solutions: Uncomplicated cystitis in women is treated with a short course of trimethoprim (3 days), nitrofurantoin (3–7 days), or pivmecillinam depending on local resistance patterns
A urine culture is recommended for recurrent cystitis, male patients, pregnant women, or suspected complicated UTI
Post-coital urination, adequate hydration, and wiping front to back reduce recurrence risk
Long-term low-dose antibiotic prophylaxis, vaginal estrogen cream (post-menopausal women), or D-mannose supplementation are options for frequent recurrent cystitis
cystitis.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor for cystitis in men, children, or pregnant women (always requires treatment and culture), symptoms not improving after 48 hours of antibiotics, cystitis with fever and loin pain (pyelonephritis), blood in urine, or more than 3 episodes per year.
cystitis.specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Urologist (for recurrent or complicated cases)

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for severe pain crisis, fever, acute chest symptoms, or stroke symptoms
Maintain adequate hydration at all times — dehydration is a major precipitant of sickling and pain crises
Stay warm and avoid cold exposure, extreme exertion, and high-altitude environments which trigger sickling
quick_solutions: Hydroxyurea therapy significantly reduces pain crises, acute chest syndrome, hospitalizations, and mortality — discuss initiation with your hematologist
Daily penicillin prophylaxis (for children) and all recommended vaccinations are mandatory as functional asplenia increases infection risk dramatically
Blood transfusion (simple or exchange) is used for severe anemia, stroke prevention, and acute chest syndrome
Bone marrow or stem cell transplantation from a matched sibling donor is the only curative treatment currently available
sickle cell anemia.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for fever above 38.5°C, severe pain crisis not responding to home analgesia, chest pain or breathlessness (acute chest syndrome), neurological symptoms (stroke), severe abdominal pain (splenic sequestration), or priapism lasting more than 2 hours.

immediate_advice: Reassure yourself — a subconjunctival hemorrhage looks alarming but is almost always harmless and resolves spontaneously
Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs if not medically necessary as these can slow resolution
Use lubricating eye drops if the eye feels irritated or gritty — the hemorrhage itself does not cause pain or affect vision
quick_solutions: No specific treatment is required — the blood is reabsorbed by the body within 1–3 weeks without any intervention
Artificial tears provide comfort if the eye surface feels dry or irritated
If taking anticoagulants (warfarin, apixaban) or antiplatelet agents, inform your doctor — subconjunctival hemorrhage may prompt a review of your anticoagulation level
Identify and manage precipitating causes: hypertension, coughing, straining, vomiting, or trauma
subconjunctival hemorrhage.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor if subconjunctival hemorrhage is associated with eye pain, blurred vision, significant trauma, recurrent episodes, or if you are on anticoagulants. Seek urgent evaluation if the hemorrhage is associated with significant facial or eye trauma to exclude open globe injury.
subconjunctival hemorrhage.specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Ophthalmologist (for trauma-related or recurrent cases)

immediate_advice: Increase dietary fiber immediately (25–35g daily from vegetables, fruits, whole grains, and legumes) to soften stools and reduce straining
Take warm sitz baths (sitting in shallow warm water) for 10–15 minutes after bowel movements to relieve pain and inflammation
Use over-the-counter topical preparations containing hydrocortisone and local anesthetic for short-term symptom relief (maximum 7 days)
quick_solutions: Ensure adequate hydration (2+ liters daily) and use fiber supplements (psyllium) if dietary fiber targets cannot be met through food alone
Avoid prolonged sitting on the toilet and straining — respond promptly to the urge to defecate and limit toilet time to 5 minutes
Office procedures (rubber band ligation, sclerotherapy, infrared coagulation) are highly effective for grade 2–3 internal hemorrhoids
Hemorrhoidectomy is the definitive surgical treatment for large symptomatic hemorrhoids not responding to office procedures
hemorrhoids.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor for rectal bleeding to confirm the diagnosis and exclude colorectal cancer (particularly in patients over 40), for hemorrhoids causing significant pain or prolapse, or for any grade 4 hemorrhoids (cannot be reduced). Seek urgent care for acutely thrombosed external hemorrhoids causing severe pain.

immediate_advice: Remove the causative substance immediately — wash the affected skin thoroughly with soap and water for at least 10 minutes
Apply cool wet compresses to relieve acute weeping and pruritus
Avoid all potential irritants and allergens in the affected area while healing
quick_solutions: Topical corticosteroids (moderate-potency for body, low-potency for face) applied twice daily are the primary treatment and significantly reduce inflammation
Oral antihistamines (cetirizine, loratadine) reduce itch, particularly at night
Emollient moisturizers should be applied liberally throughout the day to support skin barrier recovery
Patch testing by a dermatologist identifies the specific allergen in allergic contact dermatitis — essential for prevention of recurrence
contact dermatitis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a dermatologist for contact dermatitis involving the face, genitals, or large body surface areas, for severe blistering or weeping dermatitis, or for dermatitis not responding to topical steroids after 2 weeks. Patch testing is recommended for any recurrent contact dermatitis.
contact dermatitis.specialist_recommendation: Dermatologist / Occupational Health Physician (for occupational cases)

immediate_advice: Seek urgent evaluation for symptomatic sinus bradycardia — dizziness, fainting, breathlessness, or chest pain with a slow heart rate requires immediate assessment
Do not abruptly stop prescribed beta-blockers or other rate-lowering medications — discuss reduction with your cardiologist
Avoid activities with syncope risk (driving, swimming, heights) if episodes of near-fainting have occurred
quick_solutions: Asymptomatic sinus bradycardia in fit individuals (especially athletes) is a normal physiological adaptation and requires no treatment
Identify and withdraw causative medications: beta-blockers, diltiazem, verapamil, digoxin, amiodarone, and ivabradine
Treat reversible underlying causes: hypothyroidism, electrolyte abnormalities, and inferior myocardial ischemia
Permanent pacemaker implantation is indicated for symptomatic sinus node dysfunction with bradycardia causing hemodynamic compromise
sinus bradycardia.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for symptomatic bradycardia with syncope, presyncope, heart rate below 40 beats per minute, chest pain, or breathlessness at rest. Consult a cardiologist for asymptomatic bradycardia below 50 beats per minute for investigation and monitoring.

immediate_advice: Seek same-day medical evaluation — PID requires prompt antibiotic treatment to prevent long-term complications including infertility and ectopic pregnancy
Avoid sexual intercourse until you and your partner(s) have completed treatment
Inform all recent sexual partners (within the last 60 days) so they can be tested and treated
quick_solutions: Combination antibiotic therapy covering Chlamydia, Gonorrhea, and anaerobes is required — typically ceftriaxone plus doxycycline plus metronidazole
Complete the full antibiotic course (14 days) even if symptoms improve rapidly — inadequate treatment leads to recurrence and tubal scarring
IV antibiotics and hospital admission are required for severe PID, suspected tubo-ovarian abscess, pregnancy, or failed outpatient treatment
STI screening (HIV, syphilis) and contact tracing are mandatory components of PID management
pelvic inflammatory disease.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for severe lower abdominal pain, high fever, vomiting, peritoneal signs, or suspected tubo-ovarian abscess — these indicate severe PID requiring surgical evaluation. See a doctor same-day for any lower abdominal pain with cervical motion tenderness or purulent vaginal discharge.

immediate_advice: Stop all alcohol consumption immediately and permanently
Avoid all NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac) and limit paracetamol to the minimum effective dose — both are hepatotoxic in liver disease
Seek emergency care for vomiting blood, black tarry stools, increasing abdominal swelling, confusion, or jaundice that worsens suddenly
quick_solutions: Identify and treat the underlying cause: viral hepatitis (antiviral therapy), autoimmune hepatitis (corticosteroids), metabolic-associated fatty liver disease (weight loss, exercise)
Regular surveillance with 6-monthly ultrasound and AFP for hepatocellular carcinoma is mandatory in cirrhosis
Manage complications: diuretics for ascites, beta-blockers for variceal bleeding prophylaxis, lactulose for encephalopathy
Liver transplant evaluation for eligible patients with decompensated cirrhosis or hepatocellular carcinoma within transplant criteria
liver disease.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for hematemesis (vomiting blood), melena (black tarry stools), sudden increase in abdominal girth, confusion or drowsiness, fever with ascites, or sudden deterioration in liver function tests.

immediate_advice: Increase dietary fiber intake gradually to 25–35g daily — sudden large increases cause bloating and discomfort
Drink at least 2 liters of fluid daily — fiber requires adequate water to soften stool
Do not ignore the urge to defecate — respond promptly and adopt a squatting position using a footstool to optimize anorectal angle
quick_solutions: Bulk-forming laxatives (psyllium, methylcellulose) are first-line and safe for long-term use
Osmotic laxatives (polyethylene glycol, lactulose) are effective for constipation not responding to fiber and fluids alone
Regular physical activity of at least 30 minutes daily stimulates bowel motility
Review all medications with your doctor — opioids, iron supplements, calcium channel blockers, and anticholinergics commonly cause constipation
chronic constipation.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for constipation not responding to dietary and laxative measures after 4 weeks, new onset constipation in adults over 50 (requires colonoscopy to exclude colorectal cancer), constipation with rectal bleeding, unintentional weight loss, or alternating constipation and diarrhea.

immediate_advice: Rest at home and avoid contact with others to prevent spread — influenza is highly contagious for 5–7 days from symptom onset
Maintain high fluid intake — fever and sweating cause significant fluid loss
Use paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever, myalgia, and headache — aspirin should be avoided in children due to Reye's syndrome risk
quick_solutions: Oseltamivir (Tamiflu) antiviral treatment is most effective when started within 48 hours of symptom onset and is recommended for high-risk groups (elderly, immunocompromised, pregnant women, severe disease)
Annual influenza vaccination significantly reduces severity and mortality — recommended for all adults over 65, healthcare workers, and those with chronic medical conditions
Honey is effective for cough associated with flu in adults and children over 1 year
Do not take antibiotics — influenza is viral and antibiotics are only indicated for confirmed bacterial complications (pneumonia, sinusitis)
flu.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for difficulty breathing, chest pain, confusion, severe vomiting preventing fluid intake, symptoms improving then rapidly worsening, blue-tinged lips, or any high-risk patient (elderly, pregnant, immunocompromised) with severe flu symptoms.
flu.specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Infectious Disease Specialist (for high-risk or complicated cases)

immediate_advice: Do not attempt to remove a skin tag or polyp yourself using string, scissors, or any home method — this causes pain, bleeding, and infection risk
Monitor for any change in size, shape, color, or bleeding which would prompt earlier medical review
Avoid friction and irritation from clothing, jewelry, or shaving over skin polyps as this causes discomfort
quick_solutions: Most cutaneous skin tags and polyps are entirely benign and require removal only if symptomatic or cosmetically unacceptable
Removal methods include cryotherapy, electrosurgery, snip excision, or ligation — all are quick outpatient procedures performed under local anesthetic
Recurrence of skin tags is common — new ones may develop in the same anatomical areas over time
Multiple skin tags associated with obesity, insulin resistance, or acanthosis nigricans should prompt investigation for type 2 diabetes
skin polyp.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for any skin lesion with rapid growth, irregular borders, multicolored pigmentation, bleeding, or ulceration — these require biopsy to exclude malignancy before assuming a benign skin tag diagnosis.

immediate_advice: Rest the affected arm in the most comfortable position — usually slightly abducted with a pillow support
Apply ice wrapped in a cloth to the shoulder for 15–20 minutes during the acute severe pain phase
Seek urgent neurological assessment — brachial neuritis (Parsonage-Turner syndrome) requires confirmation and exclusion of other serious diagnoses
quick_solutions: High-dose corticosteroids initiated early may reduce the duration of the acute pain phase
NSAIDs and opioid analgesics are required for severe acute pain — brachial neuritis pain is characteristically severe and poorly responsive to simple analgesics
Once the pain phase subsides (weeks to months), intensive physiotherapy is essential to restore muscle strength and prevent contracture
Most patients recover significant function over 2–3 years — prognosis is generally favorable but recovery is slow and patience is required
brachial neuritis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent neurological evaluation for sudden severe shoulder or arm pain rapidly followed by weakness and muscle wasting — the characteristic presentation of brachial neuritis requires exclusion of cervical disc prolapse, Pancoast tumor, and shoulder pathology.

immediate_advice: Avoid eating within 3 hours of lying down and elevate the head of the bed by 15–20cm
Eliminate trigger foods: alcohol, spicy foods, citrus, tomatoes, chocolate, coffee, and carbonated beverages
Take prescribed proton pump inhibitors 30 minutes before breakfast — do not take on a full stomach
quick_solutions: Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, lansoprazole, pantoprazole) at standard or double dose are the primary treatment for reflux esophagitis
Eosinophilic esophagitis requires dietary elimination (six-food elimination diet) or topical swallowed fluticasone/budesonide
Infectious esophagitis (Candida, HSV, CMV) requires specific antifungal or antiviral therapy — occurs primarily in immunocompromised patients
Endoscopy is required for esophagitis not responding to 8 weeks of PPI therapy, dysphagia, or weight loss
esophagitis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a gastroenterologist for difficulty swallowing, painful swallowing, food impaction, weight loss, vomiting blood, or esophagitis not responding to proton pump inhibitor therapy — these require endoscopic evaluation.

immediate_advice: Keep the affected area clean and dry — wear breathable cotton underwear and avoid tight synthetic clothing
Avoid scented soaps, bubble baths, douches, and vaginal deodorants which disrupt natural flora
Begin appropriate antifungal treatment promptly — yeast infections do not resolve without treatment
quick_solutions: Topical antifungal agents (clotrimazole cream or suppository, miconazole) are effective first-line OTC treatments for vaginal yeast infection
Oral fluconazole (150mg single dose) is equally effective and more convenient for vaginal candidiasis
For skin yeast infections, keep skin dry, apply topical antifungal (clotrimazole) and barrier cream to protect skin folds
Identify and address predisposing factors: antibiotic use, poorly controlled diabetes, immunosuppression, and oral contraceptive pill use
yeast infection.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor if this is your first episode (to confirm diagnosis), if OTC treatment fails after 1 week, if infections recur more than 4 times per year (recurrent vulvovaginal candidiasis requiring suppressive therapy), if pregnant, or if symptoms are atypical.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency psychiatric care immediately for acute psychosis — the person may lack insight into their condition and require assisted assessment
Do not leave the person alone if there is risk of harm to themselves or others
Maintain a calm, non-confrontational environment — avoid arguing about or challenging delusional beliefs
quick_solutions: Antipsychotic medications (olanzapine, risperidone, haloperidol) are the cornerstone of acute psychosis management
Early intervention in psychosis services (within 2 years of first episode) dramatically improve long-term outcomes
Identify and treat precipitating causes: substance-induced psychosis, medical causes (delirium, encephalitis, thyroid disease), or primary psychiatric disorder
Psychoeducation for the patient and family, CBT for psychosis, and family intervention reduce relapse rates significantly
psychotic disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency psychiatric care for any acute psychosis, particularly with agitation, command hallucinations directing harm, severe thought disorganization, inability to care for basic needs, or risk of harm to self or others.
psychotic disorder.specialist_recommendation: Psychiatrist / Early Intervention in Psychosis Team

immediate_advice: Set a quit date within the next 2 weeks and tell supportive people in your life
Remove all tobacco products, lighters, and ashtrays from your home, car, and workplace
Identify your smoking triggers and plan specific alternative behaviors for each trigger situation
quick_solutions: Combination nicotine replacement therapy (long-acting patch plus short-acting gum or lozenge) doubles quit rates compared to single NRT
Varenicline (Champix) is the most effective pharmacotherapy for smoking cessation — significantly more effective than NRT or bupropion alone
Behavioral support (individual or group stop-smoking counseling) combined with pharmacotherapy produces the highest quit rates of any intervention
Every quit attempt increases the probability of eventual successful cessation — do not be discouraged by previous failed attempts
