smoking or tobacco addiction.when_to_seek_help: Consult a stop-smoking specialist or pharmacist to access the most effective evidence-based cessation support. Seek medical evaluation for any respiratory symptoms, new cough, hemoptysis, or unexpected weight loss in a smoker — these require investigation for smoking-related malignancy.
smoking or tobacco addiction.specialist_recommendation: Stop Smoking Specialist / Pulmonologist / General Practitioner

immediate_advice: Apply the PRICE protocol immediately: Protection, Rest, Ice (wrapped in cloth for 20 minutes every 2 hours), Compression (bandage), Elevation above heart level
Avoid heat, alcohol, running, and massage in the first 72 hours — these increase bleeding and swelling
Seek emergency evaluation if there is significant deformity, bone tenderness, inability to bear weight, or neurovascular compromise
quick_solutions: NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) reduce pain and inflammation effectively from day 1
Physiotherapy with progressive loading and rehabilitation exercises is essential for full recovery and prevention of re-injury
Proprioceptive rehabilitation (balance board exercises) is critical for ankle sprains to prevent chronic instability and recurrent injury
Graduated return to sport under physiotherapy guidance prevents premature return and re-injury
sprain or strain.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency evaluation for Ottawa rules positive ankle sprains (bony tenderness at specific landmarks, inability to bear weight), suspected ligament rupture, joint instability, or any sprain not improving after 2 weeks of appropriate management.
sprain or strain.specialist_recommendation: Physiotherapist / Orthopedic Surgeon / Sports Medicine Doctor

immediate_advice: Apply a heat pad to the lower abdomen and lower back — heat is as effective as NSAIDs for pain relief in dysmenorrhea
Begin NSAIDs (ibuprofen 400mg, naproxen, or mefenamic acid) 1–2 days before the expected onset of menstruation
Gentle exercise such as walking or yoga reduces prostaglandin-mediated cramps and is more effective than complete rest
quick_solutions: Regular NSAID use (not just as-needed) for the first 2–3 days of menstruation is the most effective medical approach to primary dysmenorrhea
The combined oral contraceptive pill significantly reduces dysmenorrhea in the majority of users
The levonorgestrel IUS (Mirena) reduces both pain and blood loss and is highly effective for dysmenorrhea
Investigate for secondary causes if dysmenorrhea is severe or progressive: endometriosis, adenomyosis, and fibroids require specific diagnosis and treatment
idiopathic painful menstruation.when_to_seek_help: Consult a gynecologist for pain not responding to NSAIDs and hormonal therapy, progressive worsening over time, pain associated with deep dyspareunia (pain during intercourse), bowel symptoms during periods, or infertility — these features raise concern for endometriosis.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for a tension pneumothorax (rapidly worsening breathlessness, low oxygen saturation, tracheal deviation, absent breath sounds) — this is immediately life-threatening
Sit upright — this position optimizes ventilation in a patient with pneumothorax
Call emergency services immediately and do not leave the patient unattended if symptoms are severe
quick_solutions: Small primary spontaneous pneumothorax in a well patient may be managed with observation and discharge — reabsorption occurs at approximately 1.25% of pleural volume per day
Aspiration or intercostal chest drain insertion is performed for larger pneumothoraces, secondary pneumothorax, or any hemodynamic compromise
Tension pneumothorax requires immediate needle decompression (second intercostal space, mid-clavicular line) as a life-saving emergency measure before chest drain insertion
Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS) with pleurodesis is recommended after a second ipsilateral pneumothorax to prevent recurrence
pneumothorax.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for sudden onset pleuritic chest pain with breathlessness, particularly in tall thin young men or patients with lung disease. Call emergency services for rapid deterioration, cyanosis, or tracheal deviation — these indicate tension pneumothorax.

immediate_advice: Call emergency services immediately for vertebral fractures following significant trauma — do not move the person until trained personnel arrive with spinal immobilization equipment
Maintain spinal alignment at all times if neurological injury is suspected — log-roll technique only for any necessary repositioning
Seek emergency care for any new neurological symptoms (weakness, numbness, bladder or bowel dysfunction) following spinal injury
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately for trauma-related vertebral fractures — CT spine and neurosurgical assessment are time-critical

Osteoporotic vertebral compression fractures are managed with pain control, bracing, and early mobilization — percutaneous vertebroplasty or kyphoplasty reduces pain significantly in acute painful fractures
Neurological deficits from spinal cord compression may require urgent decompressive surgery
Osteoporosis treatment must be initiated or optimized after any fragility vertebral fracture to prevent subsequent fractures
fracture of the vertebra.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for any spinal injury after significant trauma, new weakness or numbness in limbs after back pain onset, bladder or bowel dysfunction after a fall, or sudden severe back pain in an elderly patient (fragility fracture).
fracture of the vertebra.specialist_recommendation: Neurosurgeon / Orthopedic Spine Surgeon / Emergency Physician

immediate_advice: Keep new scars protected from sun exposure for at least 12 months — UV radiation darkens immature scars permanently
Apply SPF 50+ sunscreen to healing wounds and scars whenever they will be exposed to daylight
Massage healed (fully closed) scars with moisturizer or silicone gel for 5–10 minutes daily — this reduces thickness and improves texture
quick_solutions: Silicone gel sheets or ointment applied consistently over 3–6 months significantly flatten and soften hypertrophic scars and keloids
Intralesional corticosteroid injection (triamcinolone) reduces bulk and itch in hypertrophic scars and is the first-line treatment for keloids
Laser therapy (pulsed dye laser, fractional CO2 laser) improves scar color, texture, and thickness significantly
Surgical scar revision combined with adjuvant treatment (silicone, laser, corticosteroid) is considered for functionally or cosmetically significant scars
scar.when_to_seek_help: Consult a dermatologist or plastic surgeon for scars causing functional impairment (restricting movement), keloid scars continuing to grow, hypertrophic scars not responding to silicone therapy after 6 months, or scars causing significant psychological distress.

immediate_advice: Perform the Valsalva maneuver gently: pinch nostrils closed, close the mouth, and blow gently as if blowing up a balloon to equalize ear pressure — repeat several times daily
Chew gum or yawn frequently to activate the tensor veli palatini muscle and encourage Eustachian tube opening
Avoid flying, diving, or significant altitude changes during active ETD — use decongestant nasal spray before takeoff and landing if flying is unavoidable
quick_solutions: Intranasal corticosteroid sprays (fluticasone, mometasone) used regularly for 4–8 weeks are the primary treatment when allergic or inflammatory cause is present
Treat underlying causes: allergic rhinitis, nasal polyps, sinusitis, and reflux all contribute to ETD
Grommet (ventilation tube) insertion provides effective relief for persistent ETD with glue ear causing significant hearing loss
Balloon Eustachian tuboplasty (BET) is an emerging minimally invasive procedure for patients with chronic obstructive ETD refractory to medical treatment
eustachian tube dysfunction (ear disorde.when_to_seek_help: Consult an ENT specialist for ETD lasting more than 3 months, associated significant hearing loss, persistent fluid behind the eardrum, tinnitus, or for recurrent episodes significantly affecting quality of life.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately — appendicitis is a surgical emergency that progresses to perforation within 24–72 hours
Do not eat or drink anything pending surgical evaluation
Do not take laxatives or apply a hot water bottle to the abdomen — this can accelerate perforation
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — CT abdomen with contrast or ultrasound confirms the diagnosis and IV antibiotics are started promptly
Laparoscopic appendicectomy is the gold standard treatment — a minimally invasive procedure with rapid recovery
Uncomplicated appendicitis may be managed with antibiotics alone in selected patients — discuss this option with the surgical team
Perforated appendicitis requires emergency surgery, prolonged IV antibiotics, and possible percutaneous drainage if abscess has formed
appendicitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for pain that starts around the navel and migrates to the right lower abdomen, fever, nausea, and vomiting — these are classic symptoms of appendicitis. Any severe right lower abdominal pain requires urgent surgical evaluation.

immediate_advice: Seek urgent medical evaluation — untreated Graves' disease (hyperthyroidism) causes significant cardiovascular stress and can precipitate thyroid storm
Take prescribed beta-blockers (propranolol) to control heart rate and tremor while awaiting definitive treatment
Protect eyes from sun exposure, use lubricating eye drops, and sleep with head elevated if Graves' ophthalmopathy is present
quick_solutions: Antithyroid medications (carbimazole or propylthiouracil) rapidly reduce thyroid hormone production — the primary initial treatment
Radioactive iodine (I-131) ablation is a highly effective definitive treatment producing permanent remission but typically results in hypothyroidism requiring lifelong thyroxine replacement
Total thyroidectomy is recommended for patients with large goiters, suspected malignancy, or significant Graves' ophthalmopathy where radioiodine would worsen eye disease
Lifelong thyroid function monitoring is required after all definitive treatments as both hypothyroidism and relapse of hyperthyroidism can occur
graves disease.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for thyroid storm (extremely high fever, rapid heart rate, confusion, vomiting, agitation) — this is a life-threatening complication requiring ICU management. Seek urgent evaluation for new-onset rapid palpitations, significant weight loss, or severe eye pain and proptosis.
graves disease.specialist_recommendation: Endocrinologist / Ophthalmologist (for Graves' ophthalmopathy) / Thyroid Surgeon

immediate_advice: Seek medical evaluation urgently for vomiting during pregnancy that prevents keeping any food or fluid down for more than 24 hours
Rest in a comfortable position and avoid triggers: strong smells, heat, and fatty or spicy foods
Eat small, frequent bland meals (crackers, toast, dry cereal) and sip cold, clear fluids in small amounts frequently
quick_solutions: Hospital admission for IV fluid rehydration, electrolyte replacement (thiamine must be given before IV glucose), and anti-emetic therapy is required for significant dehydration or failure of outpatient measures
Anti-emetic medications safe in pregnancy: ondansetron, promethazine, metoclopramide, and doxylamine-pyridoxine combination
Vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) supplementation reduces nausea of pregnancy and is safe in the first trimester
Ginger supplements and acupressure wristbands (P6 acupoint) provide modest additional benefit for mild-to-moderate symptoms
hyperemesis gravidarum.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency obstetric care for inability to keep any fluids down for more than 24 hours, signs of dehydration (dark urine, dizziness, reduced urination), significant weight loss, blood in vomit, confusion, or jaundice.

immediate_advice: Keep feet clean and dry — wash daily with soap and water, dry thoroughly especially between toes
Change socks daily and wear moisture-wicking synthetic or wool socks rather than cotton which retains moisture
Avoid walking barefoot in communal areas: swimming pools, gym changing rooms, and showers
quick_solutions: Topical antifungal creams or sprays (clotrimazole, miconazole, terbinafine) applied twice daily for 2–4 weeks are highly effective for most cases
Continue treatment for one week after symptoms resolve to prevent recurrence from residual spores
Apply antifungal powder to shoes and change shoes daily to allow them to dry completely between wearings
Oral terbinafine is required for moccasin-type (plantar) tinea pedis or for cases not responding to topical treatment
athlete's foot.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for athlete's foot not responding to 4 weeks of topical antifungal treatment, spreading cellulitis from a secondary bacterial infection, involvement of toenails (requiring oral treatment), or in diabetic patients where any foot infection requires prompt medical attention.

immediate_advice: Seek urgent medical evaluation — a pleural effusion requires investigation to identify its cause and determine if drainage is required
Sit upright — this reduces pressure on the diaphragm and improves ventilation when a significant effusion is present
Seek emergency care for rapidly worsening breathlessness, oxygen saturation below 94%, or signs of tension (tracheal deviation, severe hemodynamic compromise)
quick_solutions: Diagnostic thoracentesis (pleural fluid aspiration) is performed to analyze the fluid and determine whether it is a transudate or exudate — this guides the underlying diagnosis
Therapeutic drainage relieves breathlessness from large effusions — intercostal drain insertion for recurrent effusions
Treat the underlying cause: heart failure (diuretics), infection/empyema (antibiotics and drainage), malignancy (chemotherapy, pleurodesis, or indwelling pleural catheter)
Pleurodesis (chemical or surgical) is recommended for recurrent malignant pleural effusions to prevent reaccumulation
pleural effusion.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for sudden severe breathlessness, oxygen saturation below 90%, or any rapidly worsening respiratory status. Consult a pulmonologist promptly for any newly identified pleural effusion — the underlying cause always requires investigation.
pleural effusion.specialist_recommendation: Pulmonologist / Thoracic Surgeon / Oncologist (for malignant effusions)

immediate_advice: Massage the affected salivary gland in the direction of the duct opening (toward the mouth) several times daily to promote drainage
Stay well hydrated and use sugar-free chewing gum or lemon drops to stimulate saliva flow and flush the duct
Apply warm compresses to the swollen gland for 10–15 minutes several times daily to reduce inflammation and promote drainage
quick_solutions: Oral antibiotics (amoxicillin-clavulanate or flucloxacillin) are required for acute bacterial sialadenitis — the most common organism is Staphylococcus aureus
Adequate hydration is critical — dehydration reduces saliva production and is a major predisposing factor
Salivary calculi (stones) obstructing the duct require removal via sialendoscopy (endoscopic stone removal) or extracorporeal shock wave lithotripsy
Chronic or recurrent sialadenitis may ultimately require parotidectomy or submandibular gland excision
sialoadenitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care for rapidly enlarging painful facial or jaw swelling with fever, trismus (inability to open the mouth), difficulty swallowing, or any spreading swelling toward the neck — these indicate a spreading deep space infection requiring IV antibiotics and possible surgical drainage.

immediate_advice: Drink at least 2–3 liters of water daily to flush bacteria from the urinary tract
Take prescribed antibiotics immediately and complete the full course
Use paracetamol or ibuprofen and urinary alkalinizers (potassium citrate) for pain and burning relief while awaiting antibiotic effect
quick_solutions: Short-course antibiotics (3 days of trimethoprim or 3–7 days of nitrofurantoin) are appropriate for uncomplicated lower UTI in women
Urine culture is recommended for recurrent UTIs, male patients, children, pregnant women, or suspected upper tract infection
Cranberry products provide modest preventive benefit for recurrent UTIs in women — evidence supports their use as prophylaxis rather than treatment
Post-coital antibiotic prophylaxis (single dose after intercourse) is effective for women with sex-related recurrent UTIs
urinary tract infection.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor for UTI in men, children, pregnant women (all require culture and guided treatment), fever with loin pain (pyelonephritis), UTI not improving after 48 hours of antibiotics, or recurrent UTIs (more than 3 per year).
urinary tract infection.specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Urologist (for recurrent or complicated cases)

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for arrhythmia with chest pain, breathlessness, syncope, or loss of consciousness
Avoid caffeine, alcohol, stimulant medications, and illicit drugs which commonly precipitate arrhythmias
If you have a known arrhythmia, carry your medication list and cardiac device information at all times
quick_solutions: Electrocardiogram (ECG) during symptoms is essential for definitive arrhythmia classification and treatment planning
Holter monitor (24–48 hours) or event recorder captures intermittent arrhythmias that are not present during a standard ECG
Rate control (beta-blockers, calcium channel blockers) or rhythm control (antiarrhythmic drugs, cardioversion, ablation) depends on the specific arrhythmia identified
Anticoagulation assessment is required for atrial arrhythmias — stroke prevention is as important as rhythm management
arrhythmia.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for palpitations with chest pain, breathlessness, presyncope, syncope, or a heart rate above 150 or below 40 beats per minute. Consult a cardiologist for any new palpitations, documented irregular rhythm, or palpitations occurring more than weekly.

immediate_advice: Identify and avoid personal triggers: eating, drinking, talking, touching the face, cold wind, brushing teeth, or washing the face
Use a straw for drinking cold liquids and eat soft, lukewarm foods on the unaffected side during acute episodes
Seek urgent neurological evaluation for any new onset severe facial pain — secondary causes must be excluded before primary trigeminal neuralgia is diagnosed
quick_solutions: Carbamazepine is first-line pharmacological treatment and provides excellent pain control in most patients — start at a low dose and titrate upward
Oxcarbazepine is an alternative with a more favorable side effect profile for patients who cannot tolerate carbamazepine
Microvascular decompression (MVD) surgery is the most effective long-term treatment for classical trigeminal neuralgia and is recommended for medically refractory cases
Stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife) is an effective non-invasive option for patients not suitable for open surgery
trigeminal neuralgia.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent neurological evaluation for any new onset severe, electric shock-like facial pain. Seek emergency care for trigeminal neuralgia with facial weakness, sensory loss, or other neurological symptoms (suggesting secondary cause — MS, tumor) or pain not responding to carbamazepine.

immediate_advice: Maintain an active exercise program every day — physical inactivity is the most damaging thing for ankylosing spondylitis
Sleep on a firm mattress without a pillow if possible to prevent flexion deformity of the spine
Apply heat to stiff areas in the morning and cold to actively inflamed joints to reduce pain and facilitate exercise
quick_solutions: NSAIDs (naproxen, diclofenac, indomethacin) taken continuously (not just as needed) are first-line treatment and reduce both pain and inflammation — they may also slow radiographic progression
Regular physiotherapy with a program of spinal extension exercises, swimming, and breathing exercises is mandatory and must continue lifelong
Biologic therapies (TNF inhibitors: adalimumab, etanercept, certolizumab; IL-17 inhibitors: secukinumab, ixekizumab) provide excellent sustained response in NSAID-refractory disease
Regular monitoring for extra-articular manifestations: uveitis (eye pain, redness), inflammatory bowel disease, psoriasis, and cardiac conduction abnormalities
ankylosing spondylitis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a rheumatologist for inflammatory back pain (worse in the morning, better with exercise) persisting more than 3 months in a young adult. Seek urgent ophthalmological evaluation for any red, painful eye (uveitis which occurs in 40% of patients).

immediate_advice: Protect insensate limbs from injury — inspect feet daily if lower limb sensation is reduced, use protective footwear, and avoid extremes of temperature
Avoid prolonged pressure on affected nerves: do not cross legs, lean on elbows, or maintain positions that compress peripheral nerves for extended periods
Seek urgent neurological evaluation for rapidly progressive weakness, sudden onset of numbness, or any autonomic symptoms (dizziness, bowel and bladder changes)
quick_solutions: Identify and treat the underlying cause: diabetes, vitamin B12 deficiency, hypothyroidism, alcohol excess, medications, vasculitis, or hereditary neuropathy
Neuropathic pain management with gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, or amitriptyline — titrate slowly to effective dose
Nerve conduction studies and EMG characterize the type (axonal vs demyelinating) and distribution of nerve damage, guiding diagnosis and treatment
Physiotherapy and occupational therapy maintain function, prevent contractures, and provide assistive devices for weakness and balance impairment
peripheral nerve disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent neurological evaluation for rapidly progressive peripheral neuropathy, weakness ascending from the legs (Guillain-Barré syndrome), autonomic instability, or any neuropathy with systemic symptoms. Consult a neurologist for any unexplained peripheral neuropathy.

immediate_advice: Do not squeeze or attempt to drain a sebaceous cyst yourself — rupturing the cyst wall causes intense inflammation and dramatically increases infection risk
Apply warm compresses to an inflamed cyst for 10–15 minutes several times daily to reduce acute inflammation
Keep the area clean and dry to prevent secondary infection
quick_solutions: Uninflamed, asymptomatic sebaceous cysts require no treatment — surgical excision is elective
Surgical excision of the intact cyst with its capsule is the definitive treatment — incomplete removal leads to recurrence
Acutely infected sebaceous cysts are treated with antibiotics first; once infection settles the cyst is excised electively
Intralesional corticosteroid injection can reduce inflammation in a non-infected cyst if surgery is deferred
sebaceous cyst.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for any lump that is rapidly growing, hard, fixed, irregular, or over 5cm — these features require biopsy to exclude malignancy. See a doctor for sebaceous cysts causing recurrent infection, functional limitation, cosmetic concern, or significant discomfort.

immediate_advice: Acknowledge that neurotic symptoms, though distressing, are not dangerous and do not indicate serious physical disease
Practice structured relaxation techniques daily: progressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing, or guided meditation
Maintain regular daily routines for sleep, meals, and exercise — structure reduces anxiety and rumination
quick_solutions: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) is the most effective evidence-based treatment for neurotic disorders including anxiety, OCD, and health anxiety
Regular aerobic exercise has robust evidence for reducing anxiety and neurotic symptoms to a degree comparable with medication
SSRIs are effective pharmacological treatment when psychological therapies alone are insufficient
Mindfulness-based cognitive therapy (MBCT) reduces rumination and prevents relapse of anxiety and depressive neurosis
neurosis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a mental health professional for neurotic symptoms lasting more than 2 weeks, causing significant distress, impairing daily function, or not improving with self-help strategies. Seek emergency care for any active self-harm or suicidal ideation.

immediate_advice: Stop all alcohol consumption immediately and permanently — continued alcohol use in chronic pancreatitis causes irreversible progression and dramatically worsens prognosis
Stop smoking — tobacco smoking is an independent risk factor for chronic pancreatitis progression
Take prescribed pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with every meal and all snacks to prevent malnutrition and steatorrhea
quick_solutions: Pancreatic enzyme replacement therapy (PERT) with every meal corrects exocrine insufficiency and resolves malabsorption, steatorrhea, and weight loss
Pain management requires a stepwise approach: regular paracetamol, NSAIDs, neuropathic agents, then opioids as last resort under specialist supervision
Fat-soluble vitamin supplementation (vitamins A, D, E, K) is required as malabsorption depletes these vitamins

Endoscopic or surgical interventions (pancreatic duct drainage, stricture dilation, total pancreatectomy with islet cell autotransplantation) are considered for refractory pain not responding to medical management
chronic pancreatitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for acute-on-chronic pancreatitis flare with severe pain, vomiting, fever, or jaundice. Consult a gastroenterologist for new onset diabetes with known chronic pancreatitis, significant weight loss, severe malnutrition, or pain not controlled with oral analgesics.
chronic pancreatitis.specialist_recommendation: Gastroenterologist / Pancreatic Surgeon / Pain Management Specialist

immediate_advice: Do not pick, scratch, or attempt to remove seborrheic keratoses yourself — this causes bleeding, infection, and scarring
Reassure yourself that seborrheic keratoses are entirely benign and do not become malignant — they are purely cosmetic
Seek medical evaluation for any lesion that is rapidly changing, bleeding, ulcerated, or irregular — these features are not characteristic of seborrheic keratosis
quick_solutions: No treatment is medically necessary for seborrheic keratoses — removal is elective and for cosmetic or symptomatic reasons only
Cryotherapy (liquid nitrogen) is the most common removal method — quick, effective, and widely available
Curettage, electrosurgery, or laser ablation are effective alternatives depending on lesion size and location
A sudden appearance of large numbers of seborrheic keratoses (Leser-Trélat sign) may rarely indicate an underlying internal malignancy and warrants investigation
seborrheic keratosis.when_to_seek_help: Consult a dermatologist for any seborrheic keratosis that bleeds, ulcerates, has an irregular border, rapidly changes in appearance, or for the sudden eruption of many new lesions over a short period.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency obstetric care for heavy vaginal bleeding, passage of tissue, severe cramping, or shoulder tip pain (indicating intraperitoneal bleeding from ectopic pregnancy)
Rest and avoid strenuous physical activity during light spotting while awaiting medical assessment
Seek emotional support — pregnancy loss is a significant bereavement and requires compassionate care
quick_solutions: Urgent pelvic ultrasound confirms the diagnosis and excludes ectopic pregnancy — this is always the first priority

Management options include expectant (waiting for natural completion), medical (misoprostol), or surgical (manual vacuum aspiration or ERPC) management depending on gestational age and clinical status
Rhesus negative women require anti-D immunoglobulin within 72 hours of confirmed miscarriage regardless of gestational age
Recurrent miscarriage (3 or more) requires specialist investigation for thrombophilia, antiphospholipid syndrome, uterine anomalies, and chromosomal causes
spontaneous abortion.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for heavy bleeding (soaking more than one pad per hour), severe abdominal pain, fever, foul-smelling discharge (septic miscarriage), or any signs of hemodynamic compromise. All women with vaginal bleeding in early pregnancy require prompt evaluation to exclude ectopic pregnancy.
spontaneous abortion.specialist_recommendation: Obstetrician / Early Pregnancy Assessment Unit / Gynecologist

immediate_advice: Avoid sexual contact during active outbreaks and for 7 days after lesions have fully healed — viral shedding continues throughout this period
Keep the affected area clean and dry — wear loose cotton underwear to reduce friction and discomfort
Take warm sitz baths with plain water to relieve painful lesions and reduce the urge to scratch
quick_solutions: Antiviral therapy (acyclovir, valacyclovir, famciclovir) started within 72 hours of symptom onset significantly reduces severity and duration of primary and recurrent episodes
Daily suppressive antiviral therapy reduces recurrence frequency by 70–80% and reduces viral shedding, lowering (but not eliminating) transmission risk to partners
Inform sexual partners — use of condoms reduces but does not completely eliminate transmission risk as shedding occurs from skin not covered by condoms
Screen for co-existing STIs — genital herpes increases susceptibility to HIV and other STIs through disruption of genital skin and mucosal barriers
genital herpes.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent evaluation for severe primary genital herpes causing urinary retention, disseminated herpes in immunocompromised patients, or neonatal herpes exposure (newborn born to mother with active genital herpes requires immediate antiviral prophylaxis).
genital herpes.specialist_recommendation: Sexual Health Specialist / Gynecologist / Infectious Disease Specialist

immediate_advice: Acknowledge the stressor and your emotional response to it — avoidance and suppression of feelings prolong adjustment reactions
Maintain basic self-care routines: adequate sleep, regular meals, and gentle physical activity even when motivation is reduced
Reach out to trusted friends, family, or a counselor — social support is the most powerful buffer against adjustment difficulties
quick_solutions: Short-term supportive psychotherapy or counseling is the primary treatment and is highly effective for adjustment disorders
Problem-solving therapy addresses the practical consequences of the identified stressor and reduces associated anxiety and depression
If the stressor cannot be changed, acceptance-based approaches and cognitive reframing reduce emotional suffering
Short-term anxiolytic or antidepressant medication may be appropriate for severe or prolonged symptoms not responding to psychological approaches
adjustment reaction.when_to_seek_help: Consult a mental health professional if symptoms persist beyond 6 months after the stressor has resolved, if they are causing significant functional impairment, or if suicidal ideation develops — at which point emergency psychiatric assessment is required.

immediate_advice: Follow a low-fat diet to minimize biliary colic episodes — fatty meals trigger gallbladder contraction and pain
Seek emergency care immediately for severe right upper abdominal pain with fever and jaundice (cholangitis) or fever and right upper quadrant pain (cholecystitis)
Take NSAIDs (diclofenac, ketorolac) for acute biliary colic — they are more effective than opioids for this type of visceral pain
quick_solutions: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy is the definitive treatment for symptomatic gallstones — a safe, minimally invasive day-case procedure in most centers
ERCP with sphincterotomy is performed for common bile duct stones — usually before or at the time of laparoscopic cholecystectomy
Urso deoxycholic acid (UDCA) dissolves small cholesterol gallstones in selected patients who are not surgical candidates — slow process taking 6–24 months
Maintain a healthy body weight and avoid rapid weight loss diets which precipitate gallstone formation
gallstone.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for jaundice with fever and rigors (acute cholangitis), persistent severe right upper abdominal pain (cholecystitis), or pancreatitis triggered by gallstones. Consult a surgeon for any symptomatic gallstones causing recurrent biliary colic.

immediate_advice: Seek urgent neurological evaluation for any new neurological symptoms lasting more than 24 hours — early MRI and CSF analysis are required for diagnosis
Avoid overheating (Uhthoff's phenomenon — heat temporarily worsens MS symptoms) — use cooling vests, avoid hot baths, and stay in air-conditioned environments during hot weather
Do not abruptly stop disease-modifying therapy without specialist guidance — rebound inflammation can occur
quick_solutions: High-dose IV or oral methylprednisolone shortens the duration of acute relapses — does not affect long-term outcomes
Disease-modifying therapies (interferons, glatiramer, natalizumab, ocrelizumab, siponimod) reduce relapse rates and slow disability progression
Symptom management: baclofen or tizanidine for spasticity, amantadine or modafinil for fatigue, oxybutynin for bladder dysfunction
Multidisciplinary care (neurology, physiotherapy, occupational therapy, speech therapy, psychology) optimizes function and quality of life throughout the disease course
multiple sclerosis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent neurological evaluation for any new or rapidly worsening neurological deficit, vision loss, severe balance disturbance, or suspected relapse. Seek emergency care for acute severe relapse causing inability to walk, swallow, or breathe safely.

immediate_advice: Do not stop prescribed maintenance medications (mesalazine, immunosuppressants) during a flare without gastroenterology guidance
Seek urgent evaluation for severe bloody diarrhea (more than 6 bloody stools per day), fever, rapid heart rate, or abdominal tenderness — these indicate severe colitis requiring hospital admission
Stay well hydrated — diarrhea causes significant fluid and electrolyte losses
quick_solutions: Oral and topical mesalazine (5-ASA) is first-line maintenance therapy for mild-to-moderate ulcerative colitis
Oral corticosteroids (prednisolone) induce remission in moderate flares — immunosuppressants (azathioprine, mercaptopurine) maintain remission
Biologic therapies (infliximab, adalimumab, vedolizumab, ustekinumab) are effective for moderate-to-severe UC not responding to conventional therapy
Colectomy (total colectomy with ileal pouch anal anastomosis) is curative and recommended for refractory disease, dysplasia, or fulminant colitis
ulcerative colitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for more than 6 bloody stools per day, fever, rapid heart rate, abdominal distension (toxic megacolon — a life-threatening emergency), or any signs of peritonism. Regular colonoscopic surveillance for dysplasia is mandatory after 8–10 years of disease.

immediate_advice: Take sublingual glyceryl trinitrate (GTN) spray or tablet immediately — spray under the tongue and sit down while waiting for effect (onset within 1–3 minutes)
If angina does not resolve within 5 minutes of first GTN dose, take a second dose — if unresolved after 2 doses 5 minutes apart, call emergency services immediately
Rest immediately when angina starts — stop all physical activity
quick_solutions: Regular use of long-acting nitrates, beta-blockers, and calcium channel blockers reduces angina frequency and improves exercise tolerance
High-intensity statin therapy and antiplatelet therapy (aspirin) are mandatory for all patients with stable angina
Coronary angiography identifies the extent of coronary artery disease — PCI or CABG may be recommended to improve symptoms and prognosis
Cardiac rehabilitation and supervised exercise training improve functional capacity and angina threshold
angina.when_to_seek_help: Call emergency services immediately for chest pain not resolving after 2 GTN doses at 5-minute intervals, chest pain at rest, chest pain that is more frequent, severe, or prolonged than usual (unstable angina), or pain accompanied by sweating, breathlessness, or arm or jaw pain.

immediate_advice: Use prescribed intranasal corticosteroid spray consistently — regular use shrinks polyps and is the most important medical intervention
Avoid NSAIDs and aspirin if aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (Samter's triad) is present — these trigger severe attacks in susceptible patients
Use saline nasal irrigation twice daily to clear secretions and improve medication delivery to the nasal mucosa
quick_solutions: High-dose intranasal corticosteroid sprays (fluticasone, mometasone) or drops (betamethasone drops applied in head-down position) are first-line treatment
Short courses of oral corticosteroids (prednisolone) provide rapid reduction in polyp size for symptom control — not suitable for long-term use
Biologic therapy (dupilumab) is highly effective for severe chronic rhinosinusitis with nasal polyps not responsive to surgery and intranasal steroids
Functional endoscopic sinus surgery (FESS) removes polyps and opens sinus drainage pathways — recurrence is common without ongoing steroid spray maintenance
nasal polyp.when_to_seek_help: Consult an ENT specialist for nasal polyps causing complete nasal obstruction, recurrent sinusitis, significant loss of smell, or polyps not responding to 12 weeks of topical corticosteroids. Seek urgent evaluation for unilateral nasal polyp — this requires biopsy to exclude malignancy.

immediate_advice: Apply broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen daily to all affected areas — UV exposure is the most significant driver of pigmentation disorders
Avoid picking or scratching hyperpigmented areas as post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation significantly worsens with trauma
Avoid photosensitizing medications and agents (tetracyclines, amiodarone, some NSAIDs) which worsen pigmentation disorders
quick_solutions: Topical skin-lightening agents (hydroquinone, kojic acid, azelaic acid, tranexamic acid, niacinamide) reduce hyperpigmentation when used consistently for 3–6 months
Chemical peels (glycolic acid, salicylic acid) performed by a dermatologist accelerate cell turnover and improve pigmentation
Laser treatments (Q-switched Nd:YAG, picosecond laser) effectively target melanin deposits in resistant or deep pigmentation
For vitiligo, topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus), phototherapy (narrowband UVB), and JAK inhibitor cream (ruxolitinib) can repigment affected areas
skin pigmentation disorder.when_to_seek_help: Consult a dermatologist for any rapidly changing pigmented lesion (possible melanoma), new widespread depigmentation (vitiligo), facial hyperpigmentation not responding to topical treatment, or pigmentary changes associated with systemic symptoms.

immediate_advice: Avoid positions and activities that reproduce or worsen pain: overhead reaching, carrying heavy bags on the affected shoulder, or sleeping on the affected side
Apply ice to the affected area for 20 minutes to reduce acute inflammation
Seek urgent evaluation for progressive weakness of the arm or hand associated with neck or shoulder pain — nerve compression requires timely decompression
quick_solutions: NSAIDs reduce nerve inflammation and pain effectively in the acute phase
Physiotherapy addressing cervical and shoulder posture, nerve mobilization exercises, and scapular stabilization is the primary treatment
Cervical epidural corticosteroid injection provides significant relief for nerve root impingement causing radicular arm pain
Surgical decompression (foraminotomy, discectomy) is recommended for progressive neurological deficits or pain refractory to 6 weeks of conservative management
nerve impingement near the shoulder.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent neurological or orthopedic evaluation for progressive arm weakness, muscle wasting, or rapidly worsening neurological symptoms. Seek emergency care for bilateral arm weakness, bladder or bowel dysfunction associated with neck symptoms (myelopathy).

immediate_advice: Seek compassionate obstetric care — a missed miscarriage is a significant loss and emotional support is essential from the outset
Do not panic if you feel no physical symptoms — the absence of bleeding or cramping is characteristic of a missed miscarriage
Discuss all management options with your obstetric team before making a decision
quick_solutions: Expectant management (waiting for natural miscarriage) is appropriate for many women but may take 2–6 weeks — requires close follow-up
Medical management with misoprostol is effective and avoids surgery — causes cramping and bleeding within 24–48 hours of administration
Surgical management (manual vacuum aspiration or ERPC under general anesthetic) provides definitive and immediate resolution
Rhesus negative women require anti-D immunoglobulin regardless of management method chosen
missed abortion.when_to_seek_help: Consult your obstetrician or early pregnancy unit for all management options after diagnosis. Seek emergency care for heavy bleeding, severe pain, fever, or foul-smelling discharge which may indicate septic miscarriage requiring urgent IV antibiotics and surgical evacuation.
missed abortion.specialist_recommendation: Obstetrician / Early Pregnancy Assessment Unit / Gynecologist

immediate_advice: Call emergency services or go to the emergency department immediately — DKA is a life-threatening medical emergency
Do not skip insulin doses even if not eating — sick day rules for insulin-dependent diabetes must be followed
Do not attempt to manage DKA at home — hospital IV fluid, insulin infusion, and electrolyte monitoring are mandatory
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (normal saline), insulin infusion, and hourly electrolyte monitoring (particularly potassium) are the treatment
Identify and treat the precipitating cause: missed insulin, infection, myocardial infarction, or new onset diabetes
Potassium replacement is critical — DKA depletes total body potassium and hypokalemia during insulin treatment is a common cause of death
Euglycemic DKA can occur in patients on SGLT2 inhibitors with near-normal blood glucose — diagnosis requires blood ketone measurement, not glucose alone
diabetic ketoacidosis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for blood glucose above 14 mmol/L with ketonuria or blood ketones above 3 mmol/L, vomiting, abdominal pain, rapid breathing (Kussmaul respiration), confusion, or any insulin-dependent diabetic who is unwell and unable to keep fluids down.
diabetic ketoacidosis.specialist_recommendation: Endocrinologist / Diabetologist / Critical Care (Emergency services first)

immediate_advice: Do not accommodate obsessions by completing compulsions — providing short-term relief, they reinforce and escalate the OCD cycle long-term
Seek emergency care for OCD causing complete inability to function, not eating or drinking, or associated with suicidal ideation
Educate family members — accommodation of compulsions by family (reassurance, participation in rituals) inadvertently maintains OCD severity
quick_solutions: Exposure and Response Prevention (ERP) therapy is the gold standard treatment for OCD — more effective than any medication alone
SSRIs at higher doses than used for depression (e.g., fluoxetine 60–80mg, sertraline 200mg) are effective pharmacological treatment
Combination of ERP plus SSRI produces the best outcomes for moderate-to-severe OCD
Intensive residential or day-hospital ERP programs are available for severe OCD not responding to standard outpatient treatment
obsessive compulsive disorder (ocd).when_to_seek_help: Consult a clinical psychologist or psychiatrist for OCD causing significant distress or impairment (taking more than 1 hour per day), or for OCD interfering with work, relationships, or daily activities. Seek emergency care for complete functional collapse or suicidal ideation.

immediate_advice: Seek urgent medical evaluation — severe hyponatremia (sodium below 120 mmol/L) or rapidly developing hyponatremia can cause cerebral edema, seizures, and death
Do not attempt to correct hyponatremia rapidly with salt or IV fluids without specialist guidance — overcorrection causes osmotic demyelination syndrome (locked-in syndrome)
Stop any medications that may be contributing: SSRIs, diuretics, carbamazepine, and PPI use associated with hyponatremia
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately for hyponatremia with seizures, coma, or severe neurological symptoms — hypertonic saline is given under close monitoring
Fluid restriction (500–1000ml/day) is the cornerstone of treatment for SIADH-related hyponatremia
Identify and treat the underlying cause: SIADH, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, heart failure, liver cirrhosis, or renal disease
Vaptans (tolvaptan) are used for SIADH not responding to fluid restriction — they require very careful monitoring due to risk of overcorrection
hyponatremia.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for confusion, seizures, coma, or any acute neurological symptoms in a patient with known low sodium. Seek urgent evaluation for sodium below 130 mmol/L even if asymptomatic — correction must be managed carefully in hospital.

immediate_advice: Avoid activities with high fall and fracture risk during any acute bone disorder flare or when bone fragility is suspected
Ensure adequate calcium and vitamin D intake through diet and supplementation as directed by your doctor
Seek urgent evaluation for acute bone pain following minimal trauma in older adults — fragility fracture must be excluded
quick_solutions: Specific treatment depends entirely on the diagnosis: bisphosphonates for osteoporosis, calcitriol for renal osteodystrophy, bisphosphonates and denosumab for Paget's disease, and calcium restriction for hypercalcemia
DEXA scan assesses bone mineral density and fracture risk — guides initiation and monitoring of bone-protective therapy
Physiotherapy with weight-bearing exercise and balance training reduces fracture risk across all bone disorders
Regular monitoring with bone markers, calcium, phosphate, and vitamin D levels guides treatment adjustments
bone disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for acute severe bone pain following minor trauma (fragility fracture), hypercalcemia symptoms (confusion, vomiting, dehydration, abdominal pain), or acute cord compression from vertebral collapse. Consult a rheumatologist or endocrinologist for any significant metabolic bone disease.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency psychiatric care for any active suicidal ideation, self-harm with intent to die, or severe dissociation with risk of harm
Establish a safety plan with your mental health team for crisis situations — identify early warning signs and pre-agreed coping strategies
Avoid using alcohol or substances during emotional crises as these dramatically increase impulsivity and risk of self-harm
quick_solutions: Dialectical Behavior Therapy (DBT) is the most evidence-based treatment for borderline personality disorder — it significantly reduces self-harm, suicidality, and hospitalizations
Schema Therapy and Mentalization-Based Treatment (MBT) are effective alternatives for BPD and other personality disorders
Medications are used adjunctively for specific symptoms: mood stabilizers for affective instability, low-dose antipsychotics for cognitive-perceptual symptoms
Long-term therapeutic relationships with consistent practitioners are crucial — frequent therapist changes are destabilizing for personality disorder
personality disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency psychiatric care for active suicidal ideation with intent or plan, severe self-harm requiring medical treatment, or acute psychotic episodes. Seek urgent outpatient review for escalating self-harm, rapidly worsening impulsivity, or treatment non-adherence.
personality disorder.specialist_recommendation: Psychiatrist / Specialist Personality Disorder Service / DBT-Trained Psychologist

immediate_advice: Seek medical evaluation within 72 hours of rash onset — antiviral treatment is most effective when started early
Keep the rash covered with a non-adherent dressing to prevent spreading the varicella zoster virus to non-immune individuals
Avoid contact with pregnant women, newborns, and immunocompromised individuals until all vesicles are crusted over
quick_solutions: Antiviral therapy (acyclovir 800mg 5 times daily, or valacyclovir 1g three times daily for 7 days) started within 72 hours reduces severity and duration and significantly reduces the risk of post-herpetic neuralgia
Pain management is critical: paracetamol, NSAIDs, neuropathic agents (gabapentin, amitriptyline), and opioids for severe acute pain
Shingles vaccination (Shingrix — recombinant zoster vaccine) is recommended for all adults over 50 and significantly reduces the incidence and severity of shingles
Post-herpetic neuralgia (persistent pain after rash resolution) is treated with gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, amitriptyline, or lidocaine patches
shingles (herpes zoster).when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent evaluation for shingles involving the eye (herpes zoster ophthalmicus — requires same-day ophthalmology review), facial shingles with ear pain (Ramsay Hunt syndrome — facial palsy risk), immunocompromised patients (require IV antivirals), or disseminated shingles.
shingles (herpes zoster).specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Ophthalmologist (for ophthalmic zoster) / Pain Management Specialist (for PHN)

immediate_advice: Take the full prescribed course of antibiotics (penicillin V or amoxicillin for 10 days) — do not stop when symptoms improve as incomplete treatment risks rheumatic fever
Rest, maintain adequate fluid intake, and use paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever and throat pain
Stay home from school or work until at least 24 hours after starting antibiotics and until fever-free
quick_solutions: Rapid strep antigen test or throat culture confirms streptococcal infection and guides appropriate antibiotic use — not all sore throats are strep
Salt water gargles, throat lozenges, and cold fluids provide effective symptomatic relief while antibiotics take effect
Penicillin allergy patients are treated with erythromycin or clarithromycin
Consider tonsillectomy for recurrent strep throat (7 or more episodes per year, or 5 per year for 2 years)
strep throat.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for difficulty swallowing own saliva, voice changes to a muffled quality, neck swelling with trismus (inability to open mouth), severe neck pain or stiffness, or any rapidly worsening throat symptoms — these may indicate peritonsillar abscess or deep neck space infection.
strep throat.specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Otolaryngologist (ENT) for recurrent cases

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for stridor (high-pitched breathing sound) or difficulty breathing caused by massively enlarged tonsils
Position the child upright or semi-upright to optimize airway patency
Avoid sedative medications which can cause airway muscle relaxation and worsen obstruction
quick_solutions: Antibiotics are prescribed if active tonsillitis is contributing to acute enlargement
A short course of oral corticosteroids may reduce acute tonsillar swelling significantly in severe obstruction
Tonsillectomy is the definitive treatment for tonsillar hypertrophy causing obstructive sleep apnea, recurrent tonsillitis, or swallowing difficulties
Sleep study (polysomnography) confirms the degree of sleep-disordered breathing and guides urgency of surgical referral
tonsillar hypertrophy.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for acute respiratory distress from tonsillar obstruction. Consult an ENT specialist for witnessed apneas during sleep, behavioral changes attributed to sleep disruption, recurrent tonsillitis (5+ episodes per year), or difficulty eating caused by enlarged tonsils.

immediate_advice: Avoid NSAIDs, aspirin, alcohol, spicy foods, and caffeine which all directly irritate the gastric mucosa
Eat small, frequent meals and avoid skipping meals — an empty stomach allows gastric acid to directly irritate the inflamed mucosa
Take prescribed antacids or proton pump inhibitors as directed — do not stop early because symptoms improve
quick_solutions: Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, lansoprazole) are the most effective treatment for reducing gastric acid and allowing mucosal healing
H. pylori eradication therapy (triple therapy: PPI plus clarithromycin plus amoxicillin for 7–14 days) is indicated when H. pylori infection is confirmed
Antacids provide rapid but short-lived symptom relief and are suitable for breakthrough symptoms
Avoid smoking — nicotine impairs mucosal healing, increases acid production, and reduces prostaglandin production which protects the gastric lining
gastritis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for vomiting blood or coffee-ground material, black tarry stools, severe abdominal pain, or significant dizziness suggesting hemorrhage. Consult a gastroenterologist for symptoms persisting beyond 4 weeks, unexplained weight loss, or any alarm features requiring endoscopy.

immediate_advice: Apply broad-spectrum SPF 50+ sunscreen to all sun-exposed areas daily and avoid sun exposure between 10am and 4pm
Seek urgent dermatological evaluation for any changing, bleeding, or suspicious skin lesion — do not delay
Never attempt to remove a suspicious skin lesion yourself
quick_solutions: Seek urgent biopsy for any lesion suspicious for melanoma (ABCDE criteria: Asymmetry, irregular Border, multiple Colors, Diameter above 6mm, Evolution/change) or non-healing skin lesion
Surgical excision with clear margins is the primary treatment for all skin cancers — margin width depends on histological type and stage
Sentinel lymph node biopsy is performed for melanomas deeper than 1mm to assess lymph node involvement
Regular annual full-body skin examination by a dermatologist is recommended for all patients with a history of skin cancer or significant sun exposure history
skin cancer.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent dermatological evaluation for any skin lesion that is growing, changing in color or shape, bleeding spontaneously, ulcerating, or not healing within 4 weeks. Any lesion suspicious for melanoma should be seen within 2 weeks.

immediate_advice: Avoid spicy, acidic, and abrasive foods that irritate ulcers — eat soft, bland foods until healing is complete
Use a soft toothbrush and avoid toothpaste containing sodium lauryl sulfate (SLS) which is associated with recurrent aphthous ulcers in susceptible individuals
Apply topical anesthetic gel (benzocaine or lidocaine) before meals for pain relief
quick_solutions: Topical corticosteroid paste (triamcinolone acetonide in Orabase) applied to ulcers 2–3 times daily significantly reduces healing time and pain
Chlorhexidine mouthwash reduces secondary infection and may reduce ulcer frequency
Investigate for nutritional deficiencies: iron, vitamin B12, folate, and zinc deficiencies are common causes of recurrent aphthous stomatitis
For very frequent recurrences, systemic colchicine, dapsone, or thalidomide (under strict supervision) are used in specialist settings
aphthous ulcer.when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for ulcers larger than 1cm, lasting more than 3 weeks without healing, very frequent recurrence (more than monthly), associated with genital ulcers (Behcet's disease), or if occurring with systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss, diarrhea) suggesting inflammatory bowel disease.
aphthous ulcer.specialist_recommendation: Oral Medicine Specialist / Dentist / Gastroenterologist (for IBD-related ulcers)

immediate_advice: Rest adequately and maintain high fluid intake — swallowing is painful but dehydration must be prevented
Take paracetamol or ibuprofen regularly (not just as needed) for fever and throat pain
Use cold fluids, ice cream, or throat lozenges for additional soothing relief
quick_solutions: Viral tonsillitis (most common) requires supportive care only — antibiotics are not indicated and do not speed recovery from viral infection
Bacterial tonsillitis (Group A Streptococcus) confirmed by rapid antigen test or culture is treated with penicillin V or amoxicillin for 10 days
Salt water gargles reduce bacterial load and provide soothing relief
Tonsillectomy is recommended for recurrent bacterial tonsillitis (7+ episodes per year, or the Paradise criteria) after weighing benefits against surgical risks
tonsillitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent care for inability to swallow own saliva, severe voice change (muffled voice), trismus (difficulty opening the mouth), unilateral swelling pushing the uvula sideways (peritonsillar abscess), or any difficulty breathing.
tonsillitis.specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Otolaryngologist (ENT) for recurrent cases

immediate_advice: Maintain consistent, predictable daily routines to minimize anxiety and behavioral difficulties
Use clear, simple, concrete communication and visual supports (picture schedules, visual cues) appropriate to the individual's level of understanding

Seek urgent evaluation for any sudden behavioral change in a person with developmental disability — this often indicates an unmet medical need (pain, infection, medication side effect) rather than behavioral deterioration
quick_solutions: Early intervention with speech therapy, occupational therapy, physiotherapy, and educational support maximizes developmental potential
Applied Behavior Analysis (ABA) and Positive Behavior Support (PBS) address challenging behaviors functionally
Respite care, family support programs, and carer training are essential to prevent carer burnout and maintain family stability
Regular health surveillance addresses the elevated rates of epilepsy, sensory impairments, mental health disorders, and communication difficulties associated with developmental disability
developmental disability.when_to_seek_help: Consult a developmental pediatrician or specialist for formal assessment and diagnosis. Seek urgent medical evaluation for any sudden behavioral change, new seizures, self-injurious behavior, or any acute physical symptom in a non-verbal individual who cannot communicate pain.
developmental disability.specialist_recommendation: Developmental Pediatrician / Child Psychiatrist / Multidisciplinary Developmental Team

immediate_advice: Move to a safe, calm environment away from the stressor immediately
Practice slow, controlled breathing: breathe in slowly for 4 counts, hold for 2, exhale for 6 — repeat for 5 minutes
Do not make major decisions during an acute stress reaction — cognitive impairment is significant in this state
quick_solutions: Psychological first aid — calm presence, practical support, and helping the person feel safe — is the most appropriate immediate intervention
Most acute stress reactions resolve within days to weeks without formal treatment when the stressor is removed and adequate support is provided
Single-session psychological debriefing is not recommended — evidence shows it does not prevent PTSD and may be harmful
If symptoms persist beyond 4 weeks, formal assessment for PTSD and referral for trauma-focused CBT or EMDR is indicated
acute stress reaction.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency psychiatric care for disorganized behavior posing safety risk, complete dissociation from reality, suicidal ideation, or inability to care for basic needs. Consult a mental health professional if symptoms persist beyond 4 weeks without improvement.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency medical evaluation immediately — delirium always has an underlying medical cause that requires urgent identification and treatment
Keep the environment safe, calm, and well-lit — avoid physical restraints which worsen agitation and increase falls risk
Ensure familiar people (family members) are present to provide orientation and reassurance
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — delirium requires urgent identification and treatment of the precipitating cause: infection, metabolic disturbance, drug toxicity, urinary retention, constipation, hypoxia, or pain
Non-pharmacological management is first-line: reorientation, familiar faces, noise reduction, maintaining normal day-night cycle, and early mobilization
Low-dose haloperidol is used for severe agitation only when non-pharmacological measures fail — benzodiazepines worsen delirium in most patients except alcohol withdrawal
Prevention is most effective: avoid anticholinergic medications, treat pain, ensure hydration, prevent constipation and urinary retention, and maintain mobility in hospitalized patients
delirium.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for any acute confusion, disorientation, or behavioral change in an elderly or unwell patient — delirium is a medical emergency with significant mortality if the underlying cause is not identified and treated urgently.
delirium.specialist_recommendation: Geriatrician / Emergency Physician / Psychiatrist (for hyperactive delirium)

immediate_advice: Identify and address the source of friction or pressure causing the callus — ill-fitting footwear is the most common cause
Soak the callus in warm water for 10–15 minutes to soften it before gentle filing with a pumice stone
Apply a thick emollient (urea-containing foot cream at 25–40%) to soften callus tissue and prevent cracking
quick_solutions: Wear well-fitting footwear with adequate cushioning and appropriate width — custom orthotic insoles redistribute pressure and prevent callus recurrence
Podiatrist debridement (professional paring) of thick or painful calluses provides safe and effective relief
Salicylic acid preparations (callus pads or liquid) applied carefully after protecting surrounding skin break down the thickened keratin layer
Diabetic patients should never self-treat calluses — all foot calluses in diabetics require podiatry review due to underlying pressure ulceration risk
callus.when_to_seek_help: See a podiatrist for painful calluses, calluses in diabetic patients, calluses not improving after 4 weeks of self-care, or calluses with an underlying dark spot (may indicate skin breakdown or foreign body beneath the lesion).

immediate_advice: Use saline nasal spray or rinse twice daily to keep the nasal passages clear and reduce crusting
Sleep on the side that corresponds to the more open nostril to improve nasal breathing at night
Avoid nasal decongestant sprays for more than 3 consecutive days as rebound congestion worsens the situation
quick_solutions: Intranasal corticosteroid sprays reduce mucosal swelling and improve airflow in mild-to-moderate obstruction from a deviated septum
Nasal dilator strips worn at night improve airflow during sleep and reduce snoring
Septoplasty (surgical straightening of the septum) is the definitive treatment for a deviated septum causing significant nasal obstruction and is performed under general anesthesia
Combined septorhinoplasty addresses both the functional septum deviation and any associated external nasal deformity
deviated nasal septum.when_to_seek_help: Consult an ENT specialist for significant nasal obstruction affecting sleep quality, causing recurrent sinusitis or nosebleeds, or preventing adequate nasal breathing. Seek evaluation for deviated septum causing significant sleep apnea symptoms.

immediate_advice: Most infantile hemangiomas are benign, self-limiting, and require only observation
Seek urgent evaluation for hemangiomas near the eye (can cause amblyopia), on the airway (stridor), near the anus (can ulcerate and cause bleeding), or showing rapid growth
Apply petroleum jelly to ulcerated hemangiomas and keep them covered to prevent infection and reduce pain
quick_solutions: Propranolol (oral beta-blocker) is the standard treatment for hemangiomas requiring intervention — it causes significant involution and is safe when properly monitored
Timolol gel applied topically is effective for small superficial hemangiomas and avoids systemic side effects
Pulsed dye laser effectively treats residual telangiectasia and flat superficial hemangiomas
Surgery or laser ablation is used for persistent residual fibro-fatty tissue after involution if cosmetically or functionally significant
hemangioma.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent pediatric or dermatology evaluation for hemangiomas near the eye, lip, or airway, any rapidly enlarging hemangioma, ulcerated hemangiomas, hemangiomas associated with PHACE syndrome features, or any hemangioma causing significant functional impairment.
hemangioma.specialist_recommendation: Dermatologist / Pediatric Surgeon / Ophthalmologist (for periocular lesions)

immediate_advice: Continue gentle daily movement — complete bed rest beyond 1–2 days is consistently harmful and delays recovery
Apply heat to the lower back for 15–20 minutes to relieve muscle spasm and improve local blood flow
Avoid analgesics as the primary strategy — address the underlying drivers (posture, deconditioning, fear-avoidance) alongside pain management
quick_solutions: Active physiotherapy with a structured exercise program (core stabilization, flexibility, aerobic conditioning) is the most effective long-term intervention
NSAIDs provide short-term pain relief during acute flares — limit use to the minimum effective duration
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for chronic pain significantly reduces pain-related disability and fear-avoidance behaviors
Multidisciplinary pain rehabilitation programs combining physiotherapy, psychology, occupational therapy, and pain medicine produce the best outcomes for chronic disabling back pain
chronic back pain.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for new bladder or bowel dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness (cauda equina syndrome). Consult a doctor for back pain with fever (discitis, spinal infection), unexplained weight loss, pain waking from sleep, or history of cancer.
chronic back pain.specialist_recommendation: Pain Management Specialist / Physiotherapist / Orthopedic Surgeon

immediate_advice: Take all prescribed heart failure medications (ACE inhibitor/ARB/ARNI, beta-blocker, aldosterone antagonist, diuretic, SGLT2 inhibitor) consistently without omission
Weigh yourself daily at the same time — report a weight gain of more than 2kg in 48 hours to your heart failure team
Sit upright immediately for breathlessness — call emergency services for sudden severe breathlessness, pink frothy sputum, or inability to speak in sentences
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care for acute decompensated heart failure — IV diuretics, oxygen, and hemodynamic monitoring are the standard interventions
Adhere strictly to a sodium restriction of less than 2g per day and monitor fluid intake as directed
Regular outpatient follow-up with your heart failure specialist, titration of medications to target doses, and monitoring of renal function and electrolytes are all essential
Cardiac resynchronization therapy (CRT) and ICD improve symptoms, exercise capacity, and mortality in eligible patients
heart failure.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for sudden severe breathlessness, weight gain of more than 2kg in 48 hours, inability to lie flat, increasing ankle edema, confusion, or syncope — these indicate acute decompensation requiring urgent hospital treatment.

immediate_advice: Wash hands thoroughly before and after touching the eyes and avoid touching or rubbing the eyes
Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup with others — conjunctivitis is highly contagious
Apply cool compresses to the closed eye for comfort and to remove crusts — use a clean compress for each eye
quick_solutions: Bacterial conjunctivitis is treated with topical antibiotic drops (chloramphenicol, fusidic acid) for 5–7 days — both eyes are usually treated even if only one appears infected
Viral conjunctivitis is self-limiting and requires supportive care only — antibiotic drops are not effective
Allergic conjunctivitis is treated with topical antihistamine drops (olopatadine, ketotifen) and avoidance of the triggering allergen
Contact lens wearers with conjunctivitis must remove lenses immediately and not reinsert until fully recovered and cleared by a doctor
conjunctivitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent ophthalmological care for conjunctivitis with severe pain, significant photophobia, blurred vision, corneal opacity, or in a newborn (neonatal conjunctivitis with gonococcal or chlamydial cause requires urgent treatment). See a doctor for conjunctivitis not improving after 3 days of antibiotic treatment.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for new bladder or bowel dysfunction, saddle anesthesia (numbness in the groin and inner thighs), or rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness — these indicate cauda equina syndrome
Maintain gentle movement — bed rest beyond 1–2 days is harmful and delays recovery from herniated disc
Apply ice for the first 48 hours, then heat for muscle spasm — avoid positions that worsen the leg or arm pain
quick_solutions: NSAIDs are first-line treatment for pain management during the acute phase
Physiotherapy with nerve mobilization exercises, core strengthening, and postural re-education is the cornerstone of rehabilitation
Epidural corticosteroid injection provides effective short-term relief of radicular pain and reduces the need for surgery in many patients
Surgical discectomy is recommended for progressive neurological deficits, cauda equina syndrome, or intractable pain not responding to 6 weeks of conservative management
herniated disk.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for bladder or bowel dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or bilateral leg weakness (cauda equina syndrome — surgical emergency). Consult a doctor for radicular pain (pain radiating down the arm or leg) with associated weakness, numbness, or reflex changes.

immediate_advice: Continue breastfeeding or pumping from the affected breast — stopping feeding worsens engorgement and milk stasis which perpetuates the infection
Apply warm compresses to the affected area before feeds to improve milk flow and reduce engorgement
Rest as much as possible — systemic symptoms (fever, flu-like illness) are common and resolve with treatment
quick_solutions: Oral antibiotics (flucloxacillin or dicloxacillin for Staphylococcal mastitis; erythromycin if penicillin-allergic) are required for bacterial mastitis — safe during breastfeeding
Frequent and effective milk removal through continued breastfeeding or pumping is as important as antibiotics for resolution
Ibuprofen is preferred over paracetamol for mastitis as it provides both analgesia and anti-inflammatory benefit
Fluctuant breast abscess requires ultrasound-guided needle aspiration or incision and drainage — antibiotics alone are insufficient for established abscess
breast infection (mastitis).when_to_seek_help: See a doctor promptly for mastitis with fever, red streaking, or increasing pain — antibiotic treatment should begin within 24 hours to prevent abscess formation. Seek urgent care for a palpable fluctuant area (abscess), fever above 39°C not responding to antibiotics, or non-lactating mastitis in a non-breastfeeding woman (requires biopsy to exclude inflammatory breast cancer).
breast infection (mastitis).specialist_recommendation: General Practitioner / Breast Surgeon / Lactation Consultant

immediate_advice: Call emergency services immediately — pulmonary embolism is a life-threatening emergency
Keep the person calm and in the most comfortable position — usually sitting upright
Do not leave the person alone — hemodynamic collapse can occur rapidly
quick_solutions: Seek emergency care immediately — anticoagulation (IV heparin or subcutaneous LMWH) and hemodynamic support are initiated immediately in hospital
CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) is the gold standard diagnostic imaging — must not be delayed if clinical probability is high
Massive PE with hemodynamic instability requires systemic thrombolysis or catheter-directed therapy — this is a resuscitative intervention
Long-term anticoagulation (3–6 months minimum, lifelong for unprovoked PE or recurrent events) prevents recurrence
pulmonary embolism.when_to_seek_help: Call emergency services immediately for sudden onset severe breathlessness, pleuritic chest pain, coughing blood, unexplained rapid heart rate, syncope, or collapse — particularly following prolonged immobility, recent surgery, or known DVT.
pulmonary embolism.specialist_recommendation: Pulmonologist / Hematologist / Emergency Physician (Emergency services first)

immediate_advice: Ensure immediate safety — remove dangerous objects from the environment and call emergency services if there is immediate risk of serious harm
Avoid punitive or confrontational responses during acute behavioral incidents — de-escalation and calm firm boundaries are more effective
Document specific behaviors, frequency, and contexts to provide accurate information for clinical assessment
quick_solutions: Parent Management Training (PMT) is the most evidence-based intervention for younger children with conduct disorder
Multisystemic Therapy (MST) is an intensive community-based intervention with strong evidence for adolescents with severe conduct disorder
Functional Family Therapy (FFT) addresses family dynamics that maintain conduct disorder behaviors
Treat co-occurring ADHD, learning disabilities, depression, and substance misuse which are present in the majority of young people with conduct disorder
conduct disorder.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent psychiatric evaluation for aggression resulting in serious injury, fire-setting behavior, weapon use, or conduct disorder with emerging psychopathic traits or significant substance misuse. Consult a child psychiatrist for any conduct disorder causing significant harm to the child or others.
conduct disorder.specialist_recommendation: Child Psychiatrist / Clinical Psychologist / Youth Justice / Multisystemic Therapy Team

immediate_advice: Seek urgent urological evaluation — the most important immediate priority is to exclude testicular torsion, which is a surgical emergency presenting with similar symptoms
Apply ice wrapped in a cloth to the scrotum and elevate the scrotum with supportive underwear to reduce pain and swelling
Do not delay evaluation — testicular torsion causes irreversible testicular loss within 6 hours of symptom onset
quick_solutions: Doppler ultrasound of the scrotum confirms normal blood flow (ruling out torsion) and confirms epididymitis
Antibiotic treatment: doxycycline for STI-related epididymitis in young men; ciprofloxacin or ofloxacin for enteric organism epididymitis in older men — complete the full 10–14 day course
NSAIDs reduce inflammation and provide effective pain relief
Sexual partners from the preceding 60 days require testing and treatment if STI-related epididymitis is confirmed
epididymitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for sudden severe scrotal pain (testicular torsion until proven otherwise), fever with scrotal swelling, or systemic sepsis. Consult a urologist for chronic epididymitis, recurrent episodes, or epididymitis not responding to antibiotics.
epididymitis.specialist_recommendation: Urologist / Sexual Health Specialist / Emergency Physician

immediate_advice: Avoid scratching or picking the lesions — this spreads the virus to adjacent skin and increases the risk of secondary bacterial infection
Do not share towels, clothing, or bathing equipment with others to prevent spread
Cover lesions during contact sports or swimming to prevent spread to other participants
quick_solutions: Most molluscum contagiosum resolves spontaneously within 6–18 months without treatment — watchful waiting is appropriate for healthy children
Cryotherapy, curettage, or topical potassium hydroxide 10% solution are effective active treatment options for persistent or spreading lesions
Imiquimod cream stimulates the immune response and gradually clears lesions — requires consistent application for several weeks
Immunocompromised patients (HIV, organ transplant recipients) require more aggressive treatment as lesions can be extensive and persistent
molluscum contagiosum.when_to_seek_help: Consult a dermatologist for extensive molluscum, lesions spreading despite treatment, facial lesions causing cosmetic distress, lesions near the eye, lesions in an immunocompromised patient, or genital molluscum in an adult (may warrant STI screening).

immediate_advice: Do not douche — douching disrupts the normal vaginal flora and pH and is consistently associated with worse vaginal health outcomes
Wear loose, breathable cotton underwear and change daily
Practice good genital hygiene with warm water only — no soaps, wipes, or scented products inside the vagina
quick_solutions: Normal physiological discharge (leukorrhea) requires no treatment — it is a sign of healthy vaginal function
If discharge is associated with itching, odor, or color change, clinical assessment and vaginal swabs are required to exclude infection
Probiotics containing Lactobacillus species support healthy vaginal flora and may reduce the risk of bacterial vaginosis
Track any changes in discharge character around the menstrual cycle — normal discharge varies with ovulation and hormonal changes
benign vaginal discharge (leukorrhea).when_to_seek_help: Consult a doctor for discharge that is gray, green, yellow, or foul-smelling, associated with itching, burning, or pelvic pain, unusual in volume or consistency, or associated with intermenstrual or post-coital bleeding — these features suggest infection requiring treatment.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency urological care for acute urinary retention (complete inability to urinate with a painful distended bladder) — catheterization is required immediately
Do not drink large volumes of fluid if unable to urinate — this worsens bladder distension and pain
Avoid medications that worsen urinary retention: anticholinergics, antihistamines, decongestants, and opioids
quick_solutions: Urethral catheterization relieves acute obstruction immediately — suprapubic catheterization is used when urethral catheterization fails
Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin) relax urethral smooth muscle and facilitate voiding — start before trial of void after catheter removal
Identify and treat the underlying cause: BPH, urethral stricture, bladder neck contracture, pelvic mass, or neurogenic bladder dysfunction
Trial without catheter (TWOC) after 24–48 hours of catheter drainage and alpha-blocker therapy allows many patients to void spontaneously
bladder obstruction.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for inability to urinate with abdominal pain or suprapubic distension, fever with urinary retention (infected obstructed system), or renal impairment from chronic obstruction.

immediate_advice: Seek urgent dermatological evaluation immediately — suspected melanoma should be seen within 2 weeks under a two-week wait urgent referral
Do not attempt to remove, biopsy, or treat the lesion yourself — this compromises pathological assessment and staging
Apply SPF 50+ sunscreen, avoid sun exposure, and never use sunbeds
quick_solutions: Wide local excision with pathologically confirmed clear margins is the primary surgical treatment — margin width is determined by Breslow thickness
