Sentinel lymph node biopsy for melanomas deeper than 1mm assesses occult lymph node metastasis and guides adjuvant therapy decisions
Targeted therapy (BRAF/MEK inhibitors for BRAF-mutant melanoma) and immunotherapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, ipilimumab) have transformed outcomes in advanced melanoma
Annual full-body skin examination and regular lymph node surveillance are mandatory after melanoma treatment
melanoma.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent dermatological evaluation within 2 weeks for any pigmented lesion with the ABCDE criteria: Asymmetry, irregular Border, multiple Colors, Diameter above 6mm, or Evolution. Any bleeding, ulcerated, or rapidly changing pigmented lesion requires immediate assessment.
melanoma.specialist_recommendation: Dermatologist / Surgical Oncologist / Melanoma Multidisciplinary Team

immediate_advice: Rest the voice completely for 48–72 hours — avoid whispering as it is as stressful on the vocal cords as normal speech
Maintain excellent hydration with warm fluids — steam inhalation and humidified air soothe inflamed laryngeal mucosa
Avoid throat clearing and coughing as much as possible — these traumatize inflamed vocal cords
quick_solutions: Most acute laryngitis is viral and resolves within 1–2 weeks with voice rest and hydration — antibiotics are not indicated for viral laryngitis
Avoid all irritants: cigarette smoke, alcohol, menthol lozenges, and very dry air
Treat gastroesophageal reflux if suspected as reflux laryngitis is one of the most common causes of chronic hoarseness — PPI therapy for 2–3 months
Hoarseness persisting beyond 3 weeks in an adult requires laryngoscopy to exclude laryngeal pathology including malignancy
laryngitis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for stridor (noisy breathing), drooling, difficulty swallowing, or any respiratory distress accompanying hoarseness — these may indicate epiglottitis or severe laryngeal infection. Consult an ENT specialist for hoarseness lasting more than 3 weeks in an adult.

immediate_advice: Change diapers frequently — every 2–3 hours and immediately after soiling to minimize skin contact with urine and feces
Clean the diaper area gently with warm water and a soft cloth at each change — avoid scented wipes with alcohol
Allow air drying of the skin for 5–10 minutes before applying a new diaper
quick_solutions: Apply a thick layer of zinc oxide barrier cream (Sudocrem, Desitin) at every diaper change to protect the skin from moisture
Allow diaper-free time for at least 1–2 hours daily — air exposure significantly accelerates healing
For candidal diaper rash (satellite lesions, skin fold involvement), topical antifungal cream (clotrimazole or miconazole) is applied at each diaper change for 7–10 days
Avoid talcum powder — it does not protect against diaper rash and carries aspiration risk in infants
diaper rash.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor for rash not improving after 2–3 days of treatment, rash spreading beyond the diaper area, presence of pustules or blistering, fever, or any suspicion of candidal infection — particularly if the rash involves the skin folds or has satellite lesions.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for vomiting blood, black tarry stools, or sudden severe abdominal pain — these indicate hemorrhage or perforation
Stop all NSAIDs and aspirin immediately unless aspirin is essential for cardiac protection and cannot be safely stopped without medical guidance
Avoid all alcohol, spicy foods, and caffeine during active ulcer treatment
quick_solutions: Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole 40mg, pantoprazole 40mg) are the cornerstone of ulcer healing — take 30 minutes before breakfast for full effect
H. pylori eradication is essential if infection is confirmed — triple therapy (PPI plus two antibiotics) for 7–14 days
Confirm H. pylori eradication with urea breath test 4–8 weeks after completing eradication therapy
If NSAIDs are unavoidable, co-prescribe a full-dose PPI for gastroprotection at all times during NSAID use
gastroduodenal ulcer.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for vomiting blood or coffee-ground material, black tarry stools, sudden severe abdominal pain (perforation), or dizziness and pallor from hemorrhage. Consult a gastroenterologist for all confirmed peptic ulcers for H. pylori testing and follow-up endoscopy if indicated.

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for a hernia that is irreducible (cannot be pushed back in), hard, tender, or associated with vomiting and abdominal pain — these indicate strangulation
Avoid heavy lifting, straining, and activities that significantly increase intra-abdominal pressure
Gently lie down and try to manually reduce a reducible hernia if you have been taught to do so — do not force
quick_solutions: Surgical repair (laparoscopic or open herniorrhaphy with mesh) is the definitive treatment — all symptomatic inguinal hernias should be repaired electively
Watchful waiting may be appropriate for small, asymptomatic, easily reducible hernias in elderly men with high operative risk
Manage constipation, chronic cough, and urinary obstruction which increase intra-abdominal pressure and hernia recurrence risk
Return to full activity after laparoscopic repair is typically possible within 1–2 weeks
inguinal hernia.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for a hernia that cannot be reduced, becomes acutely painful, is associated with nausea, vomiting, or abdominal distension — these indicate strangulation or obstruction requiring emergency surgery.

immediate_advice: Apply thick emollient moisturizer generously at least 3–4 times daily to all affected areas — this is the most important single intervention for eczema management
Apply topical corticosteroid to actively inflamed (red, itchy) areas as prescribed — do not use on normal-looking skin
Avoid known triggers: certain soaps, detergents, synthetic fabrics, excessive heat or sweating, and identified food or environmental allergens
quick_solutions: Emollient therapy (moisturizers) applied immediately after bathing in lukewarm (not hot) water within 3 minutes of drying is the cornerstone of eczema management
Topical calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) are steroid-sparing alternatives for sensitive skin areas (face, groin, skin folds)
Dupilumab (biologic injection) is highly effective for moderate-to-severe eczema not responding to topical treatments
Oral antihistamines (sedating) at night reduce nocturnal itch and improve sleep quality during flares
eczema.when_to_seek_help: Consult a dermatologist for eczema covering large body surface areas, eczema with signs of bacterial superinfection (weeping, crusting, rapid worsening), eczema significantly affecting sleep and quality of life, or eczema not responding to topical treatments after 4 weeks.

immediate_advice: Provide clear, literal, and predictable communication — avoid idioms, sarcasm, and ambiguous social cues
Respect sensory sensitivities — reduce overwhelming stimuli where possible (noise, bright lights, crowds)
Maintain consistent routines and provide advance notice of any changes to prevent anxiety
quick_solutions: Social skills training in small group settings improves social communication and reduces isolation
Cognitive Behavioral Therapy adapted for autism effectively addresses co-occurring anxiety and depression which are highly prevalent
Occupational therapy addresses sensory processing differences, daily living skills, and workplace adaptations
Educational and workplace accommodations (written instructions, quiet work environments, structured tasks) support independent functioning
asperger syndrome.when_to_seek_help: Consult a clinical psychologist or psychiatrist for formal assessment if autistic traits are significantly impairing social, academic, or occupational functioning. Seek urgent evaluation for co-occurring severe depression, suicidal ideation, or acute mental health crisis.

immediate_advice: Rinse the mouth with a non-alcoholic saline or sodium bicarbonate mouthwash (1/4 teaspoon salt plus 1/4 teaspoon bicarbonate in 1 cup warm water) 4–6 times daily
Maintain excellent oral hygiene with a very soft toothbrush after every meal — stop if gums are bleeding but continue gentle rinsing
Avoid alcohol-based mouthwashes, spicy, acidic, or very hot and cold foods which dramatically worsen mucositis pain
quick_solutions: Topical anesthetic rinses (lidocaine viscous solution) before meals significantly reduce pain and allow adequate oral intake
Magic mouthwash (combination of lidocaine, diphenhydramine, and antacid) provides broad symptomatic relief
Low-level laser therapy (LLLT) is an evidence-based treatment for chemotherapy-induced mucositis that reduces severity when available
Maintain nutrition through soft, high-calorie foods, nutritional supplements, or enteral feeding if oral intake is insufficient — contact your oncology dietitian promptly
mucositis.when_to_seek_help: Contact your oncology team promptly for mucositis causing inability to eat or drink, weight loss, fever (infection risk is very high in neutropenic patients with mucositis), or mucositis not settling by day 14 of a treatment cycle. Seek emergency care for respiratory compromise from oropharyngeal mucositis.
mucositis.specialist_recommendation: Oncologist / Oral Medicine Specialist / Oncology Dietitian

immediate_advice: Soak the affected finger or toe in warm salt water for 15–20 minutes, 3–4 times daily — this promotes drainage and reduces inflammation
Keep the area clean and dry between soaks
Do not bite nails or pick at the nail folds — this is the most common cause of acute paronychia
quick_solutions: Acute paronychia with pus formation requires incision and drainage (a simple procedure under local anesthetic) in addition to antibiotics
Oral antibiotics (flucloxacillin or amoxicillin-clavulanate) are required for spreading infection or systemic features
For chronic paronychia (often fungal/Candida): keep hands dry, wear gloves for wet work, and use topical antifungal cream
Avoid prolonged immersion of the hands in water and use moisturizing hand cream to maintain the protective cuticle
paronychia.when_to_seek_help: See a doctor for paronychia not improving after 2–3 days of warm soaks, fluctuant pus requiring drainage, spreading redness of the finger, fever, or for chronic paronychia persisting more than 6 weeks.

immediate_advice: Seek urgent haematological evaluation for any unexplained persistent lymphadenopathy, unexplained weight loss, night sweats, or recurrent unexplained infections
If currently receiving chemotherapy or immunosuppression, seek emergency care immediately for any fever above 38°C — febrile neutropenia requires same-day treatment with IV antibiotics
Avoid crowds, unwell contacts, and uncooked foods during periods of significant immunosuppression from treatment
quick_solutions: Formal diagnosis requires full blood count with differential, blood film, bone marrow biopsy, and immunophenotyping — do not delay specialist referral
Chemotherapy regimens vary significantly by disease type: ALL, AML, CML, CLL, and lymphomas all have distinct treatment protocols
G-CSF (filgrastim) is used to stimulate white cell recovery after chemotherapy and reduce the duration of neutropenia
Stem cell transplantation (allogeneic or autologous) offers potential cure or long-term remission for many haematological malignancies
white blood cell disease.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care immediately for fever in a neutropenic patient (febrile neutropenia is a haematological emergency), severe bleeding or bruising, very high white cell count causing symptoms (leukostasis), or any rapidly deteriorating haematological condition.
white blood cell disease.specialist_recommendation: Haematologist / Oncologist / Bone Marrow Transplant Specialist

immediate_advice: Seek emergency care immediately for new bladder or bowel dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness associated with known spondylolisthesis
Avoid high-impact sports and activities involving significant spinal extension (gymnastics, weightlifting, fast bowling) during symptomatic periods
Apply heat to the lower back and maintain gentle activity — complete bed rest worsens symptoms
quick_solutions: Physiotherapy focused on lumbar stabilization exercises and core strengthening is the primary treatment for symptomatic spondylolisthesis
NSAIDs provide effective pain relief during acute flares
A lumbosacral brace may be recommended during symptomatic periods to restrict the aggravating range of motion
Surgical spinal fusion is recommended for high-grade spondylolisthesis, progressive slippage, significant neurological compromise, or failure of conservative management after 6 months
spondylolisthesis.when_to_seek_help: Seek emergency care for any acute neurological deterioration, new bladder or bowel symptoms, or cauda equina syndrome. Consult an orthopedic spine surgeon for progressive neurological deficit, high-grade slip, or pain significantly limiting daily activities despite adequate conservative management.

immediate_advice: Wash hands thoroughly and frequently — viral conjunctivitis (epidemic keratoconjunctivitis) is highly contagious and spreads easily by contact
Do not share towels, pillowcases, or eye makeup with household members
Apply cool compresses to the closed eye for comfort and to remove crusts — use a separate, freshly washed cloth for each eye
quick_solutions: Viral conjunctivitis is self-limiting and resolves in 7–14 days — antibiotic eye drops are not effective and should not be used unless secondary bacterial infection is confirmed
Preservative-free lubricating eye drops provide comfort relief and help wash viral particles from the ocular surface
Cold compresses reduce periocular swelling and chemosis (conjunctival swelling) effectively
Exclude from school or work during the contagious period (while eyes are actively discharging) to prevent spread of highly contagious adenoviral conjunctivitis
conjunctivitis due to virus.when_to_seek_help: Seek urgent ophthalmological evaluation for viral conjunctivitis with significant pain, blurred vision, corneal involvement (photophobia, opacity), symptoms lasting more than 3 weeks, or in any immunocompromised patient — herpetic conjunctivitis (HSV) requires antiviral treatment and is sight-threatening if untreated.
